rudrabhashya.english:
OM

|| Sri Rudra Bhashyam ||
(Commentary on the Sri Rudra Hymn)

Composed by
Sri Abhinava Shankaracharya,
the most eminent among Paramahamsa Parivrajakas (wandering renunciants of the highest order),
the establisher of the Advaita (non-dual) doctrine.

[decorative ornament]

Printed at
Sri Vani Vilas Mudra Yantralam (Press)
Sri Ranganagara (Srirangam).
1912.
Copyright Registered.

PREFACE.

THIS excellent commentary was printed some time ago in Grantha characters by the late Mahāmahōpādhyāya Vedāntakēsari Brahmaśri Gaṇapati Śāstrigal of Pañgānād. Since it is more elaborate and helpful than that of the famous Śri Vidyāraṇya and since it even criticises the latter in some places, I thought it could be very usefully included among the Sastra Booklets that are issued from the Sri Vani Vilas Press and thus made available for a larger Public. The author of the commentary is stated to be one Abhinava Śaṅkara whose identity is not quite clear. But I am led to consider him as one with the Guru of Veṅkatanātha, the author of Brahmānandagiri—the most excellent commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, published some months ago in the Sri Vani Vilas Sastra series. Veṅkatanātha speaks of his Guru as Abhinava Śaṅkara Rāma Brahmānanda and mentions a work named Pāshaṇḍagajakēsari as his. This commentator also is named as Abhinava Śaṅkara and he also mentions the above work as his (vide p. 77). It is quite probable that the two Abhinava Śaṅkaras were one and the same and that Veṅkatanātha wrote a commentary on the Pāshaṇḍagajakesari of his Guru.

ii

These are but surmises and we have to rest satisfied with them till any manuscript of Pāshandagajakēsari is found out. I would be very thankful if any of the numerous readers of this book who come across such a manuscript, kindly send the same to me.

[signature: T. Krishnabrahmanyam]

OM

|| Sri Rudra Bhashyam ||
(Commentary on the Sri Rudra Hymn)

INTRODUCTION (Upoddhatah)

May Shiva be victorious — the Lord of all knowledge (Ishana sarvavidyanam),
the Ruler of all souls, the possessor of unbroken omniscience,
the Guru of the universe, the remover of all afflictions. || 1 ||

I expound the Shataraudriya — the section of the Yajurveda —
so that a thorough and complete commentary on the Rudra hymns
may be established, drawing out its essence. || 2 ||

This work is dedicated as a humble service
to those devotees who wear sacred ash as their armor,
who bear garlands of Rudraksha beads,
whose heads are crowned with matted locks
and who stand at the four cardinal directions,
whose hearts are purified by the daily recitation of the Shataraudriya (the Shri Rudra),
and who are praised by the lord of the immortals (Indra). || 3 ||

It is indeed well known from the Shruti (Vedic scriptures), Smriti (traditional law texts), Itihasa (epics), and Purana (ancient sacred narratives) that the Sri Rudra Upanishad (the Shri Rudra hymn) is supreme among all the Vedas. Thus, in the Jabalupanishad (Jabala Upanishad):

---
Sri Rudra Bhashyam
---

[Continuing the Introduction]

When the brahmacharins (students of sacred knowledge) asked [Yajnavalkya]: "By the recitation of what [mantra] does one attain immortality? Please tell us" — this being a question about the means of liberation through recitation (japa) — "Yajnavalkya replied: 'By the Shataraudriya.' These indeed are the names of the Immortal (the Eternal Lord). By these one verily becomes immortal." By this, the primacy of the Sri Rudra Upanishad — denoted by the word Shataraudriya — as a means of liberation through japa (recitation) is established.

[On the etymology of the word "Shataraudriya":]

The word "Shataraudriya" means: "that in which one hundred Rudras are the presiding deity" — thus, from the root Shataraudra, by the grammatical rule "sasyaita devata" (in the chapter on presiding deities), the sutra "Shataraudraddasha" enables the formation of the two grammatical forms "Shataraudriya" and "Shataraudriya" through the suffixes ca and cha respectively, as established by grammarians.

Furthermore, the word "shata" (hundred) signifies infinity, as the Shruti declares: "A hundred means infinite" (shatam anantam bhavati); and as the Mahabharata states: "Hundred, thousand, and all such words denote the inexhaustible." In this text, Rudras of infinite number are expounded, as the Veda itself declares: "Thousands upon thousands of Rudras dwell upon the earth" and "The Rudras who are spread in thousands across the directions around this [world]." Their status as deities is due to their being proclaimed by the mantras, in accordance with the Nirukta (etymology) author's statement: "The deity is the intended meaning of the mantra."

Alternatively, "Rudriya" means "that which belongs to Rudra" — by the suffix cha in the rule "shridhadhaccha," and also by the suffix ca, since the suffix ca is seen applied to the word Rudra alone in the sense of relationship, as in "ya te agne rudriya tanur yedha tokaya" and similar passages. Thus the word "Rudriya" refers to the Sri Rudra Upanishad, which is the property of (or connected with) Rudra. "Shataraudriya" then means: "the Rudriya recited across one hundred branches [of the Veda]." This is stated [elsewhere]:

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     3
---

In the Skanda Purana's Suta Samhita: "The flower of the Shataraudriya extends across one hundred branches [of the Veda]. Therefore, by the mere recitation of it, one is freed from all sins." Bhattabhaskara also states: "The Rudra Upanishad is transmitted in one hundred branches of the Yajurveda."

Alternatively, "Rudriya" means the sacred names of Rudra; "Shataraudriya" then means: "that which contains a hundred — meaning infinite — sacred names of Rudra within it." This is supported by the Jabala Upanishad's declaration: "These are verily the names of the Immortal." And the Supreme Lord himself declares in the Kurma Purana: "Hear, O sinless one, these secret names of mine."

In the Mahabharata, the Dronaparva contains a chapter called the Shataraudriya-adhyaya. There, having first proclaimed the infinite nature of the Lord's names — "In this world, this [Lord] has many names, each of which is truly meaningful" — the text cites the Veda's Shataraudriya as the authoritative support for that infinity: "And the supreme Shataraudriya found in the Veda is well known [as the source of his names]." By this, it is declared that the infinity of Rudra's names is the very basis of the designation "Shataraudriya."

Moreover, one should not object that names not listed by the Naighantuka (the Vedic lexicon, Nighantu) and similar works cannot truly be considered names of the Supreme Lord. For the Veda itself directly declares their status as names — "These are verily the names of the Immortal" — and it is not proper to abandon what is directly stated by the Veda on account of the Naighantuka. Furthermore, the Kurma Purana's statement "These are my secret names" reveals that these are secret Vedic names, and therefore any absence from the Naighantuka is naturally explained — for the Nighantu compilers concern themselves only with names used in well-known poetic works such as plays and secular poetry, and do not engage with secret Vedic passages. Indeed, well over a thousand Vedic designations can be found that are not included in the Naighantuka — for example: "Tat is verily the name of this great being," "That is the designation of water: ruja, which flows," and "That is called Ud," and so on. The absence of such names from the Naighantuka does not negate their status as names; rather, their secrecy within the Veda actually corroborates it.

[As for the objection that] their status as the Lord's names is understood from Mahabharata and Bhagavata passages such as "OM tat sat — this triad of words is declared in the Brahman" and "Like clouds blazing with lightning" — one may be surprised at such misplaced trust, whereby one who does not believe what the Veda directly states would accept something only on the authority of an Itihasa or Purana passage. Moreover, the very Mahabharata passage we have already cited — "In this world, this Lord has many names, each truly meaningful; and the supreme Shataraudriya in the Veda is the renowned source of them" — itself demonstrates this. And there are individual usages in Mahabharata and Bhagavata passages such as: "Harikeshas-tathetyu, the radiant lord with long sight," "Your form is inaccessible to Giritra, the guardians of the world, Vishnu, and the lord of the gods," "Then Midhah was proclaimed," "Then the most bountiful Midhustama was praised" — where names like Harikesha, Midhustama and others, known from the Shri Rudra, appear used [outside the Veda]. Therefore it is hereby established that the Naighantuka compilers have no claim over such names. Now let us follow the main topic —

---
4                              Sri Rudra Bhashyam.
---

[Continuing discussion of the Rudradhyaya's glory:]

...the greatness of the Rudra chapter. Let us now trace it.

Although the statement "Yajnavalkya replied: 'By the Shataraudriya'" establishes only that the Shataraudriya is the means of liberation through recitation (japa), nonetheless, upon careful examination of the structure of the question and the answer, it is established that the Shataraudriya is supreme among all Upanishads. We explain this as follows:

The true state of affairs is this: the Upanishads are means of liberation — liberation being attained exclusively through the knowledge (jnana) of the unity of Brahman and Atman — and since the Upanishads serve as means of liberation both by positive co-presence (anvaya) and negative co-absence (vyatireka), they are the Upanishads meant for recitation as a means of liberation. If this state of affairs were already known to the brahmacharins, then the question would not arise [coherently]; for once it is known that Upanishads in general are means of liberation through recitation, it makes no sense to ask again, "By the recitation of what does one attain immortality?" And if they did not yet know this, then the answer "By the Shataraudriya" would also not be appropriate. For when the general truth — that Upanishads are means of liberation through japa — is not yet known, it would be inappropriate to answer simply "By the Shataraudriya"; rather, the fitting reply would be "By an Upanishad." Otherwise, by the logical principle "a specific selection serves to exclude what is left out" (visheshagrahana sheshapratisheddhartha), there would arise the unwanted conclusion that Upanishads other than the Shataraudriya are not means of liberation through recitation — just as if, in response to "Who in this town is learned?", one says "Devadatta is learned," it implies that others like Yajnadatta are not learned. Therefore, what must be understood is that the question and answer share the same reference point: having established in general that all Upanishads are means of liberation through recitation, the question asks which among them is the highest means of recitation; the answer being: the Shataraudriya — "By the Shataraudriya." Therefore, when the doubt arises "How then is the Shataraudriya supreme among all Upanishads?", the answer given is: "These are verily the names of the Immortal" and so forth. The meaning of "etani" (these) is: the names — occurring in the Shataraudriya — are names of the Immortal (amrita), i.e., of the eternally liberated Supreme Lord. "What then follows from this?" — The text continues: "By these verily one becomes immortal" — the particle "ha va" here being emphatic — "by these very names alone one becomes free." Here, the idea is set aside that the contemplative knowledge of the Brahman-Atman unity established in the Upanishad [other than japa] is needed. The sacred names of the Supreme Lord contained in this text alone, by mere recitation (japa), bestow liberation; this is not the case with other Upanishads — hence its supremacy over all Upanishads. The particle "etani ha va" also hints at the tradition of its occurrence across one hundred branches [of the Veda]. This further demonstrates that although all the names are indeed names of the Supreme Lord — as the Shruti declares in "Ima Rudraya sthiradhanvane girah" (these praises are for Rudra of the firm bow), showing that all words in the universe directly denote the Supreme Lord; and as Ashvalayana, after introducing Shiva, states "All these are verily his names" — nevertheless, because of its being well known by its presence across one hundred branches [of the Veda] and as the foremost means of liberation, it is pre-eminently called "the names," just as among the thirty-six days that constitute a certain sacrificial rite, twelve are designated as the principal days by the term "dvadashaha" (twelve-day sacrifice), even though all are days of equal standing.

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     5
---

[Continuing the discussion of supremacy:]

This entire meaning is demonstrated in the Kurma Purana. There, after describing how Parameshvara (the Supreme Lord) was pleased with the royal sage Vasumanas, who had performed the Gayatri upasana (meditation on the Gayatri mantra) for a hundred years by the power of a boon received from Brahma, the following is recited:

"Then the greatly pleased Mahadeva (Shiva) spoke to the king: 'O sinless one, hear these secret names of mine — sung in all the Vedas, they are the destroyers of samsara (the cycle of rebirth). Worship always with these sacred Name-words, remaining pure. Recite with a single-pointed mind, with your mind fixed on me alone, this Shataraudriya chapter — the supreme essence of the Yajurveda. Let a brahmachari (celibate student), eating sparingly, devoted to sacred ash (bhasma), composed in mind, recite the Rudra [hymn] till death — he attains the highest state.'"

Here, having first established the supremacy of the Shataraudriya within the Yajurveda through the phrase "the supreme essence of the Yajurveda" (yajusham saram), the further use of the word "uttama" (supreme) refers to its supremacy over the Rigveda and other Vedas as well. Thus the injunction is: "One should recite this Rudradhyaya chapter, which is supreme over all the Vedas." By this, the meaning intended by the Jabala Shruti — "Yajnavalkya replied..." — is displayed.

In that context, to answer the question "How is it supreme over all Vedas?", the phrase "destroyers of samsara" is used as a qualifier for the names. The idea conveyed is this: not only does the mere recitation of these divine names destroy samsara, but even prostration (namaskara) to them — since the names are themselves expressions of the Lord's being — accomplishes this. Hence [the text continues]: "Worship always with these sacred Name-words" — since the verbal recitation (vacika) of these mantras that constitute acts of prostration to the Lord is itself a form of namaskara (prostration), one who recites them has also effectively performed acts of prostration. Therefore, if the bare names alone destroy samsara, how much more so if namaskara is also offered to them — the import is that they uproot the suffering of samsara altogether.

By this, the Shruti passage "By these verily one becomes immortal" is explained [through the Kurma Purana]. And the passage "These are verily the names of the Immortal" is explained by "These are my secret names." In this correspondence: "etani" (these) is represented by "imani" (these); "amritasya" (of the Immortal) is illustrated by "me" (mine), pointing to the meaning of the word amrita. "Etani ha vai" (these indeed) corresponds to "sarvavedeshugitani" (sung in all the Vedas), which shows the tradition of occurrence across all Vedic branches — meaning their fame of being present in all the hundred Vedic branches. The word "rahasyani" (secret) indicates that they are not covered by the Naighantuka commentators, since they are secret Vedic terms. The word "anagha" (O sinless one) implies that one who has exhausted his sins through a hundred years of Gayatri upasana has the qualification to engage with the Rudradhyaya without obstruction.

Thus, when liberation is available, yet through the defect of disbelief (ashraddha) the world wallows in samsara — that indeed is the power of sin. And this is illustrated by a story from the Brahma-uttara-khanda. There — after the repetition of the Shri Rudra had destroyed all sins, so that there was no longer any sinner left in the world, and Yama (the god of death), his scribes Chitragupta and others having become jobless, informed Brahma, who thereupon set in motion two daughters of Doubt born of Disbelief —

---
6                              Sri Rudra Bhashyam.
---

[Continuing the Introduction — on the glory of Rudradhyaya:]

...the world became turned away from Shri Rudra, afflicted by those [daughters of Disbelief] named Ashraddha (lack of faith) and Durmedha (poor intellect). Those passages [from the Purana] will be cited at the appropriate place in this work. Therefore it is established: this section of the Jabala Upanishad, further corroborated by the Kurma Purana, declares the Rudradhyaya to be supreme over all the Vedas. This meaning will indeed be understood in the hearts of scholars.

The Parashara Purana adds its corroboration: "The Jabala Upanishad speaks for the sake of [attaining] my knowledge; it teaches with care the primary means of liberation for living beings. Avimukta (Kashi) is my supreme and holy kshetra (sacred ground). Recitation of the Shataraudriya and excellent renunciation (sannyasa) are the primary means" — by this, the Jabala Shruti's meaning that the Shataraudriya is a means toward knowledge [and liberation] is stated most explicitly. And there, the phrase "paramam mama" (my supreme [seat]) qualifies "Avimukta" — meaning that it is supreme by the name Pranava (OM) — as the Shruti declares: "Here, as the life-breaths depart from a being, Rudra imparts to him the Taraka Brahman (the liberating knowledge of Brahman), by which he becomes immortal and is liberated." Thus among upasanas (meditative practices), the upasana at Avimukta; among things to be recited, the Shataraudriya; and among ashrams (stages of life), sannyasa — these three are declared by the revered Parashara to be the foremost means of knowledge [and liberation]. This demonstrates [the Shataraudriya's] supremacy over all the Vedas; the Jabala Shruti, corroborated by that Purana, is the authority here.

Furthermore, in the Kaivalya Upanishad alone, the Self-existent Brahma teaches the supreme Brahman-Atman identity to Ashvalayana and then teaches this [text] as the means to it: "He who studies the Shataraudriya becomes purified as if by fire, purified from the sin of drinking liquor, purified from the sin of brahminicide, purified from the sin of things done and left undone — and therefore he becomes entitled to dwell in Avimukta (the eternal abode). One of whatever ashram should always recite the Rudra, or at least once. By this, one attains knowledge — the destroyer of the ocean of samsara."

Here the first injunction is: "One who desires knowledge that liberates from samsara should recite the Shataraudriya." And in this context, when one asks what reason there could be for teaching the Shataraudriya as the means of knowledge, to the exclusion of all else in the vast ocean of the Veda consisting of the karma-kanda, upasana-kanda, and jnana-kanda — the text first demonstrates its supremacy over the karma-kanda (the portion dealing with ritual action) through the words from "He who studies the Shataraudriya" up to "purified from things done and left undone."

This is corroborated in the Suta Samhita: "He who, performing brahmacharya (celibacy) together with a partner, daily practices the Shataraudriya — he is freed from being burned in fire, from drinking liquor, from doing what ought not to be done, and from brahminicide — thus say the followers of the Kaivalya branch." Here, "from doing what ought not to be done" (akrtyacharanat) explains the phrase "purified from things done and left undone" (krtyakrtyat puto bhavati) — "krityakritya" means "that which, though done as if a ritual act, is in fact an improper deed," i.e., the performance of a forbidden act; "purified from that" is the meaning. Thus the intent of the Shruti is shown: the student of the Rudradhyaya is not only purified from brahminicide and liquor drinking, but from all sins whatsoever.

This meaning is elaborated at length in the Brahma-uttara-khanda. There:

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     7
---

[Continuing citation from the Brahma-uttara-khanda:]

"In the supreme transcendent realm, beyond darkness, shines the formless, pure, eternal Absolute — the Light of consciousness and bliss. That is the Atman alone, Shiva himself. He, in the beginning, creating Brahma from himself in the form of rajas (the quality of activity), —

---
8                              Sri Rudra Bhashyam.
---

[Continuing citation from the Brahma-uttara-khanda:]

"...assigned him to the work of creation and gave him the Vedas. Then again the Lord of the Universe, the essence of all Atman (Self), gave [Brahma] the Rudradhyaya — the supreme summary of all Upanishads. This [teaching] was first honoured by the ancient sages led by Atri and others. The gods received it from them, and their disciples received it in turn. This is your supreme mantra; this is your supreme austerity. The recitation of the Rudradhyaya is, for human beings, the supreme means of liberation (kaivalya). Those who are burdened with great sins, those who have committed minor transgressions — even they, through recitation of the Rudradhyaya, attain the highest state."

Having thus introduced the subject, the text recites: "The fruit of virtuous action is heaven; of sinful action, hell — these are the two paths, created by the Creator, bounded by the Indra and Antaka (Yama). Desire, anger, greed, pride, and other [vices] — these became the sons of Adharma (unrighteousness) and the lords of hell. From anger arose Patraghna (destroyer of the worthy), and then Matravadhah (matricide). Brahmahatya (brahminicide) arose as a daughter [of Adharma]; thereafter Gurutalpa (violation of the guru's bed), Surapana (drinking of liquor), and Pulkasi-love [sinful liaisons] — these are said to be the chief sons of Kama (desire). Deva-svahara (theft of the gods' property), Brahma-svahara (theft of Brahmic property), Suvarnasteyam (theft of gold) — these are counted among the sons of Greed. Calling together these outcaste [lords of sin], Yama, the lord of the departed and overseer of sin, established them in the governance of hell. They, thus directed by Yama, all the lords of sin, filled the hells with their followers and attendant sinners. [But then], the recitation of the Rudradhyaya — a direct means of liberation — having begun to prevail, all those lords of sin fled in terror. They went and informed Yama, along with those attendant sinners: 'We have been rendered powerless to function in our respective domains of hell. Now we are not able ourselves to summon [sinners] in the world, O Lord! We have been scattered, as if without a home, by the power of Rudra-recitation. In every village, on the banks of rivers, and in holy sanctuaries — everywhere is now filled with Rudra-reciters; how can we carry out our work? Even a thousand expiations (prayashcitta) we do not reckon as anything — but the recitation of the Rudra even for a moment we cannot endure. For us, who subsist on the great sinners, the Rudra-recitation is the supreme thief; the Rudra-recitation is the great poison.' Having been thus informed by the lords of sin, Yama himself came to Brahma and reported all this to him. Hearing this report, Brahma, greatly distressed by the predicament caused by the Rudra-recitation, contrived a stratagem to obstruct it. He sent into the mortal world two daughters of Doubt — Ashraddha (lack of faith) and Durmedha (poor intellect) — disguised as the daughters of Faith. Bewildered by those two, the world became turned away from the Rudradhyaya; and Yama, having returned to his abode, felt as if his purpose were accomplished."

Similarly, in other Puranas as well this meaning is indicated everywhere; but all of that is not written here out of fear of excessive length.

In this way, the capability of the Rudradhyaya to serve as the expiation for all sins — established by the Shruti and corroborated by the Puranas — is also recalled by the authors of the Smritis (law codes). For instance:

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     9
---

[Smriti texts on the expiatory power of Rudra recitation:]

The Shatatapa Smriti declares: "One who has drunk liquor, visited the abode of a guru's wife, committed theft, or caused the death of a brahmin — if he is covered with sacred ash and sleeps on a bed of ash, and recites the Rudradhyaya, he is freed from all sins."

The Atri Smriti declares: "A knower of dharma who has recited even eleven Rudras — even if he has been touched by great sins — is certainly freed from them; of this there is no doubt."

The Yajnavalkya Smriti declares: "A drinker of liquor, a thief of gold — if he recites the Rudra while standing in water, or recites the Sahasrashirsha (the text beginning 'sahasrashirsha') — he is freed from all transgressions."

A Smriti text declares: "One who recites the Veda even once is purified that very day. One who recites eleven Rudras is purified at that very instant."

Similarly, there are many other passages that could be cited. Thus, through the declaration that the Shataraudriya averts all sins — "He who studies the Shataraudriya becomes purified as if by fire... purified from things done and left undone" — its supremacy over the karma-kanda (the ritual section of the Veda) is demonstrated. For the karma-kanda is helpful toward knowledge by virtue of generating the purity of mind (chitta-shuddhi) required for knowledge; and such purity of mind — which consists of the removal of sins generated by performing all the rites prescribed in the karma-kanda — is achieved at once through a single recitation of the Rudra. Thus its supremacy over the entire karma-kanda is established.

Nor should one object: "Since the Shri Rudra produces purity of mind, which is the fruit of the karma-kanda, does it not also produce the fruits of the karma-kanda such as heaven? How then can it be superior to those?" The Suta Samhita explains: "Just as a tree's branches flourish when the root is nourished, so when Shiva is pleased through Rudra-recitation, all his associated deities are equally pleased." Thus for the devoted reciter of the Rudradhyaya who pleases the Supreme Lord, the fruits of pleasing Indra, Agni, and other deities — namely heaven and other results — no longer need to be separately sought, because those very fruits, in an eager competition as it were, present themselves of their own accord. This is established by declarations such as: "Whatever one desires, one attains it — of this there is no doubt," and "For one desirous of the other world, or one wishing to be freed from disease, or desirous of prosperity, or desirous of peace, or desirous of long life, or desirous of health — by performing this [Rudra recitation] he attains all these." Furthermore, Shaiva Purana passages such as "One who recites the Rudra with the mind fixed on Maheshvara — [gains results] far surpassing [a gift of] land extending to the oceans, adorned with mountains and forests, filled with wealth, gold, and healing herbs; such is the result of a single Rudra recitation" — by these, the Shri Rudra's capacity to produce results exceeding the specific fruits of each karma [in proportion] is established. Therefore it is conclusively proven that the Shri Rudra is supreme over the entire karma-kanda by virtue of generating all its fruits. This is precisely why the ritual acts of Agnihotra, Darsha-purnamasa, Panchayajna, Chaturmasya, Jyotishtoma, Sattrasavana, and Samvatsara — all karmas — are brought within the scope of the Shri Rudra through their results, and their status as ancillaries of the Shri Rudra is asserted in the Shri Rudra Laghu-nyasa through [formulas] such as "Aghihotratmane hridayaya namah" (salutation to the heart, which is the essence of the Agnihotra) and so on. But let that pass for now. Let us follow the main topic.

Thus the argument for the karma-kanda supremacy of the Shataraudriya has been stated. Now —

---
10                             Sri Rudra Bhashyam.
---

[On the Shataraudriya's supremacy in the Upasana-kanda (section on meditation):]

The text now demonstrates its supremacy in the upasana-kanda (the section of the Veda dealing with meditative practice) through the statement: "Therefore, one becomes established in Avimukta (the eternal abode)." Here, the word "tasmat" (therefore) refers back to the Shataraudriya study mentioned in the earlier sentence "He who studies the Shataraudriya"; thus the meaning is: "Through the study of the Shataraudriya, one becomes established in Avimukta." This means: one has as if performed the upasana (meditative practice) of taking refuge in Avimukta; one attains the fruit of the upasana of Avimukta. By this, its supremacy in the upasana-kanda is proven.

For in the Jabala Upanishad, the upasana of Avimukta is prescribed in the words "Therefore one should resort to Avimukta," and its fruit is stated in "As the life-breaths depart from a being, Rudra imparts to him the Taraka Brahman (the liberating knowledge of Brahman), by which he becomes immortal and is liberated." There, the upasana of Avimukta is of two kinds — adhyatmika (internal, pertaining to the individual self) and adhibhautika (external, pertaining to the physical location). The adhyatmika form is: meditation on the Supreme Lord at the junction of the eyebrow-center and the nose [i.e., the ajna chakra, the point between the brows]; the adhibhautika form is: the vision of the Supreme Lord in Kashi (Varanasi), the sacred field of Avimukta. Such is the settled determination of the Shankara school of interpretation.

Now, the fruit of both these forms of upasana — namely, the knowledge called Taraka Brahmavidya (the saving knowledge of Brahman), which is the knowledge of Atman — is easily obtained through mere recitation of the Shri Rudra, without the laborious effort involved in the upasana of Avimukta. The adhyatmika Avimukta upasana does not require dying in Kashi, as the Shruti declares: "One who reaches that [junction] is as if reaching Kurukshetra; that itself is the sacred gathering of the gods, the seat of Brahman for all beings — for here, as the life-breaths depart —

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     11
---

[Continuing on the supremacy in the upasana-kanda:]

"...a being, Rudra imparts to him the Taraka Brahman, by which he becomes immortal and is liberated." Nevertheless, even the adhyatmika upasana requires considerably greater effort compared to mere recitation (japa). This is precisely why, in the Jabala Upanishad — after teaching the upasana of Avimukta, for those brahmacharins who are incapable of that effortful meditative practice requiring single-pointedness of mind and similar disciplines, and who desire liberation through mere recitation — the question "By the recitation of what does one attain immortality?" is followed by the answer "By the Shataraudriya," and the supporting reason is given: "By these verily one becomes immortal." In this way, the Shataraudriya, which bestows the fruit of the adhyatmika Avimukta upasana without effort, is proven to be supreme over that upasana; and since it is supreme over that [most exalted] upasana, it is proven to be supreme over all upasanas. For since all upasanas require single-pointedness of mind as their instrument, the Shri Rudra — which generates the same fruit without effort — thereby attains the status of being supreme over the entire upasana-kanda.

Now, the specific mention of Avimukta — when it would have sufficed to say "one becomes established in all upasanas" — is for the following reason: in the Jabala Upanishad, both Avimukta and the Shri Rudra are taught as means of knowledge and liberation, and [the text] wishes to show that even when compared to Avimukta, the Shataraudriya is superior. The specific mention [of Avimukta] is to prevent the mistaken conclusion (by the rule of exclusion) that both are equal in their capacity to serve as means of knowledge, since both are taught in the same Upanishad for the same purpose. This is the import, and all else is elegant in its own right.

Thus the argument for the upasana-kanda supremacy of the Shataraudriya has been stated. Now, regarding the jnana-kanda —

---
12                             Sri Rudra Bhashyam.
---

[On the Shataraudriya's supremacy in the Jnana-kanda (section on knowledge):]

The text demonstrates the jnana-kanda supremacy through the statement: "One of whatever ashram should always recite the Rudra, or at least once. By this one attains knowledge — the destroyer of the ocean of samsara." Here, through the phrase "always or at least once" (sarvada sakrid va), it is stated by way of option (vikalpa) that even a single recitation of the Shri Rudra can produce the attainment of knowledge. This constitutes the argument for the jnana-kanda supremacy. For it is established in the "Avritti-adhikarana" (the section on repetition [in the Brahmasutras]) that the jnana-kanda consists of the Upanishads, and that they are to be studied repeatedly for the sake of attaining knowledge. Thus, the Upanishads — which are to be repeatedly studied — are to be recited continually; but the Shri Rudra produces knowledge through even a single recitation. Hence its supremacy over all the jnana-kanda is proven.

Although, as with other Upanishads, repeated hearing, reflection, and deep meditation (shravana, manana, nididhyasana) until the direct realization of Brahman-Atman are certainly required even for the Shri Rudra Upanishad — two modes of arising of knowledge are possible here: one through the method of hearing, reflection, and so on; and another through the method described in the Jabala Shruti — "By these verily one becomes immortal" — where, without hearing and the rest, by mere recitation alone, the divine names of the Supreme Lord, which are themselves eager to liberate, bring about the arising of knowledge through the grace of the Lord who thereby approaches of his own accord. For the first mode, the repeated recitation of the Shri Rudra prompted by [the practice of] hearing and so forth is applicable. For the second mode, through the prior conditions of devotion to sacred ash (bhasmanishtha) and firm attachment to the Supreme Lord as taught in the Kurma Purana, knowledge arises through Shiva's grace from the daily single recitation of the Rudra. Taking the second of these two modes as the basis, the intent of the Shruti is this: the supremacy of the Shri Rudra over all [texts of the jnana-kanda] is established.

The statement "One of whatever ashram" (ayashrami) does not intend an enumeration restricting the qualification to non-householders — since the brahmacharins in the Jabala Upanishad —

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     13
---

[Smriti texts on the expiatory power of Rudra recitation — continued:]

The Shatatapa Smriti declares: "One who has drunk liquor, visited the dwelling of a guru's wife [in violation of dharma], committed theft, or perpetrated brahminicide — if he is smeared with sacred ash and sleeps on a bed of ash, and recites the Rudradhyaya, he is freed from all sins."

The Atri Smriti declares: "A knower of dharma who has recited even eleven Rudras — even if touched by great sins — is certainly freed from them. Of this there is no doubt."

The Yajnavalkya Smriti declares: "A drinker of liquor, a thief of gold — if he stands in water and recites the Rudra, or recites the Sahasrashirsha (the Purusha Sukta beginning 'Sahasrashirsha Purushah') — he is freed from all transgressions."

A further Smriti text declares: "One who recites the Veda even once is purified that very day. One who recites eleven Rudras is purified at that very moment."

Similarly, there are many other passages that could be cited. Therefore, through the declaration that the Shataraudriya averts all sins — "He who studies the Shataraudriya is purified as if by fire... purified from things done and left undone" — its supremacy over the karma-kanda (the ritual section) is demonstrated.

For the karma-kanda helps toward knowledge by generating the purity of mind (chitta-shuddhi) that knowledge requires; and such purity of mind — which consists of the removal of sins accrued through failure to perform the rites of the entire karma-kanda — is achieved all at once through a single recitation of the Rudra. Thus the Shri Rudra's supremacy over the entire karma-kanda is established.

Nor should one object: "The Shri Rudra produces the purity of mind that is a fruit of the karma-kanda — but since it does not directly produce karma-kanda fruits like heaven and so on, how can it be said to be superior to the karma-kanda?" The Suta Samhita resolves this: "Just as the branches of a tree flourish when its root is watered, so when Shiva is pleased through Rudra recitation, all his associated deities are equally pleased. Therefore, through Rudra recitation alone, both liberation and all material goals become accomplished." Thus, for the devoted reciter of the Rudradhyaya — who pleases the Supreme Lord — when the Lord himself is pleased, the attendant fruits of pleasing Indra, Agni, and other deities (namely heaven and similar results) need no separate pursuit, for those very fruits, competing as if in eagerness, present themselves spontaneously. This is established by Shruti and Smriti passages such as: "Whatever one wishes, one obtains — of this there is no doubt," and "For one desiring another world, or freedom from disease, or prosperity, or peace, or long life, or health — by performing this [Rudra recitation] one obtains all of these." Furthermore, by Shaiva Purana passages such as: "One who constantly recites the Rudra while meditating on Maheshvara attains a result greater than [donating] land extending to the oceans, adorned with mountains and forests, filled with wealth, gold, and medicinal plants — by a single recitation of the Rudra he obtains more than that" — the capacity of the Shri Rudra to produce fruits surpassing the specific results of each individual karma is established. Therefore it is conclusively proven that the Shri Rudra is supreme over the entire karma-kanda by virtue of generating all its fruits.

This is precisely why all the ritual acts — Agnihotra (the twice-daily fire oblation), Darsha-purnamasa (new and full moon rites), Panchayajna, Chaturmasya (four-monthly rites), Jyotishtoma (the soma sacrifice), Sattrasavana (year-long soma session), and Samvatsara (annual rite) — all karmas — being subsumed within the Shri Rudra through their fruits, are inserted as ancillaries of the Shri Rudra in the Shri Rudra Laghu-nyasa (the brief nyasa of the Shri Rudra) through formulas such as "Agnihotratmane hridayaya namah" (salutation to the heart, which is the essence of the Agnihotra) and so on. But let us leave this for now and follow the main topic.

Thus the argument for the karma-kanda supremacy of the Shataraudriya has been stated. Now —

---
14                             Sri Rudra Bhashyam.
---

[On the Shataraudriya's supremacy in the Upasana-kanda (meditative section):]

The text demonstrates its supremacy in the upasana-kanda through the statement: "Therefore one becomes established in Avimukta (the eternal abode, i.e., Kashi)." Here, "tasmat" (therefore) refers back to the Shataraudriya study mentioned earlier in "He who studies the Shataraudriya"; thus the meaning is: "Through the study of the Shataraudriya, one becomes established in Avimukta." That is, one has as if performed the upasana of taking refuge in Avimukta; one attains the fruit of the Avimukta upasana. By this, the Shataraudriya's supremacy in the upasana-kanda is proven.

For in the Jabala Upanishad, the upasana of Avimukta is enjoined in the words "Therefore one should resort to Avimukta," and its fruit is stated as "As the life-breaths depart from a being, Rudra imparts to him the Taraka Brahman (the saving knowledge of Brahman), by which he becomes immortal and is liberated." That Avimukta upasana is of two kinds: adhyatmika (internal, pertaining to the individual self) and adhibhautika (external, pertaining to a sacred location). Of these, the meditation on the Supreme Lord at the junction of the eyebrows and the nose-tip (i.e., the ajna cakra, the junction of the brow and the nasal passage) is the adhyatmika form; the vision of the Supreme Lord at Kashi — the sacred field called Avimukta — is the adhibhautika form. Such is the settled position of the school of Shankaracharya.

Now, the fruit of both these forms of Avimukta upasana — the knowledge of the Atman called Taraka Brahmavidya (the saving knowledge of Brahman) — is easily obtained through the mere recitation of the Shri Rudra, without the laborious effort required in the upasana of Avimukta itself. For the adhibhautika Avimukta upasana, the Taraka Brahmavidya is not obtainable without dying in Kashi; hence special effort is needed there. For the adhyatmika form, there is no such dependence on dying in Kashi — as the Shruti declares: "One who reaches that [junction of brows and nose] is as if going to Kurukshetra itself; that itself is the sacred assembly of the gods, the seat of Brahman for all beings — for here, as the life-breaths depart from a being, Rudra imparts to him the Taraka Brahman, by which he becomes immortal and is liberated." Nevertheless, even this adhyatmika upasana requires considerably greater effort compared to japa (recitation). This is precisely why — in the Jabala Upanishad, after the teaching of the Avimukta upasana — for brahmacharins who are unable to perform the effortful upasana requiring single-pointedness of mind and similar disciplines, and who desire liberation through mere recitation, the question arises "By the recitation of what does one attain immortality?" and the answer is "By the Shataraudriya," with the supporting reason "By these verily one becomes immortal."

Thus the Shataraudriya — which bestows the fruit of the adhyatmika Avimukta upasana without effort — is shown to be superior to that upasana; and since it is superior to [even] that [most exalted] upasana, its supremacy over all upasanas is proven. For since all upasanas require single-pointedness of mind as their instrument, the Shri Rudra — which generates the same fruit without such effort — naturally surpasses the entire upasana-kanda.

Now, the reason for specifically mentioning Avimukta — when the text could have simply said "one becomes [as if having done] all upasanas" — is as follows: since in the Jabala Upanishad both Avimukta upasana and the Shri Rudra are taught as means of knowledge for the same purpose, there might arise the erroneous impression — following the rule "equality in specification implies equality in substance" — that both are equal as means of knowledge. To prevent this misunderstanding and to demonstrate that even compared to Avimukta upasana the Shataraudriya is superior, the specific mention of Avimukta is made. This is the import, and all else is properly elegant.

Thus the argument for the upasana-kanda supremacy of the Shataraudriya has been stated. Now, regarding the jnana-kanda supremacy —

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     15
---

[On the Shataraudriya's supremacy in the Jnana-kanda (section on knowledge):]

The text demonstrates the jnana-kanda supremacy through the statement: "One of whatever ashram should always recite the Rudra, or at least once. By this, one attains knowledge — the destroyer of the ocean of samsara."

Here, through the phrase "always, or at least once" (sarvada sakrid va), offering the option that even a single recitation of the Shri Rudra can produce the attainment of knowledge, its argument for jnana-kanda supremacy is established. For it is settled in the "Avritti-adhikarana" (the section on repetition in the Brahmasutras) that the jnana-kanda consists of the Upanishads, and that they are to be studied repeatedly in order to attain knowledge. Thus, since Upanishads in general are to be repeated [for knowledge], and since the Shri Rudra produces knowledge even through a single recitation, its supremacy over the entire jnana-kanda is proven.

Although — just as with other Upanishads — repeated hearing, reflection, and deep meditation (shravana, manana, nididhyasana) until the direct realization of the Brahman-Atman identity is also applicable to the Shri Rudra Upanishad, nevertheless there are two modes by which knowledge arises here: one is through the method of hearing, reflection, and so on; the other is through the method described in the Jabala Shruti — "By these verily one becomes immortal" — whereby, without hearing and the rest, by mere recitation alone, the divine sacred names of the Supreme Lord — names that are themselves eager to liberate — bring about the arising of knowledge through the grace of the Lord, who thereby approaches [the devotee] of his own accord.

In the first mode, the repeated recitation of the Shri Rudra is applicable, prompted by the practice of hearing and so on. In the second mode, through the prior conditions prescribed in the Kurma Purana — namely devotion to sacred ash (bhasmanishtha) and firm absorption in the Supreme Lord — knowledge arises through Shiva's grace from the daily single recitation of the Rudra.

Taking the second of these modes as the basis, the intent of the Shruti is: the supremacy of the Shri Rudra over all other means is established.

The statement "One of whatever ashram" (ayashrami) does not intend a restriction (parisankhya) meaning "only for those not in the householder stage" — for in the Jabala Upanishad, brahmacharins [who belong to the householder stage as well] are the questioners —
