---

[Continuing on the jnana-kanda supremacy — the Kaivalya Upanishad passage:]

"...the gods' seat of worship, the abode of Brahman for all beings — here, as the life-breaths depart from a being, Rudra imparts to him the Taraka Brahman, by which he becomes immortal and is liberated." Yet even so, compared to japa (recitation), the adhyatmika Avimukta upasana still requires considerably greater effort. This is precisely why, in the Jabala Upanishad — after the teaching of the Avimukta upasana, for those brahmacharins who are incapable of that effortful upasana requiring single-pointedness of mind and similar practices, and who desire liberation through mere recitation — the question "By the recitation of what does one attain immortality?" is followed by the answer "By the Shataraudriya," with the supporting statement "By these very [names] one becomes immortal."

Thus the Shataraudriya — which bestows the fruit of the adhyatmika Avimukta upasana without effort — is demonstrated to be superior to that upasana; and since it surpasses that [most exalted] upasana, its supremacy over all upasanas is conclusively proven. For since all upasanas in their entirety require single-pointedness of mind as their instrument, the Shri Rudra — which effortlessly generates their fruit — attains the status of being supreme over the entire upasana-kanda.

Now, the reason for specifically mentioning "becomes established in Avimukta" — rather than saying more broadly "has as if performed all upasanas" — is this: in the Jabala Upanishad, both Avimukta and the Shri Rudra are taught for the same purpose as means of knowledge, and there may arise the impression of their equality as means [by the principle that specific mention implies equal standing]. To prevent this [mistaken equivalence] and to show that even compared to Avimukta, the Shataraudriya is superior, the specific mention of Avimukta is made — wishing to show its superiority relative to that comparison. This is the intent of the text, and all else is properly stated.

Thus the argument for the upasana-kanda supremacy has been stated. Now, regarding jnana-kanda supremacy:

---
Introduction (Upoddhatah).                                     17
---

[Continuing on the jnana-kanda supremacy:]

The text demonstrates jnana-kanda supremacy through the statement: "One of whatever ashram should always recite the Rudra, or at least once. By this one attains knowledge — the destroyer of the ocean of samsara."

Here, through the phrase "always, or at least once" (sarvada sakrid va), offering the option that even a single recitation of the Shri Rudra produces the attainment of knowledge, the argument for its jnana-kanda supremacy is established. For it is settled in the "Avritti-adhikarana" (the section on repetition in the Brahmasutras) that the jnana-kanda consists of the Upanishads, and that they are to be studied repeatedly in order to attain knowledge. Thus, [Upanishads] that require repeated recitation are superseded by the Shri Rudra, which generates knowledge through even a single recitation — hence its supremacy over all the jnana-kanda is proven.

Now, although it is also the case that for the Shri Rudra Upanishad — as for other Upanishads — repeated hearing, reflection, and deep meditation (shravana, manana, nididhyasana) until the direct realization of the Brahman-Atman identity may be required, nonetheless two distinct modes by which knowledge arises [from the Shri Rudra] are possible: one follows the method of hearing, reflection, and so on; the other — as described in the Jabala Shruti, "By these verily one becomes immortal" — arises through mere recitation alone, without the processes of hearing and so on. In this second mode, the sacred divine names of the Supreme Lord, which are themselves eager to bestow liberation, bring about the arising of knowledge through the grace of the Lord, who approaches [the devotee] spontaneously.

For the first mode, the repeated recitation of the Shri Rudra is operative, impelled by the practice of hearing and so on. For the second mode — through the prior conditions laid down in the Kurma Purana, namely devotion to sacred ash (bhasmanishtha) and complete absorption in the Supreme Lord — knowledge arises through the grace of Shiva from the daily single recitation of the Rudra. Taking this second mode as the basis, the intent of the Shruti is: the complete supremacy of the Shri Rudra [over all sections of the Veda] is established.

The statement "One of whatever ashram" (ayashrami japeth) does not make a restrictive enumeration (parisankhya) meaning "only those not of the householder stage should recite" — for in the Jabala Upanishad, the questioners are brahmacharins [who ask about recitation] —

[R. B. 2]

18                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

Furthermore, the instruction for the recitation of Śrī Rudra (Śrī Rudra japa) is confirmed by thousands of Purāṇic statements that accord with the Devadūna tradition — such as: "The mantra known as Śatarudrīya (the Hundred-Names-of-Rudra) is to be recited by householders, forest-dwellers, and celibate students alike." These Purāṇic testimonies contradict any restriction on who may recite it. Nor should it be said that the injunction "Let the supreme renunciant (atyāśramī) recite it" applies exclusively to sannyāsins (renunciants) in the context of the Rudra being prescribed for liberation; for Śrī Rudra is available to sannyāsins precisely by virtue of its status as an Upaniṣad (esoteric scripture) — "He gave the Rudra chapter, which is the essence of all the Upaniṣads" — and because the injunction "Let him always recite the Upaniṣad" mandates the recitation of Upaniṣads for sannyāsins.

Nor is there any lack of evidence for Śrī Rudra's status as an Upaniṣad. The Śiva Purāṇa declares: "The Rudra chapter has been established as the essence of all Upaniṣads. Whether one seeks enjoyment, liberation, or expiation — there is no path other than the Rudra Upaniṣad." Similarly, learned authorities such as Bhaṭṭa Bhāskara have used the term 'Upaniṣad' in connection with it, based on the recognized sense of the word upaniṣad. Indeed, the word 'upaniṣad' is used in two derived senses within the tradition: (1) Those who dwell near (upa-niṣad) the Supreme Brahman — meaning the Vedānta texts — since they aim directly at Brahman, unlike the ritual sections (karma-kāṇḍa) which prescribe worshipful sacrifices as indirect means; or (2) The word 'upaniṣad' is derived from the verbal root ṣad preceded by the prefixes upa and ni, with the sense of 'that which destroys' — meaning it destroys avidyā (ignorance), the root cause of saṃsāra (transmigration), and thus designates brahma-vidyā (knowledge of Brahman) directly. As an instrument of that knowledge, it also applies, as in "the Chāndogya is the living one" and similar usages. This twofold derivation is especially apt in the case of Śrī Rudra. Therefore, the mention of 'atyāśramī' (supreme renunciant) in the passage beginning "those who go, being students" in the Vājasaneya tradition is a synecdoche (upalakṣaṇa) for all āśramas (stages of life) in general; the meaning is that anyone in any of the four āśramas who recites Śrī Rudra daily — always, or even once — attains liberating knowledge.

In this way, the opening phrase "Whoever studies the Śatarudrīya" and the closing phrase "Let the supreme renunciant recite it" form a unified sentence (ekavākyatā), with the word 'atyāśramī' serving as a synecdoche for any person eligible to recite Śrī Rudra. The Sūta Saṃhitā likewise confirms this unity of meaning: "Whoever a man may be that recites the Śatarudrīya with faith, day after day — such a one, even after death in the great Viṣṇu's domain, attains lordly knowledge through that knowledge, and by that higher knowledge reaches the supreme destination. By householders, forest-dwellers, and celibate students alike..."

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             19

[Continuing the discussion of Śrī Rudra's status as the supreme Upaniṣad and the eligibility of all āśramas for its recitation]

...destroys [ignorance] — brahma-vidyā (knowledge of Brahman) directly, while in the Vedānta texts it functions as an instrument [of that knowledge], just as in the phrase "the Chāndogya is the living one" and the like. This twofold derivation is highly natural in the case of Śrī Rudra.

Therefore, the word 'atyāśramī' (supreme renunciant) in the passage from the Vājasaneya tradition beginning "students go..." is to be understood as a synecdoche (upalakṣaṇa) that stands for all four āśramas (stages of life); the meaning is: anyone belonging to any of the four āśramas who recites Śrī Rudra daily — always or even once — attains liberating knowledge.

Thus, just as the opening phrase "Whoever recites the Śatarudrīya" and the concluding phrase "Let the supreme renunciant recite it" form a unified sentence (ekavākyatā), with the word 'atyāśramī' denoting all persons eligible to recite Śrī Rudra. The statements of the Sūta Saṃhitā — "By householders, forest-dwellers, and celibate students alike" — are similarly to be interpreted as supporting this unity of meaning.

Since it is thus established: The general subject indicated in the opening phrase "Whoever recites the Śatarudrīya..." satisfies the expectation for an agent (kartṛ-kāṅkṣā) required by the concluding act of recitation (japa) in "Let him always, or even once, recite it" — one may then ask: What further purpose is served by inserting the word 'atyāśramī' to indicate the general agent by way of synecdoche? Hear this explanation: In the opening of the Kaivalya Upaniṣad, the teaching is given — "Not by ritual action, not by progeny, not by wealth; only by renunciation do some attain immortality" — thus teaching renunciation as the means to liberation. And in the Jābāla Upaniṣad,

20                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

After the teaching that Śrī Rudra is a means to liberation, in response to the question posed by Janaka to Yājñavalkya — "O Venerable One, please instruct me in the nature of sannyāsa (renunciation)" — the complete description of sannyāsa along with its accessories is taught, and then its efficacy as a means to liberation is declared: "The wandering monk, wearing ochre robes, with shaved head, possessing nothing, pure, free from malice, subsisting on alms — he becomes fit for Brahman."

Now, since both sannyāsa and Śrī Rudra equally serve as means to liberating knowledge (jñāna-sādhanatva), the word 'atyāśramī' (supreme renunciant) is introduced to accommodate both of them. By this word, the primary prescription of Rudra-japa (Rudra recitation) for the attainment of liberating knowledge is shown to be specifically for sannyāsins as well, and its superiority over sannyāsa alone is demonstrated. This is because, for the arising of liberating knowledge, sannyāsa depends upon Śrī Rudra, whereas Śrī Rudra — being prescribable as a recitation for liberation for brahmacarins (celibate students) and others as well — does not depend on sannyāsa. Such is the purpose of using the word 'atyāśramī' as a synecdoche for all āśramas.

Nor should it be objected that this interpretation conflicts with the prohibition against sannyāsins reciting mantras other than Praṇava (Oṃ): For the Āraṇyaka Upaniṣad text — "Let him recite the Upaniṣad" — prescribes Śrī Rudra for sannyāsins in general by virtue of its status as an Upaniṣad; further, texts of Viśveśvara-smṛti and others specifically prescribe Rudra recitation for sannyāsins: "Let the ascetic always recite Śrī Rudra and the Praṇava"; and in the Kaivalya Upaniṣad, "Let the supreme renunciant always or even once recite it" prescribes Śrī Rudra primarily as the means of direct liberating knowledge in relation to the entire section on knowledge. By these three means of authority, the mandatory nature of Śrī Rudra recitation for sannyāsins aspiring to liberation is firmly established.

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             21

[Continuing the discussion on the prohibition of mantras other than Praṇava for sannyāsins]

...and therefore the scriptural injunction against reciting mantras other than [Praṇava] must necessarily be understood as applying to mantras other than Śrī Rudra. As for the accomplished sannyāsin (vidvān sannyāsin) who has fulfilled all duties — since the Gītā declares "He has nothing further to do" — both the injunction of Praṇava and the prohibition of other mantras are inapplicable to him; all injunctive and prohibitive scriptures apply only to those who are ignorant (avidvat) and the like. Therefore, it is firmly established that the statement "Let the supreme renunciant always, or even once, recite it" declares Śrī Rudra to be the supreme teaching in the section on liberating knowledge (jñāna-kāṇḍa).

Nor can it be argued that this passage does not concern itself with Śrī Rudra's capacity to generate liberating knowledge through a single recitation, but rather is itself making that claim about the Kaivalya Upaniṣad — and that the passage concerning Śrī Rudra only extends from "Whoever recites the Śatarudrīya" up to "he becomes free from that [bondage]." For if that were so, the mention of 'Śatarudrīya' in the Kaivalya Upaniṣad would have no connection [to the claim of liberating knowledge], and the sentence would be rendered meaningless. It cannot be claimed that the Śatarudrīya is not mentioned here as a means to liberating knowledge — because at the very beginning of the Kaivalya Upaniṣad, the sentence "Ascetics of purified being who are firmly established in the knowledge of Vedānta, through the yoga of renunciation" already addresses the Vedānta texts as means of liberating knowledge, and since that need is already satisfied there, a mention of Śrī Rudra as that same means would be redundant. Nor can it be argued that Śrī Rudra is mentioned merely as a means of mental purification (citta-śuddhi) required prior to liberating knowledge — for the means of mental purification such as Vedānta and the yoga of renunciation are themselves already mentioned in that opening passage, making a separate mention of Śrī Rudra for that purpose equally redundant. Nor, again, even in terms of a superior means of mental purification compared to sannyāsa and the like —

22                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

— can one suggest that Śrī Rudra is mentioned there to indicate what is the supreme means of mental purification. For if that were accepted, those who hold that Śrī Rudra is not the supreme text — by virtue of its being the supreme means of mental purification surpassing Vedānta and renunciation, and by virtue of its capacity to produce the fruit of upāsanā (worship) in Avimukta (the sacred region of liberation) — would not be able to maintain their position even so. Therefore, the following must be stated:

Among the Upaniṣads designated by the name Vedānta, all of which have already been identified as means of liberating knowledge, the Kaivalya Upaniṣad passage beginning "Whoever recites the Śatarudrīya..." is establishing that Śrī Rudra is the supreme among all of them, in response to the question: "Which is the highest?" In this way, the word 'atyāśramī' (supreme renunciant) in the passage which establishes Śrī Rudra must be understood as proclaiming its supremacy within the section on liberating knowledge (jñāna-kāṇḍa). If it did not serve to establish this supremacy, the word 'atyāśramī' itself would be without meaning — the sentence would become mere groundless assertion (aparyavasthita-vākya) without conveying any intended meaning — and that is unacceptable. Therefore, we rightly conclude that the use of that word necessarily conveys the supremacy of Śrī Rudra within the jñāna-kāṇḍa.

Now, an objection is raised: Even granted all this, it does not follow that Śrī Rudra is supreme among all Upaniṣads, because the Atharvaśiras (Upaniṣad) declares: "Whatever brahmin always studies the Atharvaśiras — for him it is as if a hundred thousand recitations of Rudra have been performed" — thus stating that the fruit of a hundred thousand Rudra recitations can be achieved by a single recitation of the Atharvaśiras, from which it follows that the Atharvaśiras is superior to Śrī Rudra. Nor can one deflect this by saying that the Atharvaśiras statement is mere eulogistic praise (prasaṃsā) without literal force — for then the same would apply to "Let him always or even once recite..." regarding Śrī Rudra. [Response:] That passage occurs within the Atharvaśiras itself;

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             23

[Continuation of the refutation of the objection that Atharvaśiras is superior to Śrī Rudra]

...and so it is self-evident that within the context of the Atharvaśiras, that statement is a eulogy (prasaṃsā) directed at the Atharvaśiras itself, and is thus established as having an alternative explanation (anyathāsiddhi — i.e., it serves another purpose within its own context). The passage praising Śrī Rudra, however, is not found within the Śrī Rudra Upaniṣad itself — as would be required for a similar alternative explanation to apply. Rather, it occurs in the Jābāla and Kaivalya Upaniṣads and other texts of the same scriptural lineage as the Śrī Rudra Upaniṣad. If the passage in those texts were understood as merely a eulogy of Śrī Rudra and not as a literal statement of its own import, then that praise of Rudra — being unconnected to the Kaivalya and Jābāla texts as their own subject — would be without independent validity, lacking the authority to establish its own meaning. And by the principle of the section on Arthavāda (eulogistic statement in the Mīmāṃsā school), it would become entirely meaningless. Therefore, it must be accepted as a literal statement of its own import (svārthaparatva). Further, by the principle of "the praise of one sacred site [vouched for] by another sacred site" (tīrthe tīrthāntarastutiṃ nyāya) and by the principle of "the victory document signed by the opponent himself" (pratipādikara-pita-jayapatrikā-nyāya), the statement in the Jābāla and Kaivalya śrutis praising Śrī Rudra establishes its supreme status beyond all question, and no one can refute it. Enough of elaboration. ॥

This supremacy of Śrī Rudra, established by śruti (revealed scripture), is also confirmed by smṛti (secondary scripture). As Bodhāyana the revered one has shown in his nyāsa (ritual placement of mantras) for the Śrī Rudra mahānyāsa: "The Śivāsaṃkalpa is the heart [mantra], the Puruṣasūkta is the head [mantra], the Uttara-nārāyaṇa is the topknot [mantra], the Apratiratha is the protective mantra" — and so forth — the Śivāsaṃkalpa and other texts, together with the Tryambaka, Pañcabrahma mantras and others, are shown to surpass all sin and yield supreme fruits in accordance with the prescriptions of Manu and others; they are presented as manifestations of Śrī Rudra, thereby demonstrating that Śrī Rudra is supreme among all Vedic texts. Similar examples may be multiplied. Thus, upon careful examination of both śruti and smṛti, the supremacy of Śrī Rudra among all Vedic texts is firmly established.

24                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

The Vamana Smṛti declares: "By studying the Veda, one is purified a hundredfold; by studying the Rudra Ekādaśinī (the eleven anuvākas of Rudra), one is purified right there [i.e., by that alone]." Likewise, the statement in Chandas-śāstras and related texts — "One who recites Rudra while standing in water..." and so forth — where the recitation of the Puruṣasūkta and similar texts is mentioned alongside Śrī Rudra, is intended from the standpoint of the element of removing sin (pāpa-haratva), not as indicating that those texts are of equal primacy. The distinction of degrees (tāratamya) is indeed recognized by those who seek to compare them; such is the intent of the smṛti.

Similarly, the Itihāsas (epics) also proclaim the supreme status of Śrī Rudra among all Vedic texts. In the Anuśāsana Parvan of the Mahābhārata, in response to Yudhiṣṭhira's question — "By the grace of Durvāsas and the great-souled Śaṃkara, a certain knowledge arose in me here. Please explain it to me. I wish to hear everything about the great glory of that great-souled one, and the names of Śarva, O best among speakers" — Śrī Kṛṣṇa's reply begins with "Let me tell you, after paying homage to Kapardin (Śiva)," and the following is heard: "His names, many and meaningful, are explained in various ways in the worlds; they are manifold because of his all-pervasiveness and his manifold functions. And among these, the wise know the Śatarudrīya śruti as the most excellent in the Veda." In the Droṇa Parvan, the same statement of Vyāsa is heard addressed to Arjuna.

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             25

In both passages the phrase "the Śatarudrīya is the most excellent (uttama)" raises the question: most excellent in comparison to what? Since the word "Veda" appears in the preceding clause, the most natural interpretation takes the Veda itself — which is already present in the sentence — as the term of comparison (pratiyogi), without needing to posit an unmentioned or inferred counterpart. Thus the supremacy of Śrī Rudra among all Vedic texts is directly and explicitly proclaimed.

The reason for this supremacy is also stated there — namely, that by mere recitation (japa) of it all four human aims (puruṣārtha) are bestowed. As the Lord himself has declared: "He who, in a state of ritual purity, studies this science in the early morning — for such a one who recites the Śatarudrīya in that manner, nothing whatsoever is unattainable." Thus the Lord expresses his own personal conviction that one who recites Śrī Rudra gains all desired objects (sarvārtha-lābha) from his own experience.

By this, it becomes clear — as recorded in the Kūrma, Liṅga, and Harivaṃśa Purāṇas — that the Lord Vāsudeva himself undertook the Pāśupata vow (paśupata-vrata) and recitation of the Śrī Rudra Pañcākṣarī (the five-syllable mantra of Rudra). The Kūrma Purāṇa records: "Here, aforetime, Hṛṣīkeśa, the inner-self of Viṣṇu, son of Devakī, Kṛṣṇa — always observing the Pāśupata vow — dwelt for a year, his entire body smeared with ashes, intent upon the study of the Rudra-adhyāya (the Rudra chapter), worshipping Hari [Śaṃbhu], having undertaken the Pāśupata vow." The Liṅga [Purāṇa] records: "Having thus praised the Great God [Mahādeva] and prostrated before him, the Lord recited Rudra a crore (ten million) times while standing in water." The Harivaṃśa records: "Mukunda [Viṣṇu], his forehead marked with the sacred ash, adorned with ashes and wearing white, wearing matted locks, clad in bark garments, meditating continuously in his mind on the Moon-crested One [Śiva], recited the five syllables [of the Rudra mantra]." In this way, in the Bhārata,

26                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

[Continuing the account of Kṛṣṇa's Śrī Rudra recitation during the Kailāsa Yātrā (pilgrimage to Mount Kailāsa)]

...the statements in the Mahābhārata regarding Śrī Kṛṣṇa's recitation of Śrī Rudra during the Kailāsa pilgrimage, undertaken to propitiate the Pāśupatākṣa (the eye of Paśupati [Śiva]) dwelling at the Mānasa lake, are also to be cited as evidence.

Though the Lord Vāsudeva, who is the Supreme Brahman and who has obtained all desires, has nothing that need be prayed for or obtained from any source — nonetheless, in accordance with the Gītā's teaching: "Whatever a great person does, others follow; whatever standard he sets, the world abides by it" — his performance of these acts is perfectly intelligible as being for the purpose of loka-saṃgraha (guiding the world). Indeed, the Lord himself has declared in the Mahābhārata: "As I set the example, the world follows me. There is truly nothing that I need to obtain as a boon, O joy of the Pāṇḍavas. Yet having deliberated upon this in my mind, I formerly worshipped the Lord of the universe, who is my own Self, through my own self, for the sake of [teaching about] sons [and other goals]." And in the Kūrma Purāṇa: "They do not perceive that God, being deluded by my māyā (illusory power). Therefore, to instruct the ignorant about my own [true] form, I worship Him." Thus it is firmly established that the Lord Vāsudeva himself, who performed Śrī Rudra recitation for the instruction of the world, is our primary authority regarding the glory of that recitation. In this way, the supremacy of Śrī Rudra is confirmed by the Itihāsas (epics) as well.

Similarly, the Purāṇas also make known that supremacy. Passages from the Brahma-uttara-khaṇḍa, the Kūrma Purāṇa, and others have already been cited above. Many hundreds more can be found. Rather than citing all of them — which would require great elaboration — a few are offered here by way of the "rice in the pot" (sthālī-pulāka) principle [i.e., sampling the whole by tasting a part]:

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             27

[Citing Purāṇic testimony on the supremacy of Śrī Rudra]

From the Sūta Saṃhitā: "One who recites Rudra is freed from the five great sins (mahāpātaka). He attains right knowledge (samyag-jñāna) and through it is freed from bondage. There is no recitation (japa) equal to this in truth, in śruti (Vedic revelation), or in smṛti (secondary scripture)."

Thus, having established the great glory of the Rudra chapter — as known through śruti, smṛti, Itihāsa, and Purāṇa — we now refute what has been contrived by certain modern deluded Tantrikas. They argue as follows: The Śatarudrīya is directed at Agni (the fire-deity) alone, not at the Supreme Lord (Parameśvara). For in the context of the Agnicayana (fire-altar construction ritual), [the text says]: "This [fire] is indeed Rudra — that which is Agni; thus being born, he goes everywhere, all-pervading" — identifying this Rudra with Agni as the deity being manifested at the time of the fire-layer consecration. Then: "Just as a calf, once born, seeks the udder, so does this [fire] now seek its proper share [of oblations]. Therefore, one should offer libations to it, and the sacrificer should meditate upon the Śatarudrīya — it [the fire] is thus pleased (juṣṭa)." From this injunction to offer Śatarudrīya homa (oblation) into that Agni, the conclusion follows that the Śatarudrīya is directed at Agni. They further argue: By the rule stated in Bhaṭṭa's Vārtika: "Whether by secondary attribution (gauṇa), metaphorical usage (lākṣaṇika), or by implication (vyāhāra), the Veda employs other meanings — who can object to that?" — it is possible to explain the text as pointing to a meaning other than the direct one, even without relying on the commentary made by the Lord himself. [They continue:] Just as one cannot arbitrarily determine, through the principle of "pariśeṣa" (elimination of alternatives), that the plural number [in some passage] refers to the triad — when there is already a more authoritative statement about the "Sāmpadya" — similarly in this case. Or, even granting that [Śrī Rudra] is directed at the Supreme Lord — this does not establish his being the Lord of all (sarveśvara) or his being of a purely sāttvika (Vaiṣṇava) nature.

28                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

[The author begins his refutation of the claim that Śrī Rudra is directed at Agni or is merely eulogistic]

They argue: From the salutations, from the indicators of prayers for the attainment of desired things and the avoidance of undesired things, it follows that Śrī Rudra is eulogistic in character, and thus its true meaning (tāttvika) — lordship over all, inner-controller of all (sarvāntaryāmitva), and the like — cannot be established. To this we reply:

Just as there is a śruti passage explaining [Śrī Rudra] as being directed at Agni — "This [fire] is indeed Rudra, that which is Agni" — so too there is a śruti passage explaining it as being directed at the Supreme Lord: "These are the names of the immortal one." The latter passage is corroborated by the Itihāsa and Purāṇa texts cited earlier — "In the Veda the Śatarudrīya is declared to be the most excellent" and "These are my secrets" — and is thus beyond doubt as to its intended meaning; it cannot be an occasion for suspecting that it refers to something other than the Supreme Lord.

The maxim "Strengthen the Veda through the Itihāsa and Purāṇa" establishes that śruti, smṛti, Itihāsa, and Purāṇa, in the manner of the "pride of lions" (vana-siṃha-nyāya), mutually imply and confirm one another, and thus when they converge on a meaning, that meaning becomes unshakeable. Now, since even when [the text is interpreted as] pointing to Agni, and also [when interpreted as] pointing to Brahman, the explanatory commentary produced by the Lord himself is equally applicable — how is one to decide between them in a case of doubt? The resolution must be made through the method of reasoned interpretation. As stated in the Bhaṭṭa-vārtika: "Where Dharma is being established through the Veda as an instrumental authority, the Mīmāṃsā [school of exegesis] will supply the missing portion of what is to be done." Accordingly, by the methods of śruti (direct statement), liṅga (indicative mark), and other principles of interpretation, the meaning of Śrī Rudra is established as being directed at Brahman (brahmaparatva). For example, just as the Kaṭha Upaniṣad śruti — "A being the size of a thumb dwells in the middle of the body; He is the ruler of the past and future" — is established as referring to the Supreme Lord and not to the individual soul (jīva), on the strength of the word 'Īśāna' (the ruling Lord), as adjudicated in the adhikaraṇa "śabda-deva prabhṛtaḥ" — so too, in the case of Śrī Rudra,

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             29

...the position is adopted that the Śrī Rudra Upaniṣad is directed at Brahman, on the strength of hundreds of śruti passages containing the names Śiva, Śaṃkara, Bhava, Śarva, Paśupati, and Rudra.

And just as in the adhikaraṇas (interpretive sections) — "ākāśas talliṅgāt" (Brahman, because of the indicative mark 'space'), "antas tad-dharmopadēśāt" (the inner [being is Brahman] because its qualities are described), "antar-yoni-ādhidaivādiṣu tad-dharma-vyapadēśāt" (in the passages about 'inner womb' and the like, it is [Brahman] because its attributes are ascribed), "ahṛdayatvādigua-ko dharmebhiḥ" (that which is without heart [and other limitations] is Brahman, through [its] attributes), and so on — the śruti passages such as "What is the final destination of this world? He said, 'Space.' Indeed all these beings are born from space alone..." and "That golden person who is seen within the sun..." are established as referring to Brahman, on the strength of the indicators of being the cause of all beings (sarva-bhūta-kāraṇatva) and of being the self of all (sarvātmakatva) — so too here, on the strength of the indicative marks of lordship over all (sarveśvaratva), being the self of all (sarvātmakatva), being the inner controller of all (sarvāntaryāmitva), and the like, the reference to Brahman must be adopted.

Indeed, in the Śrī Rudra Upaniṣad one hears — beginning with "Salutation to the Lord of the directions (diśām ca pataye namaḥ)" and proceeding through individual declarations of lordship over each part of the cosmos, culminating in "Salutation to the Lord of the worlds (jagatāṃ pataye namaḥ)" — the lordship over all worlds (sarva-jagad-īśvaratva); further, beginning with "You who carry arrows and bows..." declarations of being the self of each particular entity are built up, yielding the quality of being the self of all (sarvātmakatva); and similarly "Salutation to you who dwell in all" and the like establish the quality of inner-controllership of all (sarvāntaryāmitva) — all of these being qualities unique to Brahman (brahmasādhāraṇa-dharma). Further, the blue-throated form, the matted-hair, the wielder of Pināka bow, and the like are unique marks of the Supreme Lord (pāramēśvara-sādhāraṇa). Other such indicative marks and sentences are also to be observed. None of these [qualities] can belong to Agni. For one reads in the Talavakāra Upaniṣad:

30                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

In the Talavakāra Upaniṣad (Kena Upaniṣad), it is narrated: In a war between the gods and the demons of old, victory had come through the gods' own power, and they were filled with pride. To humble their arrogance, Śiva appeared before them in the form of a Yakṣa (spirit-being), and when Agni — who had come near, summoned by the desire to know the nature of that form — was set the challenge of burning even a single blade of grass placed before him, Agni could not burn even that much; similarly, Vāyu could not carry away even a single blade of grass. Then, when the gods asked "Who is this divine Yakṣa-being?", the blessed goddess Haimavatī Umā — the presiding deity of higher knowledge (brahma-vidyā), who came forward before the bewildered gods to reveal the nature of that being — taught them that the one who had appeared in the form of a Yakṣa was none other than the Supreme Lord himself (Parameśvara). And the gods recognized that the glory of that victory belonged entirely to the Supreme Lord — so runs the narrative, which is also elaborated in the Purāṇas.

Further, the passages — "The wind blows in fear of Him; the sun and fire also..." (Taittirīya), "By the fear of Him, the wind blows, by fear of Him the sun rises, by fear of Him Agni and Indra..." (Kaṭha), and "Rudra in fire, Rudra in the waters, Rudra in the plants, Rudra who has pervaded all the worlds" — are to be understood in connection with this narrative. Thus, since it is the Supreme Lord who is being praised, whose dependent (tādriya), whose power is subject to His (tad-āyatta-śakti) — since Agni cannot possess the qualities of lordship over all (sarveśvaratva), being the self of all (sarvātmakatva), and the like — the [Yakṣa narrative demonstrates that even] Agni, Indra, and all the worlds are [dependent upon and] eulogize the Supreme Lord alone. This quality of being praised by all the worlds is uniquely the indicator of the Supreme Lord, not of Agni.

Thus, by the methods of śruti and indicative marks (liṅga), supported by the Itihāsa, Purāṇa, the [commentary of] Bodhāyana, dharmaśāstra, and similar texts — by [the principle that texts] supported by both [types of evidence] are unshakeable in their own established meaning — and by the analogy of [how texts about] Āditya and others

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             31

[Continuing the discussion of the interpretation of ambiguous Vedic passages]

...in the [various] seasonal cycles, in [the sections on] the anuṣṭhāna (performance) of saṃvatsara (yearly) and samīpa (adjacent) years, in what are called the extraordinary days of the Gavam Ayana (the year-long soma sacrifice) — just as in those contexts, when there is a dispute about interpretation, the Upaniṣad's own commentary takes precedence over the karma-kāṇḍa (ritual section) commentary, as [the principle states:] "the closer/inner authority wins" — so here too, the Upaniṣad's interpretation (given by the Jābāla) prevails. And since the injunction of reciting in an undertone (upāṃśu-svara, whispering voice), which is drawn from the more specifically applicable text [of the Agnicayana context], yields precedence — just as the rule about a different tone at the Prāyaṇīya [ceremony] is taken as having the force of eulogistic praise (arthavāda) rather than as an independent injunction — and since [the Jābāla interpretation] is more specifically applicable (niravakāśa, without competing alternative application), the stronger [interpretive authority], namely the Jābāla Upaniṣad's declaration "These are the names of the Immortal One" — establishes that this Śatarudrīya is indeed directed at the Supreme Lord.

As for the interpretation [of Śrī Rudra as referring to Agni] that is given in the Agnicayana context, where it stands as the interpretation relevant to the fire-altar construction ceremony — that interpretation is to be understood as having a limited purpose: to indicate that Agni, whose identity with the Rudra described in the [Śatarudrīya] mantra is established, is praised [through the Śatarudrīya] as a means of the mantra's application (viniyoga) within that context. Its scope is only that much — it does not establish that the Śatarudrīya is inherently directed at Agni in accordance with the śruti indicators (liṅga) and other hermeneutic principles. For a mantra that is being applied to a particular purpose (viniyoga) — even if its meaning is illuminated through secondary (gauṇa), figurative (lākṣaṇika), or supplementary (abhyāhāra) modes of interpretation — does not on that account have its primary referential meaning (svārthaparatva) replaced by that derivative meaning. This very point is made in the Mīmāṃsā commentary (Vārtika) passage: "Whether by secondary attribution (gauṇa) or by figurative usage (lākṣaṇika)..." Indeed, for this very reason, even though the Aindrā [Ṛc verse] is applied in the Gārhapatya worship, the Aindrā Ṛc is not thereby understood as referring to Gārhapatya [fire]. And for the same reason, even though the Puruṣasūkta is applied in the Puruṣa-medha (human sacrifice) for the praise of the sacrificial persons — as stated in the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa: "...having stood apart from Dakṣiṇā, Brahmā praises [with the hymn] 'O thousand-headed Puruṣa'" and in the Āpastamba Sūtra: "Having performed [the rite], standing apart toward the south, the Brahmā [priest] praises with the Puruṣa Nārāyaṇa [verse]..." — and even though it is applied for the installation of a human-shaped [fire-altar structure] in the Mahāgni rite, as stated in the Āpastamba Sūtra — and even though it is applied in the worship of Varuṇa, Dhanada (Kubera), and others in the Śātatapa Smṛti and elsewhere — it is not understood as referring to those deities. This is a topic that most scholars address. Therefore, though the blessed Śrī Rudra Upaniṣad is sought by many [to be directed toward Agni or other purposes] and is pulled in various directions through many arguments — like a faithful wife who is faithful in heart even when being dragged away by many deceivers — the heart of the blessed Śrī Rudra Upaniṣad stands firm in its reference to the Brahman who is Śiva and Parameśvara (śāmba-parabrahman). Those who seek to redirect it elsewhere —

32                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

— such as Rāmānuja and others, who are like the demon Rāvaṇa who violated the blessed and venerable Sītā — are supreme sinners. Let that stand for now.

As for the argument that, since Śrī Rudra is characterized by salutations and by prayers for attaining desired things and avoiding undesired things, its true and ultimate meaning — lordship over all (sarveśvaratva), inner-controllership of all (sarvāntaryāmitva), and the like — cannot be established; to this we say: Salutation (namaskāra) is motivated by the excellence of the one being saluted — this is how it is observed in ordinary life as well. Scholars of semantics (śābdakāra) also define namaskāra as an act of reverential gesture preceded by the devotee's contemplation of the greatness of the being to whom reverence is being paid. In inquiring into the excellence of the one being saluted, since it would be improper to reject the excellence offered by the specific epithets in the mantra (śruti-viśeṣaṇa) and adopt some other kind of excellence — the true and ultimate excellence of lordship over all, inner-controllership of all, and so on — as conveyed by the specific epithets 'sat', 'cit', and others in the salutation mantras — is firmly established. What grounds are there for suspecting that it is not ultimate (tāttvika)?

Although salutation out of fear is also observed in the world, and in the śruti itself — "Salutation to your fury (harase), salutation to your light; let your weapons fly elsewhere; do not be harmful to us, be auspicious (śiva) to us" — and one might therefore suggest the alternative explanation (anyathāsiddhi) that this salutation to the Supreme Lord is motivated by his fearfulness (bhīmatva) — still, even a salutation motivated by fear cannot be possible without the one being saluted possessing some excellence. For this very reason, the statement in the Brahman's assembly — "They say, 'Salutation to your radiance (jyotiṣe)'; indeed by making salutation they serve the one who is superior" — explains that namaskāra essentially never abandons the quality of excellence (utkarṣa) belonging to its natural subject (svaviṣaya). In truth, even if it is motivated by fear, it does not diminish the namaskāra [of Śrī Rudra], because the great preponderance of salutations in Śrī Rudra that are peculiar to it (anivara-sādhāraṇa) is not something that would be produced by mere fear.

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             33

The great preponderance of fear (bhaya-bhūyastva) is indeed itself an indicator of Parabrahman (Supreme Brahman), as the śruti declares: "By the fear of Him the wind blows..." and "Great indeed is the thunderbolt; it is spoken of as dreadful" and the like. Therefore, salutation is not an indicator of mere eulogy (stuti). As for the prayers for attaining desired things and avoiding undesired things — although these too can be indicators of eulogy, they apply in the case of someone other than the Supreme Lord, not to the Supreme Lord himself. If that were otherwise — and if the Puruṣasūkta is taken as forming a single sentence together with the Uttara-nārāyaṇa [section] — then in the Uttara-nārāyaṇa's statements such as "destroy this, destroy the vital breath, destroy all" — which illustrate the pattern of prayer — the Puruṣasūkta itself would also become a text of mere eulogy (stuti-mātra), and then the content it conveys — namely, Amṛteśānatva (lordship over immortality), Virāḍ-svarūpa (the cosmic form), and the like — would also be without ultimate import. What would your response be to such an objection? Therefore, this whole debate has become unnecessarily prolonged.

Thus, the supremacy of Śrī Rudra among all Vedic texts — well-established through śruti, smṛti, Itihāsa, and Purāṇa — and its intended meaning (tātparya) have been elaborated at length. Now, the Śrī Rudra Mahānyāsa as described by Bodhāyana — for the benefit of those who perform japa (recitation) and other rites — is explained according to the [oral] tradition in terms of its meaning: "Now we shall explain the procedure of japa (recitation), homa (fire oblation), arcana (worship), and abhiṣeka (ritual bathing) preceded by the Mahānyāsa of the Pañcāṅga-Rudras." Here, the meaning of 'Pañcāṅga-Rudra': those [practices] that have five kinds of aṅga-nyāsa (placement of mantras on bodily parts) are called Pañcāṅga-Rudras. They are as follows:

34                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

[Description of the Five-Part Nyāsa (Pañcāṅga-nyāsa)]

The first is the thirty-one-member aṅga-nyāsa (limb-placement) extending from the crown tuft (śikhā) to the eye (akṣa); the second is the twenty-eight-member aṅga-nyāsa extending from the crown of the head (mūrdha) to the feet (pāda); the third is the five-member aṅga-nyāsa from the feet upward to the crown of the head; the fourth is the five-member aṅga-nyāsa from the secret region (guhya) up to the top of the head (mastaka); the fifth is the five-member aṅga-nyāsa from the heart (hṛdaya) to the eye (akṣa).

The first nyāsa (placement) is described as follows: "'Yā te rudra' at the crown tuft (śikhā); 'asmin mahati arṇave' at the head (śiras); 'sahasrāṇi' at the forehead (lalāṭa); 'haṃsaḥ śuciṣat' between the eyebrows (bhruvos madhye); 'tryambakaṃ yajāmahe' at the eyes (netrayoḥ); 'namaḥ sutyāya ca' at the ears (karṇayoḥ); 'mānas toka' at the nose (nāsikāyām); 'avatanye' at the mouth (mukhe); [the two] 'nīlagrīva' at the throat (kaṇṭhe); 'namas te astvāyudhāya' at the upper arms (bāhvoḥ); 'yā te hetir' at the forearms (upabāhvoḥ); 'ye tīrthāni' at the hands (hastayoḥ); the five anuvākas beginning with 'sadyojātim' at the five fingers (pañcasvāṅguliṣu); 'namo vaḥ kirikebhyaḥ' at the chest (hṛdye); 'namo gaṇebhyaḥ' at the back (pṛṣṭhe); 'namo hiraṇyabāhave' at the sides (pārśvayoḥ); 'bindayaṃ dhanuḥ' at the belly (jaṭhare); 'hiraṇyagarbhe' at the navel (nābhau); 'mīḍhuṣṭama' at the hip (kaṭyām); 'ye bhūtānām' at the secret region (guhye); 'ye agniṣvat' at the testicles (aṇḍayoḥ); 'saśirā jātavedā' at the perineum (apāne); 'mā no mahānti' at the thighs (ūrvoḥ); 'eṣa te rudra' at the knees (jānvoḥ); 'saṃsṛṣṭajidam' at the calves (jaṅghayoḥ); 'viśvaṃ bhūtam' at the ankles (gulphayoḥ); 'ye pathām' at the feet (pādayoḥ); 'adhyavocat' as the armor mantra (kavacam); 'namo bilmine' as the sub-armor mantra (upakavacam); 'namo astu nīlagrīvāya' as the third-eye mantra (tṛtīyanetram); 'praśuśca dhanvana' as the weapon mantra (astram); and 'ya etāvantaśca' as the directional binding (digbandhaḥ).'"

Its meaning: After reciting the mantra "Yā te rudra śivā tanūḥ" (O Rudra, that auspicious form of yours...), one should say "namaḥ" [to the śikhā] and touch the śikhā and the [other listed] limbs in order. Regarding "nīlagrīvau dvau kaṇṭhe" — two nīlagrīva mantras are to be placed successively on the northern and southern parts of the throat. Regarding "yā te hetiḥ iti upabāhvoḥ" — the word 'upabāhu' refers to the region above the wrist. Regarding "sadyojātim iti pañcānuvākān pañcasvāṅguliṣu" — after reciting with the mantra for sadyojāta and saying 'namaḥ', one places [the mantra] on the two thumbs. In the same manner, one places Vāmadeva on the index fingers, Aghora on the middle fingers, Tatpuruṣa on the ring fingers, and Īśāna on the little fingers — that is the meaning.

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             35

[Continuing the description of the first nyāsa, explaining specific mantras]

Regarding "saśirā jātavedā" — one tradition holds that this refers to the verse-mantra: "saśirā jātavedā akṣaraṃ padaṃ vedānāṃ sāramuddhṛte jātavedase śirase svāhā" (the imperishable word, the essence of the Vedas, is extracted for Jātavedas, the head — svāhā). Another tradition holds that the six mantras "jātavedase," "soma-marātīyataḥ," "agne tvaṃ pāraya," "agne naya supathā rāye," "viśvāni no durgahā," and "pūrtanājitaṃ saha" are referred to by "saśirā jātavedāḥ." The practice is to be determined by one's own tradition (ācāra).

The mantra 'adhyavocat' is to be placed [on the body] with the utterance 'hum' for the armor (kavacāya hum). The mantra 'namo bilmine' is to be placed with 'hum' for the sub-armor (upakavacāya hum). The mantra 'namo astu nīlagrīvāya' is to be placed with 'namaḥ' for the third eye (tṛtīyanetram). The mantra 'pramukhca dhanvanaḥ' is to be placed with 'phaṭ' for the weapon (astram).

This completes the first nyāsa, which extends from the crown-tuft (śikhā) to the eye (akṣa) together with the directional binding (digbandha).

Now the second nyāsa is described: One is to place the salutation 'Oṃ namo bhagavate rudrāya' [as follows]:
"Place the syllable Oṃ on the crown of the head (mūrdhni);
The syllable 'na' then at the nose (nāsikā);
The syllable 'mo' at the forehead (lalāṭa); and 'bha' at the center of the face (mukha-madhya);
The syllable 'ga' at the throat region (kaṇṭha); place 'va' at the heart (hṛdaya);
Place 'te' at the right hand (dakṣiṇa-hasta); place 'ru' on the left side (vāma);
Place 'drā' at the navel region (nābhi); place 'ya' on the two feet (pādayoḥ)."

Its meaning: After the first nyāsa with the directional binding described above, one recites the mantra 'Oṃ namo bhagavate rudrāya' and pays salutation. Then one places: 'Oṃ — namaḥ to the crown of the head (mūrdhne namaḥ)' at the crown; 'naṃ — namaḥ to the nose (nāsikāyai namaḥ)' at the nose; 'moṃ — namaḥ to the forehead (lalāṭāya namaḥ)' at the forehead; 'bhaṃ — namaḥ to the face (mukhāya namaḥ)' at the face; 'gaṃ — namaḥ to the throat (kaṇṭhāya namaḥ)' at the throat; 'vaṃ — namaḥ to the heart (hṛdayāya namaḥ)' at the heart; 'teṃ — namaḥ to the right hand (dakṣiṇahastāya namaḥ)' at the right hand; 'ruṃ — namaḥ to the left hand (vāmahastāya namaḥ)' at the left hand; 'drāṃ — namaḥ to the navel (nābhyai namaḥ)' at the navel; and 'yaṃ — namaḥ to the two feet (pādābhyāṃ namaḥ)' at the feet. This is the second nyāsa.

Now the third nyāsa is described: "Place Sadyojāta (sadyam) at the two feet;
Place Vāmadeva (vāmam) at the middle of the belly (udara-madhya);
Place Aghora (aghora) at the heart (hṛdi);
Place Tatpuruṣa (tatpuruṣam) at the face (mukhe);
Place Īśāna (īśānam) at the crown (mūrdhni) — [this is] 'Haṃsaḥ' known as Sadāśiva.
Having performed this nyāsa procedure, one then worships [the deity] with palms joined (sapuṭam ārcayet)."
Then [follow the mantras:] 'trātābhimantrya', 'tvaṃ no agne', 'sugaṃ naḥ panthām', 'asunvantam', and 'tattvā-[dhāvana]'...

36                        Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

[Explanation of the first nyāsa, continued]

Its meaning: After reciting the mantra "Yā te rudra śivā tanūḥ" (O Rudra, that auspicious form of yours...), one should say "namaḥ to the śikhā (crown tuft)" and touch the śikhā and succeeding [limbs]. Regarding "nīlagrīvau dvau kaṇṭhe" (the two nīlagrīva [mantras] at the throat) — the two nīlagrīva mantras are to be placed successively on the northern and southern parts of the throat. Regarding "yā te hetiḥ iti upabāhvoḥ" (the mantra 'yā te hetiḥ' at the forearms) — the word 'upabāhu' denotes the region above the wrist. Regarding "sadyojātim iti pañcānuvākān pañcasvāṅguliṣu" (the five anuvākas beginning with sadyojāta at the five fingers) — one recites with the words "namaḥ" along with the Sadyojāta mantra, and places [the mantra] on the two thumbs. In the same manner, one places Vāmadeva on the index fingers (tarjanī), Aghora on the middle fingers, Tatpuruṣa on the ring fingers (anāmikā), and Īśāna on the little fingers (kaniṣṭhikā) — that is the meaning.

Regarding "saśirā jātavedā" — one tradition (sampradāya) holds that the verse-mantra "saśirā jātavedā akṣaraṃ padaṃ vedānāṃ sāramuddhṛte jātavedase śirase svāhā" (the imperishable word, the essence of the Vedas, is offered to Jātavedas, the head — svāhā) is the mantra referred to by this phrase. A second tradition holds that the six mantras — "jātavedase," "soma-marātīyataḥ," "agne tvaṃ pāraya," "agne naya supathā rāye," "viśvāni no durgahā," and "pūrtanājitaṃ saha" — are what is meant by "saśirā jātavedāḥ." The practice is to be determined according to one's own tradition (ācāra).

The mantra 'adhyavocat' is to be placed with the utterance 'hum' for the armor (kavacāya hum). The mantra 'namo bilmine' is to be placed with 'hum' for the sub-armor (upakavacāya hum). The mantra 'namo astu nīlagrīvāya' is to be placed with 'namaḥ' for the third eye (tṛtīyanetram). The mantra 'pramukhca dhanvanaḥ' is to be placed with 'phaṭ' for the weapon (astram).

This completes the first nyāsa (ritual placement), which extends from the crown-tuft (śikhā) to the eye (akṣa), together with the directional binding (digbandha).

                        Upoddhāta (Introduction)                             37

Now the second nyāsa is described: One is to place the salutation 'Oṃ namo bhagavate rudrāya' as follows:

"Place the Oṃkāra syllable on the crown of the head (mūrdhni);
Then the syllable 'na' at the nose (nāsikā);
The syllable 'mo' at the forehead (lalāṭa); and 'bha' at the center of the face (mukha-madhya);
The syllable 'ga' at the throat region (kaṇṭha-deśa); place 'va' at the heart (hṛdi);
Place 'te' at the right hand (dakṣiṇa-hasta); place 'ru' on the left side (vāmataḥ);
Place 'drā' at the navel region (nābhi-deśa); place 'ya' on the two feet (pādayoḥ)."

Its meaning: After the first nyāsa with the directional binding described above, one recites the mantra 'Oṃ namo bhagavate rudrāya' and makes obeisance. Then one places:
'Oṃ — namaḥ to the crown of the head (mūrdhne namaḥ)' at the crown;
'naṃ — namaḥ to the nose (nāsikāyai namaḥ)' at the nose;
'moṃ — namaḥ to the forehead (lalāṭāya namaḥ)' at the forehead;
'bhaṃ — namaḥ to the face (mukhāya namaḥ)' at the face;
'gaṃ — namaḥ to the throat (kaṇṭhāya namaḥ)' at the throat;
'vaṃ — namaḥ to the heart (hṛdayāya namaḥ)' at the heart;
'teṃ — namaḥ to the right hand (dakṣiṇahastāya namaḥ)' at the right hand;
'ruṃ — namaḥ to the left hand (vāmahastāya namaḥ)' at the left hand;
'drāṃ — namaḥ to the navel (nābhyai namaḥ)' at the navel;
'yaṃ — namaḥ to the two feet (pādābhyāṃ namaḥ)' at the feet.

This is the second nyāsa.

Now the third nyāsa is described:

"Place Sadyojāta (sadyam) at the two feet (pādayoḥ);
Place Vāmadeva (vāmam) at the middle of the belly (udara-madhya);
Place Aghora (aghora) at the heart (hṛdi);
Place Tatpuruṣa (tatpuruṣam) at the face (mukhe);
Place Īśāna (īśānam) at the crown of the head (mūrdhni) — [this is] 'Haṃsaḥ', named Sadāśiva.
Having performed this nyāsa procedure, one then worships [the deity] with palms joined (sapuṭam ārcayet)."

[The following mantras are then to be used:] 'trātābhimantrya', 'tvaṃ no agne', 'sugaṃ naḥ panthām', 'asunvantam', and 'tattvā-[dhāvana]'...

[Note: The page ends mid-sentence, continuing onto the following page.]

38                    SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM

"yami," "ano niyuji," "vayam soma," "tam ishanam," "ase rudra,"
"syona prthivi" — having placed these as a sandwiching enclosure (samputa)
in the directions beginning with Indra's direction, and having thus performed
the raudrikarana (the rite of identifying oneself with Rudra) within oneself,
one is freed from all sins residing in the skin and bones, and becomes
unvanquished among all beings. Thereby, sorcerers, pretas (spirits of the
departed), pishacas (flesh-eating spirits), brahma-rakshasas (brahmin demons),
bhakshaya (devouring spirits), evil messengers, shakinis, dakinis, serpents,
dogs, quadrupeds, thieves, sudden adversities, and sudden attacks — all of
these see him as one blazing in a great conflagration.

The meaning of this is as follows: One should touch the feet [of the image]
while reciting the sadyojata mantra as the opening formula (mukharya), with
"panca-anga (five-limbed) namah" as the concluding formula. Similarly, one
should perform nyasa (ritual placement of mantras on the body) from the feet
upward to the crown of the head, using the formulas beginning with Vamadeva.
Thereafter, having said "hamsa hamsa," one should touch the crown of the
head. "Hamsa" is the well-known name of Sadashiva himself; and one who does
this indeed becomes Sadashiva, who is known by the name Hamsa. The action
to be performed thereafter is stated: "Then one should begin the samputa
(enclosure gesture)." Having formed the anjali mudra (gesture of cupped
hands) indicated by the word samputa, one should first direct it eastward and
recite the opening portion of the mantra "trataram indram," saying "namah to
Indra." Then turning toward the southeast, one should recite the opening
portion of the mantra "tvam no agne," saying "namah to Agni." In this manner
one should continue through the directions from the south to the northeast.
Thereafter one should perform raudrikarana (identification with Rudra). That
is of two kinds: the first is performed by appending the formula "raudrena
anikena pahi ma agne pitrhi ma ma ma himshi" to all the mantras from
"vishurasi pravahana" through "adhirasi khudribhyah"; the second is the one
performed beginning with "prajananam brahma tishthat" —

Upodghata (Preface/Introduction).                           39

— "may [the deity] stand on all sides, surrounded by a garland of flames; may
they protect me" — this is the raudrikarana (identification with Rudra)
consisting in the ritual installation of deities in one's body, in the manner
to be described below. It is to be practiced according to the tradition of one's
disciplic lineage (sampradaya). The results of all this are stated by the
formula: "one is freed from all sins residing in the skin and bones," etc.
This is the third nyasa (ritual placement). Now the fourth nyasa is stated:
"mano jyotir abodhy agnim murdho murdhanam marmani te jataveda — with this,
one should consecrate the genitals, navel, heart, throat, face, and head, and
then perform self-protection."

The meaning of this is: Using the mantra beginning with "mano jyotih" as
the opening formula, having said "namah to the generative organ," one should
touch the genitals. Similarly, using the formulas beginning with "abodhy agnih,"
having said "namah to the navel," one should touch the navel, heart, throat,
face, and head in succession. This very act of consecrating [the body parts]
beginning with the genitals is designated "atma-raksha" (self-protection).
This is the fourth nyasa.

Now the fifth nyasa is stated: "Shiva-sankalpa (the hymn of auspicious
resolve) for the heart; Purusha-sukta (the Vedic hymn on the Cosmic Person)
for the head; Uttara-narayana for the topknot (shikha); Apratiratha for the
armor (kavacha); Pratipurusha for the eyes; Shataraudriya (the Shri Rudra
hymn) for the weapon (astra); and bhur bhuvah suvah om for the directional
binding (digbandha)."

The meaning of this is: Having recited the Shiva-sankalpa-sukta consisting of
twenty-six verses beginning with "yenedam bhutam bhuvanam bhavishyat," one
should place it at the heart with the gesture "namah to the heart." Having
recited the Purusha-sukta, one should place it at the head with "svaha to the
head." The anuvaka (section) "pratipurusham ekakapa lam nirvapati" — and the
brahmana section "jata eva praja rudro agnir avadyate" —

40                    SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM

— both anuvaka (sections) [constitute] "pratipurusa." Having recited them,
one should touch the three eyes [of the image] with the gesture "namah to the
three eyes, vaushat." [Then:] The anuvaka "svam agne rudra" and likewise the
anuvaka "deva deveshu shrayadhvam" [constitute] the Shataraudriya. Although
in the anuvaka "svam agne rudra" there is strictly speaking no ground for
applying the term Shataraudriya as previously defined, nonetheless the term
is assigned to it on account of: the universality of the deity ("vishve devash
cha netuh"), the mantra "yujam vrinishvasya" being identified as "pausnayetena"
on account of its Pushna-character; the mantra "shadrchas tvame" having a
connection with Rudra through a reduced nominal stem alone (laghu-pratipadika-
yoga), which yields the character of a lunar deity (lu-daiva-sattva); the mantra
"mano devi" etc. bearing an element of the nominal stem of a Shanid deity,
and therefore bearing the character of a Shanid deity; and also for Agni-deity
passages — the view that "svam agne rudra" is a Shataraudriya text is held
simply by virtue of the occurrence of the word "rudra." In truth, however, the
designation "Shataraudriya" for the anuvaka "svam agne rudra" is because Agni,
who is its subject, is praised there in the form of the Shataraudriya.

The designation of the anuvaka "deva deveshu" as Shataraudriya is to be
understood from the fact that the statement "deva agnir ekadashabhir shadbhih
iti" treats Agni as the Shataraudriya subject, and some of the meanings of
individual verses are traceable [to Rudra themes]. Having recited both those
anuvaka-s, one should perform the nyasa at the weapon with "namah to the
weapon, phat." This is the fifth nyasa.

Now the action to be performed after the five-limbed nyasa is stated: "One
should whisper (sankusha) the five-limbed [sequence] and then perform the
eight-limbed prostration." One should whisper the five-limbed [mantras]
beginning with Sadyojata; then one should perform the ashtanga (eight-limbed)
prostration. Those eight mantras are stated: "Hiranyagarbhah," "yah pranatah,"
"brahmajajnanam," "mahi dyauh," "vapash casac," "agne

Upodghata (Preface/Introduction).                           41

"naya," "ya te agne," "imam yam prastaram" — these eight constitute the
eight-limbed prostration.

Regarding these mantras, one view holds that the prostration is indicated
simply by reciting them; another view holds that the prostration with all eight
bodily parts (sashtanga, like a staff falling flat) is to be performed while
reciting these mantras.

The action to be performed thereafter is stated: "Then one should meditate
upon oneself as the beautiful form of Shri Rudra —

[Dhyana-shloka, verse for meditation on Rudra's form:]

Pure as crystal, three-eyed, with five faces,
Bearing the Ganga, with ten arms, adorned with serpents as ornaments;
With a blue throat, crescent-mooned, wearing a sacred thread of serpents,
Draped with a tiger-skin upper garment, bestowing blessings and the gift of fearlessness;
Accompanied by a water-pot and a cluster of flowers, holding a trident;
Blazing with tawny matted locks, causing the topknot to emit radiance;
Purified by nectar, joyful, sharing the body with Uma;
Seated on a divine throne, endowed with divine enjoyments;
Attended by the deities of the directions, saluted by gods and demons;
Eternal, everlasting, pure, immovable, imperishable, immutable;
All-pervading, the lord Isha (ruler), Rudra, of universal form —

Having thus meditated, one who has undertaken the vow should properly
commence worship."

Now the commentary on the procedure of abhisheka (ritual bathing) is given:
"Now we shall explain the procedure of the bathing and consecration (abhisheka)
of Rudra. First, having bathed at a sacred ford, having put on freshly-washed
clothes brought in the approved manner, pure and restrained, observing
brahmacharya (celibacy), and dressed in white — having made an image of
Ishana, one should stand to the south of it, facing it, and establish the deities
within oneself: 'May Brahma stand in the generative organ. May Vishnu stand
in the feet. May Shiva stand in the heart. May the Earth stand in the belly.

42                    SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM

May Agni stand in the stomach. May the Vasus stand in the throat. May
Sarasvati stand in the mouth. May Vayu stand in the nostrils. May the Moon
and Sun stand in the eyes. May the Ashvins stand in the ears. May the Rudras
stand in the forehead. May Pinaki (the bearer of the bow Pinaka, i.e., Shiva)
stand at the back. May Shuli (the trident-bearer) stand in front. May Shiva
and Shankara stand at the two sides. May Vayu stand on all sides. Thereafter
may the deity stand on all sides, surrounded by a garland of flames. May all
deities stand in all the remaining [parts]. May they protect me.'

Having performed the nyasa (ritual placement) according to the scriptural
injunction as in "agnih me vaci shritah" (Agni is established in my speech),
one should worship oneself with fragrance, flowers, incense, a lamp, and
food-offerings made in silence.

Then one should prepare [the Lord]: 'You have been worshipped by humans,
by siddhas (perfected beings), by celestial nymphs and others. With devotion
I worship You; accept me, O Maheshvara.' And also [with the mantra]
"tryambakam." After this internal worship, the procedure of external worship
is described:

'May the benevolent Hari (Vishnu) come quickly — his banner gleaming,
with swift white horses, victorious over Vata (the wind) and others, mighty,
with the speed of the mind — O Sharva (Rudra), come swiftly for my oblation
— om.' With this, one should invoke [the deity]. For an established [image],
there is no invocation.

Then the seat [is offered] with "sadyojatam" [mantra]. Foot-water (padya)
[is offered] with "bhave bhava." Arghya (respectful offering of water) [is
offered] with "bhavodbhavaya namah." Achamaniya (water for sipping) [is
offered] with "vamadevaya namah." Then one bathes [the image] with three
verses beginning with "apo hi shtha," with four [verses] beginning with
"hiranyavarnah," after purifying [the water] with the anuvaka "pavamanas
suvarjana," and also with "sarvo vai rudrah," "kaya nash chitrah," and
"apo va idam sarvam."

Upodghata (Preface/Introduction).                           43

[Then various upachara (service) offerings are made:]

Cloth (vastra) [is offered] with "vamadevaya namah." Sacred thread
(upavita) [is offered] with "jyeshthaya namah." Achamaniya (water for
sipping) [is offered] with "rudraya namah." Ornaments (bhushana) [are
offered] with the Pranava (Om). Fragrance (gandha) [is offered] with "kalaya
namah." Aksata (unbroken rice) [is offered] with "kalavikaranaya namah."
Flowers (pushpa) [are offered] with "balavikaranaya namah." Incense (dhupa)
[is offered] with "balapramathanaya namah." A lamp (dipa) [is offered] with
"sarvabhutadamanaya namah." Food-offering (naivedya) [is offered] with
"manonmanaya namah."

Then one offers eight flowers [to the deity] with eight mantras: "bhavaya
devaya namah" and the others [of the ashta-murti, the eightfold form]. Then
one should recite the Rudra-tanu-mantra (mantra for the body of Rudra)
"aghorebhyo tha ghorebhyah" before [the image]. Then one should recite the
Rudra-gayatri "tat purushaya vidmahe" a hundred times, or an unlimited
number of times, or at least ten times.

Then one should pronounce the auspicious benediction (ashish) over [the
Lord]: "Ishanah sarvavidyanam" (the Ruler of all knowledge). Thereafter,
while continuously pouring a stream of water from a pot over [the Lord's]
crown, one should recite the eleven anuvaka-s [of the Shri Rudra] beginning
with "namaste rudra manyave." One should also recite the twelve other Raudra
mantras such as "sarvo vai rudrah" and "ima rudraya." After the recitation,
one should recite one anuvaka at a time from the eleven anuvaka-s beginning
with "agnavishnuh sajoshasema."

One who seeks the removal of sins, the cure of illness, prosperity, peace,
long life, or good health should perform this [rite]; by performing it thus, one
attains all these [results]. One should offer a great oblation of payasam (rice
cooked in milk) and other such offerings. Then one should give a dakshina
(sacrificial fee) — eleven cows, or at minimum one cow if eleven are not
available, as stated by the revered Bodhayana. Thus the completeness of
Shri Rudra —

44                    SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM

— [the supremacy of Shri Rudra] in being the remover of all sins, in being
the bestower of the results of all modes of worship, and in bestowing through
recitation (japa) the knowledge that is the fruit of the path of knowledge — [thus
demonstrating] that [Shri Rudra is] the greatest of all the Vedas, comprising
within itself the paths of ritual action (karma-kanda), devotion (upasana-kanda),
and knowledge (jnana-kanda). And therein, all the procedures for [Rudra's]
propitiation have been set forth in accordance with the purport of the Shruti
(Vedic revelation), Smriti (recollected tradition), Itihasa (epics), and Purana
(mythological texts).

Now we shall explain the meaning of the Shri Rudra Upanishad.

[decorative symbol/illustration]

[Image: A decorative illustration of a deity (Shiva/Rudra) seated in a circular mandala, with multiple arms, holding various implements, surrounded by ornamental border]

|| Om ||

|| SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM ||

———*———

Om, salutation to You, O Rudra, to Your anger, and also to Your
arrow — salutation. Salutation to Your bow and to Your two arms
— salutation to them.

        I bow in constant adoration to the blissful one, the source of bliss,
          the gracious one,
        Of the nature of the Self, endowed with self-knowledge;
        The king of yogis, worthy of praise, the physician of the disease of
          worldly existence —
        To Shrimad Rudra, the eternally auspicious. || 1 ||

        Salutation to Dakshinamurti,
        Who is the moon-like nectar filling the lake of the assembly of the
          wise,
        Protector of all the worlds. || 2 ||

Now we shall explain the meaning of the Rudropanishad. Here there are
eleven anuvaka-s (sections/chapters). In the first [anuvaka], [the devotee]
seeks the grace of the Lord. In the second through sixth, there is salutation
to the Lord in [His aspects as] the Self of all beings (sarvatmaka), the
indwelling controller of all (sarvantaryami), and so forth. Then in the tenth
[anuvaka] there is a prayer to the Lord. In the eleventh —

sanskrit.samhita: नम॑स्ते रुद्र म॒न्यव॑ उ॒तोत॒ इष॑वे॒ नमः॑ । नम॑स्ते अस्तु॒ धन्व॑ने बा॒हुभ्या॑मु॒त ते॒ नमः॑ ॥
sanskrit.pada: नमः॑ । ते॒ । रु॒द्र॒ । म॒न्यवे᳚ । उ॒तो इति॑ । उ॒त । इष॑वे । नमः॑ । नमः॑ । ते॒ । अ॒स्तु॒ । धन्व॑ने । बा॒हुभ्या᳚म् । उ॒त । ते॒ । नमः॑ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नम॑स्ते । ते रु॑द्र । रु॒द्र म॒न्यवे᳚ । म॒न्यव॑ उ॒तो इति॑ । उ॒तो उ॒त । उ॒त इष॑वे । इष॑वे॒ नमः॑ । नम॒ इति॑ नमः॑ । नम॑स्ते । ते अ॑स्तु । अ॒स्तु॒ धन्व॑ने । धन्व॑ने बा॒हुभ्या᳚म् । बा॒हुभ्या॑मु॒त । उ॒त ते॒ । ते नमः॑ । नम॒ इति॑ नमः॑ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | te | rudra | manyave | uto | iti | te | ishave | namah | namah | te | astu | dhanvane | bahubhyam | iti | bahu-bhyam | uta | te | namah || 1 ||

Sayana's Commentary: O Rudra, let there be salutation to your anger (manyu), your wrath. May that anger spread only among our enemies, not against us. And (uto) also salutations to your arrow (ishu). Similarly, salutations to your bow (dhanvan). And also salutations to your two arms (bahu) which are equipped with the bow and arrow. The intention is: let all these operate only against enemies, not against me. (1)

Bhatta Bhaskara's Commentary: Om. For 'Namaste rudra manyava', the Rishi is Kashyapa. The Deity is Rudra. The Metre is Anushtup. These first five mantras are directly addressed (pratyakshakrita). Om. Svaha, Svadha, Vashat, Namah—these are the five names of Brahman. To achieve selfhood (atmatva) and Brahman-hood through worship with speech, mind, and body...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
46                    SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM

— in the eleventh anuvaka, there is a prayer to the group of Rudras who are
portions of the Lord — such is the purport of the eleven-chapter Shri Rudra
adhyaya (recitation).

Now, first of all, in order to propitiate the Lord for the removal of sins
accrued through three [previous] lifetimes, [the devotee] offers salutation:
"namaste rudra" — "Salutation to You, O Rudra."

O Rudra — O You who drive away the suffering of those who prostrate [to
You] — to Your manyave (wrath, anger), namah (salutation) be. One should
not object that wrath cannot be attributed to the Lord who is the ocean of all
auspicious qualities, free of all defects — for wrath directed toward the
wicked is not a blemish when it serves the purpose of restraining the guilty
and favoring the righteous; the Lord Himself has said: "The Shruti and Smriti
are My commandment; whoever transgresses that commandment becomes My
enemy and is to be punished by Me." Nor should one raise the objection that
this implies partiality (vaishamy a) or cruelty (nairghrunyam), since the
Brahma-mimamsa (the Vedanta philosophy, i.e., the Brahmasutras) explains in
detail that the Lord's bestowal of grace and punishment follows the [prior]
karma of individual beings. Here the vocative "rudra" is significant: the word
Rudra is etymologically derived by the Puranic tradition as meaning "one who
causes distress to flow away (dravayita)" — "He who causes suffering (ru) or
the cause of suffering (rut) to flow away (dravayita) — that compassionate one
is called Rudra; Shiva is His supreme cause" — thus the word is analyzed.
Accordingly, since You are the one who drives away the sins that are the cause
of the suffering of the prostrated devotee, it is entirely fitting that wrath
arising from [those] sins should be removed upon prostration.

Thus, the intent of the vocative "rudra" is evident. Having thus propitiated
the Lord, [the devotee] propitiates [the Lord's] weapons too with salutation:
"uto ta" — "uto" means "also, moreover." O Rudra, to Your ishave (arrow),
namah (salutation) be. Similarly, to Your dhanvane (bow) — the source from
which the arrow is released — namah be. And moreover, to the two arms
occupied by the bow and arrow, namah be. The sequence is: the Lord becomes
angry at the sinner who transgresses the divine commandment in the form of
Shruti and Smriti; for the purpose of striking that [sinner], He reaches for His
arrows; fixing His gaze on the bow from which those arrows are drawn, He
extends His arms to grasp the bow and arrow. In this very sequence [the
salutation] is offered here. The divine weapons are, as it were, forms of the
Lord's divine power — a power without limit and infinitely varied — worthy of
worship by the world, just like the divine forms of the deity used in worship.
Hence salutation and propitiation directed toward them are entirely appropriate.

Indeed it is heard in the Mahabharata: when Krishna and Arjuna journeyed to
Kailasa to recover the knowledge of the Pashupata weapon (which had been
forgotten), and having received the Lord's command went to fetch it, the
weapon — the Pashupata astra (the weapon of Pashupati, i.e., Shiva) — was
stationed there, terrible and exceedingly fearsome, with flames shooting from
the mouths of innumerable serpent-hoods. The power of Shri Krishna's
recitation of the Shataraudriya chapter (i.e., Shri Rudra) caused it to
become pacified and come to hand. This episode should be kept in mind here.

Now the seer (rishi), meter (chandas), and deity (devata) of this mantra are
to be indicated, for without knowing these a mantra recitation is prohibited.
Hence it is recited by the Chandogas (followers of the Samaveda): "He who
performs a rite or causes another to perform a rite with a mantra without
knowing [its] authorship (arsheya), meter (chandas), and deity (devata) in the
manner prescribed by the Brahmana text becomes a stump, falls into a pit, or
perishes — he becomes a sinner —

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
४६                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

भगवद्‌दंश भूतरुद्रसंघप्रार्थेनेलयेकादशातमकश्रीरुद्राध्यायार्धे:
तत्र तावत्रिजपापक्षयार्थेमीश्वरं प्रसादयितुं नमस्करोति—
नमस्ते रुद्रेति । हे रुद्र प्रणतदु:खद्रावक, ते त्त-
मन्यवे कोधाय, नमः अस्तु। न च निरस्तसमस्तदोषनि-
खिलकल्याणगुणगणसमुद्रे भगवति कथं कोधप्रसक्तिरिति
वाच्यम्, दुष्टनिग्रहशिष्टानुग्रहक्षमाभावे निग्राह्याविषये कोध-
स्यालंकारत्वेनादोषत्वात्; 'श्रुतिस्मृतिर्मेमैवाज्ञा यस्तामुल्लङ्घ-
यर्तते । आज्ञाच्छेद्वी मम द्वेष्य: स मे वध्यश्च जायते'
इति भगवतैवोक्तस्वात्। न चैवं वैषन्यनैर्घृण्यप्रसक्तिरिति वा-
च्यम्, तत्त्थाणिकर्मांनुसारेण निग्रहानुग्रहौ कुर्वेतस्तस
तत्प्रषक्तेरिति ब्रह्ममीमांसायां प्रपञ्च: । अत्र रुद्रेति हेतु-
गर्भे संबोधनम्। अत्र रुद्रशब्दो हि रुतं द्रावयतीत्यर्थे
'रुहु:खं दु:खहेतुर्वां तद्रावयित न: प्रसु:। रुद्र इत्युच्यते
तस्यात्छिव: परमकारणम्' इति पौराणिकैर्व्युत्पादित:
पुरोद्राद:; तथा च प्रणतदु:खत्वहेतुभूतपापद्रावकस्य तव
प्रणते सति पापनिमित्तक: क्रोध: परिहर्तुं युक्त एवेति भाव:।
एवमेपि रुद्रपदसंबोधनासिप्रायो द्रष्टव्य:। इत्थं भगवन्तं
प्रसाध तद्‌युघान्यपि नमस्कारेण प्रसाधयति— उतो त
इति; उतो अपि चेत्यर्थे; हे रुद्र, ते तव, इषचे बाणाय, नम:

sanskrit.samhita: या त॒ इषुः॑ शि॒वत॑मा शि॒व-म्ब॒भूव॑ ते॒ धनुः॑ । शि॒वा श॑र॒व्या॑ या तव॒ तया॑ नो रुद्र मृडय ॥
sanskrit.krama: या ते॒ । ते इषुः॑ । इषुः॑ शि॒वत॑मा । शि॒वत॑मा शि॒वम् । शि॒व-म्ब॒भूव॑ । ब॒भूव॑ ते॒ । ते धनुः॑ । धनु॒रिति॑ धनुः॑ । शि॒वा श॑र॒व्या॑ । श॒र॒व्या॑ या । या तव॑ । तव॒ तया॑ । तया॑ नः । नः॒ रु॒द्र॒ । रु॒द्र मृ॒डय । मृ॒ड॒येति॑ मृडय ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Ya | te | ishuh | shiva-tama | shivam | babhuva | te | dhanuh | shiva | sharavya | ya | tava | taya | nah | rudra | mridaya || 2 ||

Sayana's Commentary: O Rudra, that arrow of yours which has become most auspicious (shivatama), most peaceful. Similarly, that bow of yours which has become auspicious (shivam), peaceful. And that quiver (sharavya) of yours which is auspicious—with that peaceful arrow, that peaceful bow, and that peaceful quiver, make us (nah) happy (mridaya), give us joy. Their 'peacefulness' consists in the absence of their application against devotees. (2)
...

sanskrit.samhita: या ते॑ रुद्र शि॒वा त॒नूरघो॒रा-ऽपा॑पकाशिनी । तया॑ नस्त॒नुवा॒ शन्त॑मया॒ गिरि॑शन्ता॒भिचा॑कशीहि ॥
sanskrit.krama: या ते॒ । ते रु॑द्र । रु॒द्र शि॒वा । शि॒वा त॒नूः । त॒नूरघो॒रा । अ॒घो॒रा-ऽपा॑पकाशिनी । अ॒पा॑पकाशिनीति॑ अ॒पा॑प-काशिनी । तया॑ नः । नः॒ त॒नुवा॑ । त॒नुवा॒ शन्त॑मया । शन्त॑मया॒ गिरि॑शन्त । गिरि॑शन्ता॒भि । अ॒भि च॒क॒शी॒हि । च॒क॒शी॒हीति॑ चकाशिहि ॥

translations.sayana: [Word-for-word]: Ya | te | rudra | shiva | tanuh | aghora | apapa-kashini | iti | apapa- ...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
50                    SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM

— That form (tanuh): of what nature is it? It is answered: It is aghora —
not terrifying by means of weapons without cause; aghora means not ghora
(not terrifying). Not only is it the absence of terror, but further it is shiva —
auspicious, a bestower of happiness. And not merely a giver of worldly
happiness; rather, it bestows the knowledge that is the means to liberation
(moksha), which is the supreme human goal (paramapurusharta) — this is
declared by: "apapakashini." By the word apapa, the knowledge that is opposed
to sin — that is, the knowledge of Brahman (brahman-jnana, the knowledge of
the supreme reality) — is indicated, as per the Shruti: "tad yathaisika-tulam
agnau protam praduyeta evam ha va asya sarve papmanah praduyante" (just as
a reed's tip thrust into fire is burned up, so too all sins of this person are
burned away); and as the Gita states: "the fire of knowledge burns all actions
to ashes." Apapa (freedom from sin) + kashini (illuminer) = one who illumines
freedom from sin, i.e., a giver of knowledge — such is the meaning. [This is
confirmed by] the Shruti: "atmabuddhi-prakasham" (the light of Self-knowledge).

Such being that form of Yours: with that shantamaya (utterly tranquil)
tanva (form), the form that is of the nature of unclouded bliss — abhichakashihi
(shine forth upon us, illuminate us on all sides) — illuminate us completely,
as beings shining on all sides with the non-dual identity of Brahman and the
Self. "May all of this be known when this one thing is known" — [as the
Shruti says.] The form abhichakashihi, being a yak-tug verb form (an intensive),
shows the intensification of the verbal action indicating the seeking of complete
illumination — the meaning being "bring us to a direct, unmediated
realization of Brahman." The phrase "shantamaya abhichakashihi" shows that
the Supreme Lord is of the nature of consciousness-bliss (chidananda).

But, one might think: "If you want liberation through mere salutation alone,
[without] the great efforts of hearing, reflection, and deep meditation
(shravana, manana, nididhyasana), [why use the term girisha?]" — so the
second mode of address is used, correcting this misunderstanding:

"Girisha" —

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                       ४९

शरैव्ये ब्रह्मसंशिता' इति श्रवणात्; यद्रा श्रृणोतेर्हिंसाकर्मेण:
शप्रलये शरु:, शरव: शरा:, तेभ्यो हिवा शरव्या श्रुधि:;
तया शरव्यया— उपलक्षणमेतत्— तेन धनुषा, तया इष्वा च
न: अस्मान्, मृडय मुखय। अस्य मन्त्रस्य आत्रेयकृर्षि:;
अनुष्टुप्छन्द:, शंभुर्देवता। 'ध्यायेद्देवं सस्मितं स्यन्दनस्थं देव्या
सार्थे तेजसा दीप्यमानम् । इष्टिवषासालंकृताभ्यां भुजाभ्यां
शूराकारं स्तूयमानं सुरौघै:' । द्वादशदिनानि नित्यकर्मांवि-
रोधेन निशिशिथिनांव्यतिरेककाछे निरन्तरजपात्पुरश्चरणसि-
द्धि:; तत: प्रयोगाहौं भवति। दुर्भिक्षराष्ट्रक्षोभादिपरिहार-
प्रधानोऽयं मन्त्र: ।।

              या ते रुद्र शिवा तनूर्घोरापापका-
              शिनी। तया नस्तनुवा शांतमया गिरि-
              शांताभिचाकशीहि ।।

एवं द्वितीयेन सांसारिकसुखमाशाह्म तृतीयेन मोक्षहेलु-
ज्ञानमाशास्ते— या ते रुद्रेति। हे रुद्र, ते तव, या तनू:
प्रसिद्धा, श्रूयते हि— 'तस्यैते तनुवौ घोरान्या शिवान्या'
इति; भारतेऽपि 'द्वे तनू तस्य देवस्य देवज्ञा ब्राह्मणा-
विदु: । घोरा चान्या शिवा चान्या ते चैव शतधा पुन:'
                         R. B. 4

sanskrit.samhita: यामिषु॑-ङ्गिरिशन्त॒ हस्ते॒ बिभ॒र्​ष्यस्त॑वे । शि॒वा-ङ्गि॑रित्र॒ ता-ङ्कु॑रु॒ मा हिग्ं॑सीः॒ पुरु॑ष॒-ञ्जग॑त् ॥
