translations.sayana:
10  Sayanacharya—Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya-Sahita— [Na. Pra. Am.]

...meaning the suffix 'matup' expresses possession. He who resides with manifest happiness in places like Kailasa is 'Girishanta'.

Now for the Purashcharana of this mantra: having eaten sacrificial food (havishya) and chanting for three nights, one completes the Purashcharana. Now, with this, one should perform 'Go-shanti' (peace for cattle). Having made a fire-altar of one cubit in all directions in the middle of a cowshed, and having churned the fire, one should offer ten thousand sticks of Vaikangata and Apamarga wood, smeared with curd, honey, and ghee. Now, we will describe another peace-rite for cattle with this same mantra. Having consecrated pot-water with all perfumes by the Brahmins, and having chanted it a hundred thousand times, one should sprinkle the cows. Peace for the cows will ensue.

Meditation (Dhyanam):
"One should meditate on the Lord of Beings (Pashupati), the Controller, who has a smiling face, who is adorned with the crescent moon, who holds the Ganga, who is accompanied by the Daughter of the Mountain (Parvati), who has three eyes, and who is adorned with ashes and serpents." (3)

|| He speaks the fourth verse ||

That arrow, O dweller on the mountain, which you hold in your hand to throw;
Make that auspicious, O protector of the mountain; do not harm our people or our moving world. (4)

[Word-for-word]: Yam | ishum | giri-shanta | haste | bibharshi | astave || shivam | giri-tra | tam | kuru | ma | himsiih | purusham | jagat || 4 ||

Sayana's Commentary: O Girishanta, that arrow (ishu) which you hold in your hand to throw (astave) at enemies. "Giritra" means he who protects or guards (trayate) the mountain (giri) named Kailasa. O Rudra, make that arrow held in your hand auspicious (shiva), peaceful towards us. Do not harm our human people (purusham) or our 'jagat'—moving beings other than humans, like cattle etc. (4)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: For the two mantras 'Yamishum girishanta' and 'Shivenavachasa', the Rishi is Gautama. Some say Godhuma. The Deity is Rudra. 'Yamishum' is in Nichrit-Anushtup metre. 'Shivenavachasa' is in Anushtup. By these two, the fierce body engaged in the burning of Tripura is pacified. O Girishanta—explained already. That arrow which you hold (bibharshi) in your hand. For what purpose? To throw (astave) at the demons who are to be killed, after the request of the gods. As the gods said: "Who will throw this (arrow)?... Rudra is indeed cruel, let him throw it." He said "Let a boon (be given) to me." Or, after the burning of the straw of Tripura, that arrow which you hold in your hand to withdraw, having fulfilled its purpose as instructed—make that arrow auspicious (shiva), welfare-causing. Just as it targets only those to be killed, it should be understood as protecting your devotees. 'Ma himsiih'—do not harm me...
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM.                       54

— Even though in this way [the Lord has been] propitiated through salutation
and praise, [one might wonder:] how would the averting of misfortunes [caused
by] Yama (the god of death), the unrelenting and fierce ones, and other
terrible [beings] and other gods [who are] instruments of [fulfilling the results
of] the wicked person's sins — how would their [influence] be removed? Not
knowing this, [the devotee] now desires to know the path for its removal,
[realizing] that all these [other gods] are helpless and without any capacity
[to act] independently — this is stated:

"Adhy abocat" — adhivacana means speaking on behalf of, championing
[someone's cause], as [confirmed by] the Brahmana: "yad brahmano 'shajjagad
prathameyatam brahmanaya chivruyat" (whenever a brahmin goes before the
world, he should declare [the cause of the brahmin]). The meaning here is as
follows: adhivakta — He who is of the nature of championing the devotees'
cause, that is, foremost and supreme; daivya — He who exists among the gods,
in the sense of the indwelling controller of the gods (devantaryami-artha);
bhishak — the physician of all suffering, the healer of all the afflictions of
worldly existence such as disease and others. [This is confirmed by] the Shruti:
"bhishaktamam tva bhishajam shrinomi" (I hear that You are the greatest
physician among physicians). Such a Lord adhy abocat — has declared [His
stance] on behalf of [His devotees], "chandishi lungi" — in the optative [the
aorist is used in the sense of optative], meaning He makes a declaration
of partisanship for His devotees. What does He do [while declaring this]?
While destroying (sambhayan, annihilating) all ahi-s (snakes, and other visible
harms such as thieves and tigers), and all yatudhanya-s — classes of yatudha-
ja (evil spirits); rakshas, pishachas, and other invisible assailants — adhy
abocat, He has declared [His protection].

Here, the objection might be raised: "How can the Lord, who is impartial
toward all, make a partisan declaration in your favor? Doesn't this lead to
the charge of partiality (vaishanya) on His part?" To counter this, the word
adhivakta is used — the declaration on behalf of devotees is motivated by
their act of devotion; therefore it does not bring any defect upon the Lord.
"Vaishanya-nairghrnye na sapekshatvat" (partiality and cruelty do not apply
to the Lord, because His actions are conditioned [by devotees' karma]) —
such is the principle, as stated in the Vedanta.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                       ५३

ऽपि मम नाभानेदिच्छष्येव त्वां भजत: पुत्रपौत्रादिकं पाल-
येति संबोधनाभिप्राय: । इति चतुर्थ: ।।

              शिवेन वचसा त्वा  गिरिशाच्छा
              वदानसि। यथा न: सर्वेमिञ्जगद्‌यक्षमं-
              सुमना असत् ।।

तदेवं नमस्ते रुद्रेति नमस्कृत्य द्वितीयंतृतीयचतुर्थैर्गिरिश-
प्राध्यनिष्टपरिहारौ प्रार्थेतौ; इदानीं स्तुतिपूर्वेकं तनुभयं
प्रार्थेयते— शिवेन वचसेति। गिरिशब्देन गिरिरूपा वेदाः
उच्यन्ते, तत्र श्रुति:— 'तं ह त्रीन् गिरिरूपानविज्ञातानिव
दर्शयांचकार' इति प्रस्तुय, 'वेदा वा एते' इति। तथा
च गिरिरूपेषु वेदेषु शेते तिष्ठतीति गिरिश: वेदप्रतिपाद्य:;
हे गिरिश, अच्छा निर्मलम्, त्वां शिवेन वचसा वदा-
मसि स्तुम इत्यर्थे: । कीदशी स्तुतिस्तत्राह— यथा येन
प्रकारेण कृतया स्तुत्या, न: अस्माकं संबन्ध, सर्वेमित्त्  सर्वे-
ऽपि, जगत् पशुपुत्रादिरूपं जङ्गमजातम्, अयक्ष्मम् अरोगम्,
सुमना: सौमनस्योपेतं सुखितं च, असत् स्यात्, तथा स्तुम
इति; अत्र गिरिशेत्यनेन निखिलवेद्वर्णेनीयस्य स्तोत्रं युक्त-
मेवेति दिर्शतम्। तत्र पापिष्ठस्य उक्तिसंबन्धात्परमेश्वरस्य

translations.sayana:
Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: For 'Adhyavochadadhivakta', the metre is Anushtup. Some say Nichrit. The Rishi is Kanva. The Deity is Shambhu. These four mantras starting from here are in the third person (parokshakrita). 'Adhyavochat'—let him speak for (us). The past tense is used in the sense of the imperative in the Veda. 'Adhivachanam' means a speech that is superior or elevated. It means speaking with partiality (favor). As in "one should speak for a Brahmin." Or, it means speaking with lordship or superiority; may he speak for us devotees. 'Adhivakta' means one whose nature is to speak for others; or one who is always capable of speaking for his devotees. Those who are not lords, if they speak for others, become objects of laughter. He states the reason for his being an advocate: 'Prathamah'—the foremost, the chief. 'Daivyah'—existing among the gods. Or, 'devana' (from which 'deva' is derived) means play, desire for conquest, business, light, praise, or movement. He is the form of joy in play; the form of victory in the desire for conquest; the form of clarity in business; the form of light in illumination; the form of the object of praise in hymns; and the form of the destination or the form of wind in movement. Or, the gods are those endowed with divine knowledge; he is the foremost among them. 'Bhishak'—physician. He is the healer of afflictions (vyasana) like disease, heat, and worldly existence. There is another mantra: "I hear you are the physician of physicians." 'Bhishak' can also mean he who makes fear perish, or he who is reached by the mind in the midst of fear, because he is the ground of fearlessness. Or, he who graces through the breaking of the body (of ego). Doing what should he speak for us? He says here: 'Ahimmshcha sarvan jambhayan'—by means of destroying. 'Jambhanam' means destruction. 'Ahayah' are those who come and harm beings; those whose nature is to strike, the direct killers of subjects like tigers, serpents, scorpions, etc.—destroying all of them. The word 'cha' (and) should be seen after 'all', meaning 'destroying all of them indeed'. And all the 'yatudhanis'—destroying all the races of Yatudhanas (sorcerers/demons). 'Yatus' are the sorrows or the illusions that cause pain; 'Yatudhanas' are those in whom these reside. This is what is said: may he speak for us while destroying all those who wish to strike, like scorpions, tigers, thieves, etc., and those who are not peaceful.
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM.                       55

[Continuing the commentary on the sixth mantra "adhyabocat..."]

Those who are fierce, relentless, and unvanquishable — the servants of
Yama and other deities — and those who are instruments of delivering the
fruits of that wicked person's sins, [as well as] other gods — how would
the averting of their [inflicted] misfortunes be possible? Not knowing this,
the [devotee] desires to know the remedy; [the Lord] declares that all of
them are incapable and powerless [on their own]:

"adhyabocat" — adhivacana is speaking in favor of, championing [a cause]:
as [confirmed by] the Brahmana, "when a brahmin goes before the world,
he should declare [the cause of the brahmin]." The meaning here is: He who
is by nature an adhivakta (advocate, champion) of devotees — that is,
foremost and supreme; daivya — dwelling among the gods, meaning the
indwelling controller of the gods; bhishak — the physician, the healer of
all the afflictions of worldly existence such as illness and others. [As the
Shruti says:] "bhishaktamam tva bhishajam shrinomi" (I hear You are the
greatest physician among physicians). Such a Lord, adhy abocat — has
proclaimed [His partisanship]. What does He do [while proclaiming]?
Sambhayan — destroying all ahis (serpents and other visible assailants
including thieves and tigers) and all yatudhanyah — classes of yatudha
beings; those who are invisible assailants like rakshasas, pishachas, and
others — while destroying them, He has proclaimed [His protection].

Here, [if one objects:] "How can a Lord who is the same toward all make
a partisan proclamation in your favor? Does this not lead to partiality
(vaishanya)?" — to dispel this objection, the word adhivakta is used.
The Lord's declaration on behalf of devotees is motivated by the devotees'
act of devotion; hence it brings no fault upon the Lord. "Vaishanya-
nairghrnye na sapekshatvat" (partiality and cruelty do not apply, because
of [the Lord's actions being conditioned by] devotees' actions) — such is
the principle here.

Even though the Lord champions [His devotees'] cause, there might be the
fear: "will not the suffering resulting from the actions of Yama and other
independent deities still occur?" To remove this doubt, prathama (foremost)
is used: [scriptural passages confirm the Lord's supremacy:] "yo devanam
prathamah" (He who is foremost among gods); "tameshvaranam prathamam
maheshvaram" (the foremost of all lords, the great Lord); "yo devah
sarvadeveshu mahadeva iti smritah, tasmai namastuh kurvana divi tishthanti
devatah" (the gods known among all gods as Mahadeva — those who
salute Him dwell in heaven). Since the one who champions [the devotee's
cause] is the foremost of all gods, there is no room for fear of suffering
caused by subordinate gods.

Not only [freedom from] suffering caused by other gods, but [the devotee
also attains] the status of being worshipped by all gods — because, by
virtue of the Lord being the indwelling controller (antaryami) of all those
gods, the singular supreme deity who has become a partisan advocate [of
the devotee] — this is indicated by the word "daivyah," which implies the
Lord's quality of being the indwelling controller of all gods.

Through the word "bhishak" (physician), and through the analogy of the
physician from the Shiva Purana — "a physician who knows diagnosis causes
harm to the patient through surgery, cauterization, and the like, but here
there is no cruelty; compassion alone motivates him" — the supreme
compassion of the Lord is expressed. And thereby, even while striking the
ahis and yatudhanyahs, His compassion toward even those beings is implied.
Just as a surgeon (vrana-vaidya), though momentarily causing suffering to
the wounded through surgery and cauterization, yet heals the patient's
wounds and thus brings happiness — and is therefore compassionate — so
too the Lord who is the samsara-vaidya (physician of worldly existence),
even while striking the ahis and yatudhanyahs, by removing the sins that
are the cause of their future birth and thus purifying them, progressively
brings them to happiness — therefore His compassion is not diminished.

[The text shows that:] since He declares [His protection] — saying "adhy
abocat" (He has declared), not "jambhayatu" (may He destroy) — the Lord's
faultless sovereignty is demonstrated. Not the manner of a subordinate who,
incapable of subduing the ahis and yatudhanyahs himself, propitiates them
and intercedes for the devotee saying "please spare this one who is mine"
— but rather: Himself capable, first protecting the devotee by destroying
the ahis and yatudhanyahs, [then] when the gods come asking "how should
this sinner be protected?", making the partisan declaration "This one is
mine — what sin does he have?" — hence even great sinners [are protected].
Stories of hunters and others whose Yama-messengers were destroyed by the
Lord's divine emissaries, and who were brought to Kailasa, and to whom
Yama and Chitragupta reported their deeds — and to whom the Lord replied:
"Even if inadvertently, these have performed acts such as throwing bilva
leaves [at My image] and prostrations; therefore they are Mine and you
have no authority over them" — such stories are repeatedly told in the
Puranas.

With this understanding, in this mantra "raksha ca no adhi ca deva bruhi"
(protect us, and then champion our cause, O God) — protection comes first,
then the declaration of partisanship — this is the sequence shown. Thus
this mantra is the remover of all afflictions from serpents, rakshasas,
pishachas, and all other beings. Hence its designation as a kavacha
(armor/protective formula) — as stated by the revered Bodhayana:
"adhyabocat iti kavacham" (the mantra 'adhyabocat' is the protective armor).

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                       ५५

ऋगुताद्दितामितंदुर्दान्तानासन्येषां च त्वदीयपापप्रसांधिसूता-
नामन्येषां देवानां सर्वेण कथं तदानिष्टपरिहार: स्यादित्व-
ज्ञेच्य तत्पथादे सति ते सर्वेऽव्यविकिंचितकरा इत्याह—
अध्यबोचद्दिति। पक्षपातवचनमधिवचनम्, 'यद्ब्राह्मण-
ब्राशज्ञगद्ध प्रभमेयातां ब्राह्मणायाचिव्रूयात्' इति ब्रह्म-
णात्। तथा चायमर्थे:— अधिवक्ता भक्तेभ्योऽधिवचनस्थ-

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Asau | yah | tamrah | arunah | uta | babhruh | su-mangalah || ye | cha | imam | rudrah | abhito | dikshu | shritah | sahasrashah | iti | sahasra-shah | avai | esham | hedah | imahe || 7 ||

Sayana's Commentary: That Rudra who is in the form of the Sun (Aditya) in the solar disc—he is 'tamrah', extremely red at the time of sunrise. 'Arunah'—slightly red after rising. 'Uta'—and also 'babhruh'—tawny/brown further after that. Other colors occurring at different times should also be understood. 'Sumangalah'—being of various colors, he is extremely auspicious as he removes darkness etc. at various times. And those others, the Rudras in the form of rays, who are spread around (abhito) this earth, situated in all the directions (dikshu) like the East etc., and who are in the thousands (sahasrashah)—many thousands in number—of all these Rudras in the form of the Sun and his rays, we remove (av-imahe) their 'heda'—the sharpness which is like anger—through devotion, salutation, etc. (7)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Asau yastamrah' and the following is in Aastara-pankti metre. "If the last two feet are of twelve syllables, it is Aastara-pankti," according to the rule. Some say it is a Pankti of five feet. 'Asau yo-vasarpati' is in Jagati metre of six feet. For these two, the Rishi is Kaala (Time). Some say Marutvan. The Deity is Rudra of the nature of Aditya (the Sun). "With 'Asau yastamrah', one waits upon the Sun," says the Shatapatha Brahmana. Since Aditya is included among the eight bodies of the God which perform the withdrawal of the universe. Through these two (mantras 7 and 8), he prays for his grace regarding devotees. 'Asau'—he points with his finger. That Sun in the upper world. What kind of a one? 'Tamrah'—extremely red at the time of rising. He shines thus because of the contact with heat. Some say: because of the contact with the dust of the earth. Then 'Arunah'—slightly red after rising. And also 'Babhruh'—tawny after that. Some say fair (gaura).
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
58                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

---

The seer (ṛṣi) of this mantra is Kaṇva, the metre (chandas) is Anuṣṭup, and the deity (devatā) is Śaṃsu. [The dhyāna-śloka for meditation is:] "One should meditate upon the three-eyed Lord (trilochana), who is adorned with a digit of the moon, who bears a serpent as his sacred thread, and who is resplendent like blazing fire." The purascaraṇa (preliminary consecration) is accomplished by reciting [the mantra] forty-five thousand times. Thus [ends] the sixth [mantra].

    "He who is that copper-red one, the ruddy Aruṇa, and also the tawny Babhru, the greatly auspicious one — and the Rudras who dwell in all the directions around this [earth], stationed in the thousands — may we appease their fury (heḍa)."

Thus with six mantras the Lord Supreme has been graciously propitiated. Now, the Blessed Lord who is the witness of all actions and the indwelling self of all beings is propitiated through two [mantras]: "Asau yas tāmra" — meaning: He who was described above, that very Blessed Lord, is Asau, directly visible, manifest in the sun. His visible form is further described by its characteristics: tāmra (copper-red), Aruṇa (ruddy), and Babhru (tawny). At the time of sunrise he is deeply crimson, hence "tāmra" (copper-red); a little later he becomes a pale red, hence "Aruṇa" (ruddy); then he becomes tawny-coloured, hence "Babhru." As the verse goes: "Piṅgala (tawny), Lakula (club-bearer), Khalati (bald), and Viṣṇu — these four are known by the word 'babhru.'" He is "Sumaṅgala" (supremely auspicious) because, by removing darkness and the like, He causes [creation] to awaken and stand; for without the Lord in the form of the sun, the world sunk in darkness would indeed be utterly inauspicious — such is the meaning.

translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                   61

---

    "May there be salutation to the blue-throated one (Nīlagrīva), to the thousand-eyed one (Sahasrākṣa), to the boon-granting one (Mīḍhuṣe). And likewise may I offer salutation to those attendant beings (sattvānaḥ) of his."

Having thus propitiated the solar form through the seventh and eighth [mantras], now through the ninth [mantra] one offers salutation to seek the grace of Śrī Nīlakaṇṭha, who dwells on Mount Kailāsa: "Namo astu" — to Nīlagrīva, to the one of the form of the blue-throated one (Nīlakaṇṭhamūrti), may there be salutation. Of what nature [is he]? To Sahasrākṣa — the word "sahasra" (thousand) denotes the unlimited, as is known from the Smṛti: "Viśvam, śatam, sahasram ca — all these are used to mean 'all' or 'the totality'." He whose eyes are in the thousands — to him, Sahasrākṣa, meaning one of infinite, unbounded vision. By this [epithet] it is implied: "Even though you dwell on Kailāsa, my salutation here is within the field of your vision." Again, of what nature [is he]? To Mīḍhuṣe — the granter of desires to those who prostrate before him. By this [epithet], the capacity to bestow as the fruit of salutation the fulfilment of all desired objects is indicated. And by [the epithet] Nīlagrīva — meaning "one who bears the blue throat caused by drinking the Kālakūṭa poison" — the well-known glory of the Lord is implied: "The wise one takes credit for the virtue and does not become pained by the fault; he first honours it on his head and then holds the latter at the throat." By this, the Lord Supreme, who contains the entire mass of faults (like the kālakūṭa poison) within himself without allowing even a fraction to escape, yet honours every little virtue on his head, is revealed as one who is easy to worship. Having thus saluted the Blessed Lord, [the devotee] now salutes his retinue (pāriṣada): "Atho ya iti" — "And also, those sattvānaḥ (beings of virtuous nature, the close attendants)

translations.sayana:
18  Sayanacharya—Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya-Sahita— [Na. Pa. Am.]

...thus he says. There, he begins to show the God's favors to the worlds as he sets. The cowherds see this venerable God setting. While waiting for the time when the cattle return to the village and town, the water-carriers also see him and think, "The Lord is setting, oh, we are released from this (work)." Or, they see that the drawing of water must be done before sunset, as it is prohibited once he has set. What more? All beings see him setting, as the going to shelters of deer, birds, boars, etc., is dependent on that. The twice-born also wait for the venerable one to set, being desirous of the time for Agnihotra and Sandhyavandana. Everyone indeed sees this God setting. And that is prohibited:

"One should never look at the Sun while he is rising, nor while he is setting."

The mention of cowherds etc. is to show that he is seen by those of such names. In that, even if seen during a prohibited time by oversight, or during the time of worship (upasthana), may he look upon us and make us happy (mridayati). Even if he is seen due to oversight etc., having removed that sin and given the fruit of worship, may he make us happy.

Now for the Purashcharana of these two (mantras 7 and 8): having performed one Krichhra penance, one should chant continuously for sixteen days. With these two, when there is no rain, one should act for the sake of rain. Either during the Ardra constellation or ending with it. Having invited five perfected Brahmins, it should be performed by them. One should give them turbans, clothes, earrings, necklaces, bracelets, etc. One should offer a lakh (100,000) of the prepared offering material—sesame, rice, and barley smeared with curd, honey, and ghee. Similarly, one should offer vetasa-wood sticks soaked in milk. Among those five, one should chant and four should offer for eleven days of initiation; on the final day, one should worship with eight thousand red lotuses (rakta-tamarasa) for each. Having chanted once, at the time of the ritual, one should sit having touched the tiger-skin and looking at it; otherwise, he becomes subject to atonement. Fire-protection must be done until the completion. On the final day, the Lord Aditya releases a great rain. If one offers ten thousand vetasa flowers, Savitar grants a boon. One should worship the rising and setting Sun with these two. Standing in neck-deep water, one should throw water filled in both hands toward the Sun while chanting these two. Doing this eight thousand times each day for nine days, Savitar then grants the heart's desire within a year. If one offers twenty-one thousand hibiscus (japa) flowers smeared with honey, by the grace of Savitar he attains the memory of previous births (jatis-maratva) and is born in a family of the four Vedas.

Meditation (Dhyanam):
"One should meditate on the Golden One residing within the solar disc, with a shining body and a pure smile, of fierce rays and unbroken brilliance, who is served by thousands of sages." (8)

|| He speaks the ninth verse ||

Salutation be to the blue-throated one, the thousand-eyed, the bountiful;
And those who are his attendants—to them also I make salutation. (9)
...

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Ya | te | hetih | midhuh-tama | haste | babhuva | te | dhanuh || taya | asman | vishvatah | tvam | ayakshmaya | pari | bhuj || 13 ||

Sayana's Commentary: O most bountiful one (midhustama), greatest shedder of desires—that weapon (heti) of yours in the form of a sword etc., and also that bow which was in your hand; with that weapon and with that kind of bow, which is 'ayakshmaya'—not causing injury or disturbance—you protect (paribhuj) us from all sides (vishvatah), guard us entirely. (13)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: Now 'Ya te hetih' and the following three are in Anushtup. The Rishi and Deity are the Lord Himself. O Midhustama—'midhvan' has been explained; the greatest shedder. That weapon (heti) belonging to you, named the bow, which exists (babhuva) in your hand. 'Heti' is derived from the root 'han' (to kill/strike). With that weapon named the bow, which is 'ayakshmaya'—not a cause of disease or injury to devotees. From all causes of fear (vishvatah), you protect (paribhuj) us entirely. Guard us at all times, in all ways, and from all sins. The root 'bhuj' has a Vedic variation. Or: protect all of us (vishvatah), including sons and grandsons. Some say: 'heti' means violence; that violence performed by you, for the sake of which the bow, the means of violence, stays in your hand—protect us from that, etc., is the same. (13)

|| He speaks the fourteenth verse ||

Salutation be to your weapon which is unstrung and powerful;
And also salutation to your two arms and to your bow.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                   65

---

    "Let the bow of the matted-haired one (Kapardin) be unstrung, and the quiver too be emptied of arrows. Let the arrows of his become incapable of wounding, and let his quiver be swift [but empty]."

Now, even for the arrows that have been blunted, the prayer is offered for their incapacitation: "Vijyam iti" — may the bow of Kapardin (the Lord with matted hair, who is the Supreme Lord), be vijya — without its bowstring; and may the arrow-holder (bāṇavān — one who bears arrows) also be viśalya — devoid of arrows. Even if the quiver holds shafts, there is the concern about arrows elsewhere, hence the [mantra] says: "Aneśan iti" — may the arrows of this Kapardin be incapable of wounding, having attained destruction in the form of inability to pierce (aneśan — may they fail to reach; nāśa in the optative-like usage of the Vedic [aorist]). Furthermore, may his niṣaṅghiḥ (the quiver) be āśu (swift/ready) — meaning capable only of holding the quiver but not qualified to perform the task of pulling out and discharging the arrows. Here, [the text] "Prabhuśca dhanvana" and so forth constitutes a single mantra for the teacher. The seer (ṛṣi) of this pair of [mantras] is Nārada, the metre is Anuṣṭup, and the deity is Śrī Rudra. [The dhyāna-śloka is:] "One should meditate upon Rudra, blazing like ten million rising suns, his form like a flaming fire, his limbs adorned with fearsome serpents, bearing various weapons." Through this [mantra] enemies are destroyed. Thus [ends] the twelfth [mantra].

translations.sayana:
[Na. Pra. Am.] Rudradhyaya. 5

...this is the meaning of the collection of sentences. Some say: having pleased the God Rudra with the first anuvaka, then, through the next eight anuvakas, not knowing what seat the God might be presiding over, one salutes everything moving and unmoving with the suspicion that it might be his seat, and then prays for fearlessness in the final ones. Indeed, the God is the ground of absolute freedom; presiding over various things, he performs punishment and grace for the world. For instance—appearing in the form of a Yaksha, he deluded the gods. Taking the guise of a cheat, he deluded the seven sages. Adorned in the guise of a hunter (Kirata), he played with the son of Pandu (Arjuna). With the body of Bhairava, along with the Goddess who took a certain guise, he generated Gajanana (Ganesha). In the form of Indra, he desired to lead some devotee to the attainment of penance. In the form of the Arka tree, he resided in the lap of the Goddess. With the body of an owl, he taught knowledge to someone. Adorned in the guise of a madman (unmatta), he showed the organ of procreation to the best of gods as the decorated cause of the universe. To prevent some devotee who wished to kill his sinful self, he appeared from the laundry-stone of a washerman. He partook of the oblation placed in a hole in the ground by someone. Thus, this universe is the play of the God of gods who accepts various guises according to his will. Therefore, one salutes him by praising his universal form. As it is said:
'Your deeds are diverse, secret, and deep; O Shankara, you are hard to be known by all including Brahma.'

|| Here, in the first anuvaka, he speaks the first verse ||
Harih Om.

Salutations to your anger, O Rudra; and salutations to your arrow.
Salutations be to your bow, and salutations to your two arms. (1)

[Word-for-word]: Namah | te | rudra | manyave | uto | iti | te | ishave | namah | namah | te | astu | dhanvane | bahubhyam | iti | bahu-bhyam | uta | te | namah || 1 ||

Sayana's Commentary: O Rudra, let there be salutation to your anger (manyu), your wrath. May that anger spread only among our enemies, not against us. And (uto) also salutations to your arrow (ishu). Similarly, salutations to your bow (dhanvan). And also salutations to your two arms (bahu) which are equipped with the bow and arrow. The intention is: let all these operate only against enemies, not against me. (1)

Bhatta Bhaskara's Commentary: Om. For 'Namaste rudra manyava', the Rishi is Kashyapa. The Deity is Rudra. The Metre is Anushtup. These first five mantras are directly addressed (pratyakshakrita). Om. Svaha, Svadha, Vashat, Namah—these are the five names of Brahman. To achieve selfhood (atmatva) and Brahman-hood through worship with speech, mind, and body...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
66                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

---

    "and your bow. With that weapon of yours, free from disease (ayakṣmayā), and with your bow too — protect us from all sides."

Having thus prayed through three mantras for the withdrawal of the weapons, [the devotee] now through these [mantras] prays for self-protection: "Yā te heti riti" — O Mīḍhuṣṭama (you who grant desires to the highest degree), this address carries within it the implicit reason [for the prayer]: "the weapon (heti) of yours, whatever bow you have in hand, and with that weapon which is free from disease (ayakṣmayā — the cause of health for devotees), and with your bow too" — this is to be understood as included. "You, protect us from all sides (viśvataḥ), from all distress." Thus [ends] the thirteenth [mantra].

    "May there be salutation to your weapon (āyudhāya) which is stretched out and firm (anatatāya dhṛṣṇave). And likewise may there be salutation to both your arms and to your bow."

Now, having thus prayed, he again offers salutation: "Namaste iti" — O Blessed Lord, to your weapon (āyudhāya) may there be salutation. Of what nature [is the weapon]? Anatatāya — not stretched out, not strung [for attack]; the opposite of stretched (ānata) is unstrung (anānata), which is this. Dhṛṣṇave — capable of striking with force. And furthermore, to both your arms (ubhābhyāṃ bāhubhyāṃ) may there be salutation. Although the dual is already sufficient to indicate the two, the mention of both arms separately, out of extreme fear —

translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                   67

---

[continuing the fourteenth mantra commentary]

and repeating "ubhābhyām" (to both) — "and also salutation to your bow (dhanvane)." Thus [ends] the fourteenth [mantra].

    "Let the weapon launched from your bow protect us from all sides. And that quiver of yours — place it here, far away from us, in the midst of our enemies."

Now, after offering salutation, [the devotee] again prays: "Pari te dhanvana iti" — O Blessed Lord, the weapon (heti) — meaning an arrow — belonging to your bow (dhanvanaḥ), and [thus] understood as having been placed upon the bow — let such a weapon (heti) protect us from all distress (viśvataḥ — from all sides), making us well-guarded. But how, when there is a mass of sins present, can protection from distress [caused] by you come about? That is addressed: "Atho — and also, your quiver (iṣudhi) in which your arrows rest — place that (tam dhehī) in our enemies' quarter (āre — in the multitude of adversaries), in the mass of our sins, and destroy [them there]." Thus [ends] the fifteenth mantra. For the pair of verses beginning "Yā te heti" and so forth, the metre is Anuṣṭup; the seer and deity are the Blessed Lord himself. [The dhyāna-śloka is:] "May one perpetually meditate upon the Father of the worlds, the holder of the Pināka (bow), whose lotus feet are reddened with the pollen of the garlands of flowers worn on the crowns of the hosts of prostrating immortals, seated with his consort." This mantra is primarily [efficacious] for the destruction of enemies.

शत्रुनाशप्रघानोऽयं मन्त्र: ॥

sanskrit.samhita: नमो॑ हिरण्यबाहवे सेना॒न्ये॑ दि॒शा-ञ्च॒ पत॑ये॒ नमो॒

translations.sayana:
Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: For this anuvaka starting with 'Namo hiranyabahave', the Rishi is Manduka. The Metre is Maha-Gayatri. Rudra is the Deity. There, having pacified the Lord Rudra and his means of destruction through the first anuvaka, and having had them withdrawn, he now begins to praise and salute him in his universal form (vaishvarupya) through the next eight anuvakas. Regarding that, Shakapuni says: "In three anuvakas (2nd, 3rd, 4th), a single Yajus starts with 'Namah' and ends with 'Namah'." Yaska says: "The 'Namah' at the start is one Yajus, and the 'Namah' at the end is another Yajus." Kashakritsna says: "The eight anuvakas are eight (long) Yajus mantras." There, praise and salutation are for pairs of Rudras. As seen in the Shatapatha Brahmana: "One offers to two and two." Even in a single entity, the duality is established by imposing a difference based on eight different causes of salutation; where many are mentioned, one should be understood as an adjective for the others. For example: "Salutation to the golden-armed commander of armies, and to the lord of the directions." Among them, some have salutations at both ends (ubhayato-namaskara), and some have salutation at only one end. There is a Brahmana text on this: "Those Rudras which have salutations at both ends are more fierce (ghoratara). And those which have salutation at only one end are more peaceful (shantatara)." Formerly, when prayed to by Brahma for the sake of creation, the God created Rudras of similar nature to himself. It is said elsewhere: "Equal in creation, power, and wisdom." There, salutations are made to those attendants of the God (in plural forms); or in the singular forms, salutations are made everywhere to everything out of the suspicion that the God resides there.

Now we state the meters of the individual Yajus: for those of one syllable, Daivi Gayatri; two syllables, Daivi Ushnik; three syllables, Daivi Anushtup; four syllables, Daivi Brihati; five syllables, Daivi Pankti; six syllables, Daivi Trishtup or Yajushi Gayatri; seven syllables, Daivi Jagati or Yajushi Ushnik; eight syllables, Prajapatya Gayatri or Yajushi Anushtup; nine syllables, Yajushi Brihati or Asuri Jagati; ten syllables, Yajushi Pankti or Asuri Trishtup; eleven syllables, Yajushi Trishtup or Asuri Pankti; twelve syllables, Yajushi Jagati or Prajapatya Ushnik or Asuri Brihati. Or, the Gayatri of the Samas, etc., should be understood according to the scriptures. There, at the beginning, to show that "this universe which is yours, you are its Master, and no Master harms his own property," he establishes the God's Lordship over the universe (vishva-patitva). And because of its primacy, first the material...
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Second Anuvāka

    "Salutation to the golden-armed one (Hiraṇyabāhave), to the leader of armies (Senānye), and to the lord of the directions (diśāṃ ca pataye namaḥ)."

Thus in the First Anuvāka, the Blessed Lord along with his retinue was propitiated through fifteen mantras. Now, in the Second and subsequent eight anuvākas, the Blessed Lord is saluted in his form as the Lord of all (sarveśvara) and as the indwelling self of all (sarvāntaryāmin). Beginning here [with this mantra] and extending through "abhayaḥ apatisyaśca vo namaḥ," all mantras have the structure of salutations (namaskāra) both at the beginning and end. In each mantra, "namas" appears at the beginning and "namaḥ" at the end, making each a single complete mantra — for example: "namo hiraṇyabāhave senānye diśāṃ ca pataye namaḥ." The same holds for those that follow. Now then, in order to establish the universal lordship (sarveśvartva) of the Lord by first demonstrating his sovereignty over individual things within the manifest world, the commentary begins: "Namo hiraṇyabāhave iti" — Hiraṇyabāhave: to the one whose arms are adorned with golden armlets (keyūra) and the like. The mention of arms (bāhu) is also indicative [of the whole body]: from head to foot his entire divine auspicious form is of a golden hue, endowed with supremely celestial golden ornaments — thus do the Vedic texts proclaim. For example: "Namo hiraṇyabāhave hiraṇyavarṇāya" (salutation to the golden-armed, golden-coloured one), "Ābhraṇakhāt sarve suvarṇaiḥ" (all [his limbs] are golden from ornament to skin), "Yadāpamayaḥ paśyate rukmavarṇam" (when the sage perceives him of golden colour) — and so forth. The Smṛti texts too: "Rukmārsaṃ svapravīgamyam" (perceivable by one of purified mind), "Cārucāmīkarābhāsam" (resplendent like beautiful gold) — and so forth.

Senānye: the senānī is one who leads armies (senā nayati — the one who leads the host). He is the lord of the various armies in the form of the aggregations of all divine beings (devādinā) assembled to combat the sufferings of the world. In the Āśvalāyana school, introducing Śiva, it is stated: "All consecrations (nāmadheyāni) indeed belong to him; all armies (senāḥ) and all standards (ucchrāyaṇāni)." Thus, as senānī, and also as the lord of the directions (diśāṃ — from east onwards, pataye — to the lord), may there be salutation. [The grammatical rule] "ṣaṣṭhīyuktaścha chandasi vā" accounts for the ghi-designation (a special grammatical form). Here the primary syntactic connection is "diśāṃ pataye namaḥ." Now, how is the Supreme Lord the lord of the directions, when Indra, Agni and others are lords of the directions? To dispel this doubt, "Senānye" has been specified — through which it is shown that it is the Supreme Lord, as the overlord of all the hosts of divine beings, who has lordship over the directions; Indra and the others are merely lords of their respective directions by his command. And regarding the question of how the Supreme Lord can be lord over Indra and the others, "Hiraṇyabāhu" is specified — by which the form of the Supreme Lord that is worshipped in the Antara-Āditya vidyā (the meditative practice of [the Lord] within the sun) is meant, as [the Vedic text states]: "He who is that golden-hued person within the sun (ya eṣo'ntarāditye hiraṇmayaḥ puruṣaḥ)." Hence the application of "namo hiraṇyabāhave" in the Antara-Āditya vidyā is made in the Purāṇas and other texts. "Those worlds above, and whatever there are of the worlds [below] — he is the lord of those, and of whatever they desire for themselves" — thus the Antara-Āditya vidyā —

translations.sayana:
Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: Salutation to those who are in the form of trees, or who reside in trees. The word 'tree' points to the resident by the container, as in "the platforms are shouting" (meaning people on them). 'Harikeshebhyah'—those who have leaves in the place of hair that are green, or those whose hair is green. Or: 'Vriksha'—all beings from Brahma down to a blade of grass, grown in the Cosmic Egg (Brahmanda), are indeed 'trees'. Derived from the root 'vriksh' (to cut/destroy); the meaning is those who are subject to destruction. Salutation to him who is the self of those. 'Harikeshebhyah'—to him with green-colored hair. Salutation to the Lord of cattle (pashu); to him who exists as the Master of those cattle belonging to him, whether two-legged or four-legged. As it is said: "Of which cattle Pashupati is the Lord, the four-legged and the two-legged."
And in the Purana: "All from Brahma down to the stationary things are proclaimed as 'Pashu' (tethered beings). Because he is their Lord, Bhavah is remembered as Pashupati."
In the Mahabharata: "Because he always protects the 'Pashus' and delights with them, and because he is their Overlord, he is remembered as Pashupati." (2)

He speaks the third Yajus:
Salutation to the one who is golden-red like young grass, to the radiant one, salutation to the lord of paths.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                   73

---

— referring to the triple-quality (triguṇa) karma-kāṇḍa (the ritual section of the Vedas) characterised by leaves in the form of Vedic metres (chandas), as [indicated by the Vedic text]: "The Vedas deal with the subjects of the three guṇas," and "Leaves are its hymns (chandāṃsi yasya parṇāni)." Just as in the case of a tree, leaves serve to protect it, so the Vedic metres (chandas) that are of the nature of karma-kāṇḍa serve to protect the tree of saṃsāra, since they perpetuate worldly existence by inculcating action. And these, on account of their infinite abundance surpassing the understanding of the head (śiras), are described as hair (keśa). Thus the portion of the mantra "namo vṛkṣebhyo hirikeśebhyaḥ" is concluded in meaning as: salutation to the Supreme Lord in his aspect as the tree of saṃsāra, intertwined with the threefold web of karmic activity. Through this, the two questions — how [creatures] have pāśutva (the condition of being paśu, bound), and how [the Lord] has patitva (the condition of being pati, lord) — are both answered: creatures are paśu by virtue of being bound by the ropes of saṃsāra (saṃsārapāśa), and the Supreme Lord is pati of those [creatures] by virtue of causing them to perform their respective functions. This has been clearly amplified by some: "From Brahmā to the stationary beings, all are the paśus (bound livestock) of the trident-bearing Lord; they are called paśus because they are under the sway of saṃsāra. Because of his lordship over them, the Lord — indeed Śiva — is known as Paśupati (lord of the bound). Mahāśvara has bound the paśus from Brahmā to the pillar. By these bonds (pāśa), the Lord God himself causes them to perform their work. He alone is their liberator, when properly worshipped with devotion." Thus have similar statements from the Skānda, Śaiva and Kūrma Purāṇas been cited. Here, by the statement "from Brahmā to the stationary" it is explained that in the mantra "Eṣāmīśe" (the lord of these), the two-footed and four-footed [beings] serve as indicative of the entire manifest world, composed of conscious and inert beings.

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | hari-keshaya | iti | hari-keshaya | upavitine | iti | upa-vitine | pushtanam | pataye | namah || 5 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Harikeshaya'—one whose hair is blue (black), free from greying. 'Upavitine'—to Rudra who wears the sacred thread (yajnopavita) for the sake of auspiciousness. 'Pushtanam pataye'—salutation to the Master of men who are full of complete qualities. (5)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo harikeshaya'—salutation to the one with blue hair, who is ever young. 'Upavitine'—to the wearer of the sacred thread; it means for the sake of auspicious conduct. 'Pushtanam pataye namah'—to the Master of those who are full of complete qualities. Or: 'pushtayah' refers to nourishment. There are ten types of nourishment: nourishment of speech, of knowledge, of the body and senses, of house and field, of wealth and grain, of progeny, of cattle, of the village, of Dharma, and of occult powers like Anima etc. Salutation to the protector of those. (5)

He speaks the sixth Yajus:
Salutation to the weapon of existence, salutation to the lord of the worlds.

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । भ॒वस्य॑ । हे॒त्यै । जग॑ताम् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | bhavasya | hetyai | jagatam | pataye | namah || 6 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Bhavah' is worldly existence (Samsara); 'heti' is its weapon. Salutation be to Rudra who cuts (destroys) Samsara. Salutation be to Rudra who is the protector of the worlds. (6)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo bhavasya hetyai'. 'Bhavah' is Samsara; 'heti' is the weapon; to the cutter of Samsara. Or: to the form of that injury which is the cause of Samsara, salutation to him in that form. [Grammatical note on the word 'heti']. 'Jagatam pataye namah'—to the Lord of the worlds like the Earth, etc. [Grammatical note on the word 'jagatam'].
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
80                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

---

    "Salutation to the weapon (heti) of Bhava (Śiva the cause of existence); salutation to the Lord of the worlds (jagatāṃ pataye namaḥ)."

Thus through five mantras, the Lord has been saluted with demonstrations of his lordship over certain specific things such as the directions and so forth. Now, since it is impossible to salute him individually in respect of his sovereignty over each and every thing, the sixth mantra is employed to salute him as the "Lord of the worlds" (jagatpati): "Namo bhavasya iti" — Bhava means saṃsāra (the cycle of existence); the heti (weapon) which cuts it — meaning he is the one who severs the stream of worldly existence (saṃsāravṛtta). He is the lord (pati) of all the worlds (jagatāṃ), both moving and unmoving. To him may there be salutation. Although the lordship over the entire world, from Brahmā to the stationary, was already stated by [the mantra] "Paśūnāṃ pataye namaḥ" — [the difference is that] there, it was stated in the sense of his being the cause of the bondage of saṃsāra; here, the distinction is that he is the liberator from saṃsāra. Both aspects, however, are attributes of Brahman.

    "Salutation to Rudra (the one who dispels affliction), to the one who stretches the bow (ātatāvitane), to the lord of the fields (kṣetrāṇāṃ pataye namaḥ)."

Now, to further support what has already been said about his lordship over the world, and in order to salute the Blessed Lord again through [the demonstration of] his sovereignty over various particular things — in the manner of a persistent supplicant walking again and again — the commentary begins the seventh [mantra] and those following: "Namo Rudrāya iti" — Rudra is he who dispels (drāvaka) the suffering of saṃsāra; hence, because he protects (avati, i.e., rakṣati) with the stretched (ātatena) bow, he is Ātatāvitin. Kṣetrāṇi means bodies (śarīrāṇi), as the Smṛti says: "This body, O son of Kuntī, is called the kṣetra (the field)" — he who is the lord of these bodies is kṣetrāṇāṃ patiḥ, to him may there be salutation. Just as in ordinary life the inhabitants of a land are protected by the overlord of that land —

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
८०                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

    नमो भवस्य हेत्यै जगतां पतये नम: ॥

   इत्थं पञ्चभिर्मन्त्रैदिंगादिकतिपयवस्त्वाधिपत्यप्रदर्शनपूर्व-
कमीश्वरो नमस्कृत:; इदानीं प्रतिवस्त्वाघिपत्यवचनपूर्वक-
नमस्कारस्याशक्यतया जगत्पतित्वेन तं नमस्कर्तुं षष्ठो मन्त्र:
भयुक:— नमो भवस्येति । भव: संसार:, तस्य च्छेद्ने
हेति: आयुघम्, संसारवृत्तस्य च्छेत्तेत्यर्थ: । स्थावरजङ्गमानां
लोकानां स्वामी जगतां पति:, तस्मै नम: । यद्यपि
'पशूनां पतये नम:' इत्यनैव ब्रह्मादिस्थावरान्तसकलजग-
त्पतित्वमुक्तकम्, तथापि संसारपाशबन्धहेतुत्वेन तदुक्तकम्;
अत्र संसारमोचकत्वेनेति भेद:, उभयोरपि ब्रह्मघर्मत्वात् ॥

    नमो रुद्रायाततावितने क्षेत्राणां पतये
नम: ॥

   अथ उक्तमेव जगत्पतित्वसुपलपादयितुं श्रमाबधि घाव-
न्न्यायेन पुनरपि तत्तद्वस्त्वाघिपत्येन भगवन्तं नमस्कर्तुमारभे
सप्तमादिभि:— नमो रुद्रायेति । रुद्र: संसारदु:खद्रावक:,
अत एव आत्वज्येन धनुषा अवति रक्षतीति आततावितने;
क्षेत्राणि शरीराणि, 'इदं शरीरं कौन्तेय क्षेत्रमित्यभिधीयते'
इति स्मृते:, तेषां स्वामी क्षेत्राणां पति:, तस्मै नम: इत्य-
र्थ: । लोके हि देशाधिपतिकलत्रपद्रेभ्यो देशवासिनो रक्षित,

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । रु॒द्राय॑ । आ॒त॒ता॒विने᳚ । क्षेत्रा॑णाम् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | rudraya | atatavine | iti | ata-tavine | kshetranam | pataye | namah || 7 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Atatavi' is he who protects (avati) with an 'atata'—a drawn bow. Salutation be to that Rudra. Salutation be to Rudra who is the protector of fields (kshetra). (7)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo rudraya'—explained already. 'Atatavine'—he who protects the worlds with an 'atata'—a bow on which the string is fixed; or he who holds such a bow. [Grammatical note on the word 'atatavine']. 'Kshetranam pataye namah'—to the protector or Master of the 'kshetras' (bodies). For instance:
"The Lord, O Arjuna, resides in the heart-region of all beings, making all beings revolve by his power (maya) as if mounted on a machine."
Or: to the Master of holy places (punya-kshetra) like Avimukta (Kashi) etc. Or: to him in the form of the protector of holy fields. The idea is: salutation to the cutter and promoter of Samsara. (7)

He speaks the eighth Yajus:
Salutation to the charioteer, to the one who cannot be harmed, salutation to the lord of forests.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
80                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

---

    "Salutation to the weapon (heti) of Bhava (Śiva the cause of existence); salutation to the Lord of the worlds (jagatāṃ pataye namaḥ)."

Thus through five mantras, the Lord has been saluted with demonstrations of his lordship over certain specific things such as the directions and so forth. Now, since it is impossible to salute him individually in respect of his sovereignty over each and every thing, the sixth mantra is employed to salute him as the "Lord of the worlds" (jagatpati): "Namo bhavasya iti" — Bhava means saṃsāra (the cycle of existence); the heti (weapon) which cuts it — meaning he is the one who severs the stream of worldly existence (saṃsāravṛtta). He is the lord (pati) of all the worlds (jagatāṃ), both moving and unmoving. To him may there be salutation. Although the lordship over the entire world, from Brahmā to the stationary, was already stated by [the mantra] "Paśūnāṃ pataye namaḥ" — [the difference is that] there, it was stated in the sense of his being the cause of the bondage of saṃsāra; here, the distinction is that he is the liberator from saṃsāra. Both aspects, however, are attributes of Brahman.

    "Salutation to Rudra (the one who dispels affliction), to the one who stretches the bow (ātatāvitane), to the lord of the fields (kṣetrāṇāṃ pataye namaḥ)."

Now, to further support what has already been said about his lordship over the world, and in order to salute the Blessed Lord again through [the demonstration of] his sovereignty over various particular things — in the manner of a persistent supplicant walking again and again — the commentary begins the seventh [mantra] and those following: "Namo Rudrāya iti" — Rudra is he who dispels (drāvaka) the suffering of saṃsāra; hence, because he protects (avati, i.e., rakṣati) with the stretched (ātatena) bow, he is Ātatāvitin. Kṣetrāṇi means bodies (śarīrāṇi), as the Smṛti says: "This body, O son of Kuntī, is called the kṣetra (the field)" — he who is the lord of these bodies is kṣetrāṇāṃ patiḥ, to him may there be salutation. Just as in ordinary life the inhabitants of a land are protected by the overlord of that land —

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | rohitaya | sthapataye | vrikshanam | pataye | namah || 9 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Rohitah' is the red-colored one. 'Sthapatih' is the Chief (Prabhu). Salutation be to that Rudra. Salutation to Rudra who is the protector of trees (vriksha). (9)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo rohitaya'—to the red-colored one. Someone says: the syllable 'Ro' refers to the form of speech; he who is beneficial (hita) for that, or residing in that. 'Sthapataye'—to the cutter (khandaka). Or: the creator of houses, etc. Another says: he who stays (sthata) everywhere and is the protector (pata) of everything. Salutation to the Lord of trees (vriksha). [Trees are those which he establishes and cuts]. (9)

He speaks the tenth Yajus:
Salutation to the minister, to the merchant, salutation to the lord of thickets.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
82                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam
---

[Mantra 9:]
Obeisance to Rohita (the red-colored one), to the Sthapati (the lord-who-stands), to the Lord of trees — obeisance.

Now the ninth [commentary]: — "Namo rohitaya" (Obeisance to Rohita). "Rohita" means the one of red (lohita) color; "sthapati" means the lord who both stands (sthata) and protects (pata) — [this is] a word of the prshodaradi class (a grammatical class of irregularly formed words). Obeisance to such a one who is the Lord and master of trees — this is the meaning. Here the word "sthapati" conveys the reason for His lordship over trees: it is fitting that He who dwells within trees and protects them should be their lord. The word "rohita" (red) befits the Supreme Lord as the presiding deity of trees, for it will be stated later: "They who in trees are yellow-green, blue-necked, and deep red." Thus ends the ninth.

[Mantra 10:]
Obeisance to the wise counsellor (mantrin), to the merchant (vanija), to the Lord of thickets — obeisance.

Now the tenth [commentary]: — "Mantrine" (to the wise counsellor). The dense growths of forest-shrubs and the like are called "kakshas" (thickets); obeisance to their Lord — this is the primary meaning. But [one might object]: How can the Lord exercise sovereignty over what is exceedingly dense and impenetrable? Addressing this doubt about the impenetrability [of thickets], the text specifies with the word "vanijaya" (to the merchant): A body of merchants is called "vanija"; just as a merchant comprehends and makes clear all the dealings of a country, so too this [Lord] comprehends all things — nothing is impenetrable to Him — such is the intent. Another reason for His lordship over thickets is given

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
८२                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

नमो रोहिताय स्थपतये वृक्षाणां पतये
नमः ॥

अथ नवमः— नमो रोहितायेति । लोहितवर्णो रो-
हितः, स्थाता चाक्षो पाता चेति स्थपतिः, पृषोदरादिः;
एवंभूताय वृक्षाणां पतये स्वामिने नमः इत्यर्थः । अत्र
स्थपतिशब्देन वृक्षस्वामित्वे हेतुरुच्यते, वृक्षेषु स्थित्वा
वृक्षान्पालयतस्तत्स्वामित्वौचित्यात् । रोहितशब्देन वृक्षाधि-
ष्ठातुः परमेश्वरस्य स्वरूपयुक्तं भवति, 'ये वृक्षेषु सस्पिञ्जरा
नीलाग्रीवा विलोहिताः' इति हि वक्ष्यते । इति नवमः ॥

नमो मन्त्रिणे वाणिजाय कक्षाणां प-
तये नमः ॥

अथ दशमः— मन्त्रिण इति । वनगुल्मादयोऽतिगहन-
पदार्थाः कक्षाः तेषां स्वामी कक्षाणां पतिः, तस्मै नमः इति
प्रथममन्वयः; नतु अतिगहनेषु कथमीश्वरस्याधिपत्यं स्या-
त्? दुरवगाह्त्वादित्याशङ्कय विशिनष्टि— वाणिजायेति।
वणिजां समूहो वाणिजः, यथा वणिक् सर्वजनपद्व्यवहारा-
न्परिच्छिद्य विशदीकरोति, तथा अयमपि सकलवस्तुजाव-
मिति, नास्य किञ्चिदुरवगाहमस्तीति भावः । केषुवि-

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । म॒न्त्रिणे॑ । वा॒णि॒जाय॑ । कक्षा॑णाम् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ म॒न्त्रिणे॑ । म॒न्त्रिणे॑ वाणि॒जाय॑ । वाणि॒जाय॒ कक्षा॑णाम् । कक्षा॑णा॒-म्पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | mantrine | vanijaya | kakshanam | pataye | namah || 10 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Mantri' is one skilled in deliberation/counsel (mantra-alochana) in the royal assembly. He is 'vanijah' as he exists in the form of the master of merchants. Salutation be to that Rudra. To the protector of thickets (kaksha), which are bushes etc. found in the forest. (10)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo mantrine'—possessing the 'mantras' which are the five Brahmans. Or: 'mantra' is secret speech...
...

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | bhuvantaye | varivah-kritaya | iti | varivah-kritaya | oushadhinam | pataye | namah || 11 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Bhuvanti' is he who extends (tanoti) the earth (bhuvam). Salutation be to that Rudra. 'Varivah' is wealth; its maker is 'varivaskrita'. Salutation be to that Rudra. Salutation to Rudra who is the protector of medicinal plants (oushadhi), both of the village and the forest. (11)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo bhuvantaye'. He in whom the universe exists (bhavati) as the material cause, predominating in being, is 'bhuvantah'. Or: he who exists (bhavanti) always and everywhere. [Grammatical note]. Or: 'bhuvanti' is he who extends (tanoti) the earth by being the support for grass, bushes, herbs, and trees. 'Varivaskritaya'—'varivah' is wealth; its maker is 'varivaskara'. [Grammatical note]. Or: he who is connected as a helper to those who earn wealth. Another says: 'varivah' is service/worship; its performers are 'varivaskritah'—the devotees; he is 'varivaskrita' for them. It means he is 'bhakta-vatsala' (fond of his devotees). Or: to him who has made his residence (vasa) in water (vari)—to him in the form of Vishnu or Varuna. [Grammatical note]. Salutation to the Lord of medicinal plants (oushadhi)—to the Master of both village and forest varieties; in the form of Soma (the moon), being the protector of those produced in the form of beings. 'Oshadhayah' are those which grow/stay until they ripen (osha-antam dhiyante). (11)
...

sanskrit.krama: नम॑ उ॒च्चैर्घो॑षाय । उ॒च्चैर्घो॑षायाक्र॒न्दय॑ते । आ॒क्र॒न्दय॑ते पत्ती॒नाम् । पत्ती॒ना-म्पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | uchaih-ghoshaya | akrandayate | pattinam | pataye | namah || 12 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Uchairghoshah' is he whose sound (ghosha) or noise is loud/raised (uchrita) during war. 'Akrandayate'—to him who makes enemies wail/cry (rodayita). Salutation be to that Rudra. Salutation to the protector of 'pattis'—the warriors who walk on foot (foot-soldiers) are called 'pattis'. (12)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah uchairghoshaya'—to him with a loud sound or loud hymns of praise. Or to him in the form of loud sounds like clouds, etc. 'Akrandayate'—to him who causes 'akrandana'—the loud shouting/calling in war. Or to him who makes enemies cry everywhere. It means the one who brings victory in wars. 'Pattinam pataye namah':
"One chariot, one elephant, five foot-soldiers, and three horses—these together are called a 'patti'."
To their Master. Or: to the Lord of foot-soldiers; to him who is capable of conquering all three worlds himself in the form of a foot-soldier. (12)

He speaks the thirteenth Yajus:
Salutation to the one who surrounds the whole (army), to the one who runs, salutation to the lord of the good/attendants.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
84                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam
---

Here the word "suvantaya" expresses the reason for His lordship over herbs: since the Supreme Lord is the master of the three worlds consisting of the entire earth-sphere, His lordship over herbs — which are transformations of the earth — also follows from that same [fact]. By the word "varivaskritaya," expressing His dwelling in the body of devotees, the being-eaten-by-the-Lord aspect is revealed — as the Lord Himself says through the mouth of [scripture], conveying His role as the enjoyer: "I am not pleased in the same way by fire-sacrifice, by the oblation offered by the sacrificer at the time of revelation, as I am by [the worship of] one who moves about freely and joyfully, satisfied [with simple service], marked with the signs of his own deeds." By that [status as] enjoyer of the fruit of herbs, the Supreme Lord's lordship over herbs is indicated — on the principle that it is appropriate that the master of something be the enjoyer of its fruit. Thus it is stated. Contemplating precisely this exalted quality of being food-for-the-Lord, [scripture] declares — through Shruti, Smriti, and other sources — that food prepared by devotees is indeed food prepared [for Him], and not that prepared by others: "The one who craves inwardly [the food offered] with the heart, the Supreme One grasps with the mind; he grasps the taste with the tongue; the exclusive sinner becomes the exclusive eater" — such Shruti passages; and from the Parashara Purana: "He who always craves [the food offered] inwardly with the heart and with devotion, grasps it with his tongue as taste-filled, immortal water. But those who do not offer [food] inwardly to Bhava together with Bhavani — they grasp only excrement with the tongue, without doubt." Thus ends the eleventh.

[Mantra 12:]
Obeisance to the one of loud cry, to the one who makes enemies weep, to the Lord of foot-soldiers — obeisance.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
८४                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

ष्वादीनाम्, पितिः स्वामी, तस्मै नमः इत्यर्थः । अत्र
सुवंतय इत्यनेन ओषधिस्वामित्वे हेतुरुच्यते, सकलभूवल-
यत्रयस्थया तत्स्वामिनः परमेश्वरस्य भूविकारौषधिस्वामित्-
वमापि तत एव प्रास्नेः; वारिवस्कृतायेत्यनेन च भक्तकाय-
स्थत्वोक्त्या 'नाहं तथाग्निं यजमानह्विर्विदतानि च्योतनुत-
युतमदनहुतसुखुखेन। यद्राक्षणस्य सुखतश्वरतोऽनुघासं तुष्टस्य
मत्यविह्वैर्निजकमेणांकैः' इति भगवतोक्तं सत्मुखेन भग-
वतो भोक्तृत्वमाविष्कृतम् । तेन चौषधिफलभोक्तृत्वेनापि
परमेश्वरस्यौषधिस्वामित्वं  तत्फलभोक्तुस्तस्वासित्वौचित्यात्
इत्युक्तं भवति । एवं परमेश्वरभोजनरूपत्वातिशयमभिप्रेत्येव
उपासकैः कृतमेव भे भोजनं नान्ये: कृतमिति श्रुतिस्मृत्यादि-
भिराज्ञायते— 'अन्तरिच्छति तं जनो हृदं परमो मनीषया
गृह्णाति जिह्वया रसं केवलाघो भवति केवलादी' इत्याधा-
श्रुतयः, 'अन्तरिच्छति यो हृदं सदा वन्दं मनीष्या। गृह्णाति
जिह्वया सोऽस्नं रसपूर्णामृतोद्कम् ।  अन्तरनेच्छिन्ते ये हृदं
भवान्या सहितं भवम् ।  पुरीषमेव गृह्णन्ति जिह्वया ते न
संशयः' इत्यादिपराशरपुराणवचनानि । इत्येकादशः ॥

नम उच्चैर्घोषायाक्रन्दयते पत्तीनां प-
तये नमः ॥

|| This Second Anuvaka is completed. ||

translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    85
---

Now the twelfth [commentary]: — "Nama uccairghoshaya" (Obeisance to the one of loud roar). "Uccairghoshah" means one whose roar or battle-cry in wars and the like is exceedingly loud; obeisance to Him; "akrandayate" means one who causes the enemies to weep, one who destroys them — that is the import; "patthinam" means of foot-soldiers (padatin), their Lord (svamin) — obeisance — this is the meaning. Here, having established through the phrase "Lord of foot-soldiers" that the Lord's valor is relative to those who go on foot and similar [warriors], the text, in order to dispel [the implication that He depends on them], states "akrandate" (He who makes [enemies] cry) to show that He Himself has the capacity to destroy enemies. Even in destroying enemies Himself, He has no need of weapons — it is by His roar alone that [the destruction happens] — and in order to show this, the text says "uccairghoshaya" (of loud roar). Accordingly, [His] carrying of weapons and the like is merely ornamental; [this is shown by] the fact that He destroyed the cities of the demons, Manmatha (the god of love), Yama (the god of death), and others, by merely a glance and a laugh — without dependence [on weapons] — such is the import. Thus ends the twelfth.

[Mantra 13:]
Obeisance to the one who has pervaded everything, to the one who runs [to protect devotees], to the Lord of sattvic beings — obeisance.

Now the thirteenth [commentary]: — "Namah kritsnavitaya" (Obeisance to the all-pervader). "Kritsnavitah" means one by whom the entire (kritsna) world is pervaded (vita) — in the sense of being the substratum and as the inner controller (antaryamin); obeisance to Him; "dhavate" means He who runs swiftly behind [His devotees] to protect them, following after them, as the saying goes: "Like a cow [running] after her calf, the Lord of Gauri (Shiva) follows the one who runs [to Him]"; "sattvanam" means of sattvic beings (those with the quality of purity and goodness), obeisance to the Lord (svamin). Here the reason for His lordship over sattvic beings is stated by the word "dhavate" — for it is fitting that He who runs behind the sattvic ones to protect them, like a cow following her calf, should be their lord. The word "kritsnavitaya" (all-pervading) further supports that very running — since the all-pervading [Lord] is able to run after devotees from all directions. This very all-pervasiveness as the Lord's capacity to protect from everywhere is heard in the Grihya-sutra (household ritual text) of the Vajasaneyins (followers of the Yajurveda): "When one is about to enter the house, one recites [this mantra]: 'Obeisance to Rudra who sits in the house; may He bring us across auspiciously.' Going along a path, one recites: 'Obeisance to Rudra who sits on the path; may He bring us across auspiciously.' About to enter a forest, one recites: 'Obeisance to Rudra who sits in the forest; may He bring us across auspiciously.' Therefore, whatever one does anywhere, one should always say "Obeisance to Rudra" — for Rudra dwells everywhere, as the Shruti declares." This entire second anuvaka (chapter section) is one mantra. Its seer (rishi) is Manduka; its meter is Mahagayatri; its deity is Sri Rudra. [The meditation verse (dhyana) is:] "Meditate on Hara (Shiva) adorned with all limbs, bearing the moon and the Ganga, at the root of the wish-fulfilling tree (kalpavriksha), together with Uma, in deep absorption." This mantra is pre-eminent in bestowing the fruits of destruction of enemies, subjugation, and lordly power (aishvarya).

Thus ends the second anuvaka.

[decorative floral ornament]

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    ८५
---

अथ द्वादशः— नम उच्चैर्घोषायेति । युद्धादिषु उच्चैरु-
च्छतो घोषो यस्य उच्चैर्घोषः, तस्मै; आक्रन्द्यते वैरिणां
रोदयित्रे, तन्नाशकायेति यावत्; पत्तीनां पदातिनाम्,
स्वामिने नमः इत्यर्थः । अत्र पत्तीनां पतये इत्यनेन पदा-
दिषापेक्षमीश्वरस्य शौर्यमिति प्राप्ते तद्विवारणाय स्वयमेव
शत्रुनाशने सामर्थ्ये दर्शितुम् आक्रन्दते इत्युक्तम् ।
स्वयं शत्रुनाशनेऽपि नायुधापेक्षा, किं तु हुंकारमात्रेण
तदिति दर्शयितुम् उच्चैर्घोषाय इत्युक्तम् । तथा च आयु-
धपरिधारादिकमलङ्कारमात्रम्, हासबीक्षणखादिभिसिरपुर-
मन्मथयमादीन् नाशितवत्तत्त्वदपेक्षाविरहादिति भावः । इति
द्वादशः ॥

नमः कृत्स्नवीताय धावते सत्त्वनां
पतये नमः ॥

अथ त्रयोदशः— नमः कृत्स्नवीतायेति । कृत्स्नं ज-
गत् वीतम् अधिष्ठानतया अन्तर्यामितया च व्याप्तं येन, स
कृत्स्नवीतः, तस्मै; धावते भक्तान् रक्षितुं घृष्टतोऽनुमजते,
'वत्सं गौरिव गौरीशो धावन्तमतुषावति' इति वचनात्;
सत्त्वनां सात्विकानाम्, पतये स्वामिने नमः । अत्र स-

नमः॑ ककु॒भाय॑ । ककु॒भाय॑ निष॒ङ्गिणे᳚ । निष॒ङ्गिणे᳚ स्ते॒नाना᳚म् । स्ते॒नानां॒ पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | kakubhaya | nishangine | iti | ni-sangine | stenanam | pataye | namah || 2 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Kakubhaya'—to him who is like a peak (kakubh), meaning the foremost/chief. 'Nishangine'—to him with a sword in his hand. 'Stenanam pataye'—salutation to the protector of 'stenas' (secret thieves). Rudra, like an actor, assumes various roles as a play (leela). Or: because he is the self of the whole world, in whatever form (people) exist anywhere, he is in that very form...
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    88
---

[Mantra 4:]
Obeisance to the one who deceives, to the one who thoroughly deceives, to the Lord of sneaky pilferers (stayus) — obeisance.

Now the fourth [commentary]: — "Namo vanchata" (Obeisance to the deceiver). Those who, while being considered as one's own people, having gained the trust of others, steal their goods — they are the stayavah (sneaky pilferers); obeisance to their Lord. In order to establish the Lord's lordship over sneaky pilferers, the two qualifying epithets "vanchate" (deceiver) and "parivanchate" (thorough deceiver) are given: one who, while appearing as a trustworthy person in others' affairs and in transactions of buying and selling of goods belonging to others, steals something here and there — he is called a "vanchashrita" (partial deceiver); one who commits deception in all transactions is called a "parivanchashrita" (complete deceiver); [the Lord appears in] such forms — this is the meaning. Thus ends the fourth.

[Mantra 5:]
Obeisance to the one who roams quietly, to the one who roams on all sides, to the Lord of forest-dwellers (aranyanas) — obeisance.

Now the fifth [commentary]: — "Namo nicerava" (Obeisance to the quiet roamer). Those who live in forests are the aranyavasi (forest-dwellers), and of these aranyanahs, obeisance to their Lord — this is what is being established. [The text] supports this very point with: "Namo nicerave paricara" — one who by nature roams quietly (nicerava) [in forests], surpassing [in stealth] even the forest-dwelling thieves; and "paricara" — one who roams in all directions to steal [more than them]; the idea being that since He is supreme among them by virtue of being a more skilled roamer than even those [forest thieves], it is fitting that He be their lord. Thus ends the fifth.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
८८                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

स्यापरिहार्यत्वादिल्याशङ्कथ, भक्तापराधसह्नस्वभावे भगवति
नेयं शङ्केत्याह— सहमानायेति । इति प्रथमः ॥

नमः ककुभाय निषङ्गिणे स्तेनानां पत-
ये नमः ॥

अथ द्वितीयः— नमः ककुभायेति । ककुभाय महते,
निषङ्गिणे खड्गवते, स्तेनानां गुप्तचोराणां स्वामिने नमः ।
अत्र ककुभशब्दवाच्यमहत्त्वस्य प्रतियोग्याकाङ्कायाम्, स्व-
वाक्योपात्तस्तेनानासमेव तत्प्रतियोगित्वेलसान्वयः; तथा च स्ते-
नेषु महत्त्वं स्वेनस्वामित्वे हेतुः ककुभाय इत्यनेन चोच्यते ।
निषङ्गिणे इत्यनेन स्तेनकियापेक्षितोपकरणसंपत्तिः प्रदर्श्य-
ते । इति द्वितीयः ॥

नमो निषङ्गिण इषुधिमते तस्कराणां
पतये नमः ॥

अथ तृतीयः— नमो निषङ्गिण इति । धनुषि संबाहुं
हस्ते धृतो बाणः निषङ्गः, पृष्ठे बद्धो वाणाधारः इषुधिः,
तदुभययुक्ताय; तस्कराणां प्रकाशचोराणां स्वामिते नमः ।
अत्र तस्कराधिपत्सानुगुणरूपप्रदर्शनार्थत्वेन विशेषणं संगय-
यितव्ये । इति तृतीयः ॥

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । नि॒-ष॒ङ्गिणे᳚ । इ॒षु॒धि-म॑ते । तस्क॑राणाम् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ निष॒ङ्गिण॑ । निष॒ङ्गिण॑ इषुधि॒मते॑ । इषुधि॒मते॒ तस्क॑राणाम् । तस्क॑राणां॒ पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | nishangine | ishudhimate | iti | ishu-dhi-mate | taskaranam | pataye | namah || 3 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Nishanga' is an arrow held in the hand to be placed on the bow. 'Ishudhi' is the container of arrows bound on the back. Salutation to him who has both. 'Taskarah' are manifest (open) thieves; salutation to their protector. (3)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo nishangine'—to the one having constant attachment (sakti) to his devotees. 'Ishudhimate'—'nishanga' and 'ishudhi' have been explained; to him who has both. The word 'nishangi' is mentioned again to establish the possession of both. Or: 'nishanga' is extreme attachment. Carrying the desire for theft and the quiver. 'Taskaranam pataye namah'—those who reside in forests etc. and steal openly, like those who rob caravans, etc., are 'taskaras'. [Grammatical note on 'taskara']. (3)
...

अथ द्वितीयः— नमः ककुभायेति । ककुभाय महते,
निषङ्गिणे खड्गवते, स्तेनानां गुप्तचोराणां स्वामिने नमः ।
अत्र ककुभशब्दवाच्यमहत्त्वस्य प्रतियोग्याकाङ्कायाम्, स्व-
वाक्योपात्तस्तेनानासमेव तत्प्रतियोगित्वेलसान्वयः; तथा च स्ते-
नेषु महत्त्वं स्वेनस्वामित्वे हेतुः ककुभाय इत्यनेन चोच्यते ।
निषङ्गिणे इत्यनेन स्तेनकियापेक्षितोपकरणसंपत्तिः प्रदर्श्य-
ते । इति द्वितीयः ॥

नमो निषङ्गिण इषुधिमते तस्कराणां
पतये नमः ॥

अथ तृतीयः— नमो निषङ्गिण इति । धनुषि संबाहुं
हस्ते धृतो बाणः निषङ्गः, पृष्ठे बद्धो वाणाधारः इषुधिः,
तदुभययुक्ताय; तस्कराणां प्रकाशचोराणां स्वामिते नमः ।
अत्र तस्कराधिपत्सानुगुणरूपप्रदर्शनार्थत्वेन विशेषणं संगय-
यितव्ये । इति तृतीयः ॥

sanskrit.pada: नमो॒ । वञ्च॑ते । प॒रि॒-वञ्च॑ते । स्ता॒यू॒नाम् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो॒ वञ्च॑ते । वञ्च॑ते परिवञ्च॑ते । परि॒वञ्च॑ते स्तायू॒नाम् । स्तायू॒नां॒ पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | vanchate | pari-vanchate | stayunam | pataye | namah || 4 ||

Sayana's Commentary: Having become a trusted person of the Master, and in his dealings like buying and selling, stealing some wealth whenever possible—this is 'vanchana' (cheating). Deception in all dealings is 'parivanchana'. Salutation to the one in both those forms. Secret thieves are of two types: those who come from a distance and steal wealth by opening doors at night without being known are 'stenas'; those who are known to the owner (insiders) and steal either at night or by day are 'stayavah' (stayus). Salutation to the protector of those 'stayus'. (4)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo vanchate'—to the one whose nature is to deceive or cheat. 'Parivanchate'—to him who goes everywhere deceiving and is not obstructed by anyone. 'Stayunam pataye namah'—'stayus' are those who walk in disguise and steal wealth, such as those in the guise of false holy men. As seen in: "He gave it to him and not to the thief (taya)." [Grammatical note on the word 'stayu'].

He speaks the fifth Yajus:
Salutation to the one who moves about secretly, to the one who moves about in public, salutation to the lord of forest-thieves.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
90                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam
---

[Mantra 6:]
Obeisance to those who protect themselves with a srkava (a mechanical device), to those who desire to kill, to the Lord of grain-thieves (sushnatam) — obeisance.

Now the sixth [commentary]: — "Namah srkavibhya" (Obeisance to those with the srkava). "Srkavinah" means those who protect themselves with a mechanical device called srkava; "jighamsamtah" means those who desire to kill living beings; obeisance to the one of both these forms; "sushnatam" means the Lord of grain-thieves who steal grain from fields (kedara) and the like — obeisance. Here also the two qualifying epithets [serve to establish the reason for lordship over grain-thieves]: the lordship of a [person over] grain-thieves at a certain time belongs to one who is both skilled in protecting grain and who desires to oppose [thieves]. The plural [form] is Vedic usage (chandasa); otherwise there would be no [grammatical] relation between the qualifying words and what they qualify. The same applies to the next mantra also. Thus ends the sixth.

[Mantra 7:]
Obeisance to those with swords, to the night-wanderers, to the Lord of predatory thieves (prakuntanah) — obeisance.

Now the seventh [commentary]: — "Namo'simanjya" (Obeisance to the sword-bearers). "Asimantas" means sword-bearers (those carrying swords); "naktamcharantah" means night-wanderers (those who roam at night) — night-robbers; obeisance to such a form. "Prakuntah" means thieves who steal while [fleeing] — obeisance to their Lord. Here the two qualifying epithets [are given] to show the form appropriate to lordship over predatory thieves. Thus ends the seventh.

[Mantra 8:]
Obeisance to the turban-wearer (ushnishthin), to the mountain-roamer, to the Lord of land-thieves (kulunjchas) — obeisance.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    ८९
---

नमो वञ्चते परिवञ्चते स्तायूनां पतये
नमः ॥

अथ चतुर्थः— नमो वञ्चत इति । ये स्वकीया एव
भूत्वा अन्येर्ज्ञाताः सन्तः द्रव्यमपहरन्ति, ते स्तायवः,
तेषां स्वामिने नमः । देवस्य स्तायुस्वामित्वमुपापादितुं
वञ्चते परिवञ्चत इति विशेषणे; स्वामिन भाष्ये भूत्वा
तद्दीयद्रव्यक्रयविक्रयादिव्यवहारेषु यत्र कुत्रापि यत्किञ्चित्-
नापहारी वञ्चश्रितेत्युच्यते, सर्वेष्वपि व्यवहारेषु अपह्नव-
कृत् परिवञ्चश्रितेत्युच्यते, तदरूपायेत्यर्थः । इति चतुर्थः ॥

नमो  निचेरवे  परिचरायारण्यानां
पतये नमः ॥

अथ पञ्चमः— नमो निचेरव इति । अरण्येषु वर्तमा-
नात्मोरा आरण्याः, तेषां स्वामिने नमः; एतदेवोपापाव-
शति— नमो निचेरवे परिचरायेति । आरण्यकचोरा-
पेक्षायाप्यपहारविषये नितरां संचरणशीलो निचेरुः, एवं
तदपेक्षया परितोऽपहरितुं संचरणशीलः परिचरः, ताद्श-
त्य तेषु श्रेष्ठत्वातत्तस्वामित्वं युक्तमिति भावः । इति
पञ्चमः ॥

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । नि-चे॒रवे᳚ । प॒रि॒-च॒राय॑ । अर॑ण्यानाम् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ निचे॒रवे॑ । निचे॒रवे॑ परिच॒राय॑ । परिच॒रायार॑ण्यानाम् । अर॑ण्याणां॒ पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | nicherave | paricharaya | iti | pari-charaya | aranyanam | pataye | namah || 5 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Nichera' is one who has the nature of moving about (charana-shila) constantly and carefully within the Master's house itself with the intention "when shall I steal?". 'Parichara' is one who has the nature of moving about in marketplaces, streets, gardens, etc., with the intention of stealing. Salutation to him in both those forms. 'Aranyas' are thieves who stay constantly in the forest to harass travelers on the path by stealing their wealth; salutation be to their protector. (5)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo nicherave'—to him whose nature is to move about (charana-shila) secretly or constantly. [Grammatical note].
...

---

नमः ॥

भूत्वा अन्येर्ज्ञाताः सन्तः द्रव्यमपहरन्ति, ते स्तायवः,

पेक्षायाप्यपहारविषये नितरां संचरणशीलो निचेरुः, एवं
