sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ सृका॒विभ्यः॑ । सृका॒विभ्यो॒ जिघाग्ं॑सद्भ्यः । जिघाग्ं॑सद्भ्यो मुष्ण॒ताम् । मुष्ण॒ता-म्पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | srikavibhyah | iti | srikavi-bhyah | jighahmsadbhyah | iti | jighahmsat-bhyah | mushnatam | pataye | namah || 6 ||

Sayana's Commentary: The word 'srika' refers to the thunderbolt (vajra). Those who protect (avanti) their bodies with that are 'srikavinah'. Thieves who desire to kill (jighahmsantah) living beings. Salutation to the one in both those forms. Those who, while being farmers, steal the master's grain are 'mushnantah'; salutation to their protector. (6)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah srikavibhyah'. 'Srikam' is a name for the thunderbolt. By that, any weapon is indicated. To those who possess that, or to the attendants of Rudra. [Grammatical note]. Or: to the protectors of weapons like 'srika' etc. [Grammatical note]. Another says: these are special classes of men who live by their skill with specific weapons. 'Jighahmsadbhyah'—to those who desire to kill. Others say these are special classes of men. 'Mushnatam pataye namah'—the taking away of wealth etc. is 'mosha' (theft). From the root 'mush' (to steal). [Grammatical note]. (6)

He speaks the seventh Yajus:
Salutation to the sword-bearers who move at night, salutation to the lord of those who kill and steal.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    89
---

Now the eighth [commentary]: — "Ushnishine" (to the turban-wearer). Those who steal (apahrita) land — such as houses and fields — are called "kulunjchas" (land-thieves); obeisance to their Lord. There are two types of kulunjchas: some, who live among the people while wearing turbans (ushnisha) and being [outwardly] reputable, steal fields from wicked people; others, living in mountains and forests as apparently virtuous persons, steal fields and the like from ordinary people. Both of these forms are indicated in order by the two epithets "ushnishine" (turban-wearer) and "girichara" (mountain-roamer). The idea is that it is fitting that the Lord of such kulunjchas should be of such a form.

[Mantra 9:]
Obeisance to the arrow-bearers and the bow-bearers — obeisance to you all.

Thus, through these mantras ending with "namo hiranyabahave" (obeisance to the golden-armed one) — each mantra displaying the lordship of the [Lord] over one particular thing — and through the explicit declaration "jagatam pataye namah" (obeisance to the Lord of worlds), His lordship over all creation — celebrated in the Shruti as "This is the Lord of all, this is the ruler of all worlds" — having been stated, [the text] now begins to describe the Lord's being-the-self-of-everything (sarvatmakatva) in the form of His being the very substance of each thing — beginning with "nama ishumanjya" (obeisance to the arrow-bearers) and so on. Those who carry arrows in their hands and bows for striking the worlds — the "ishumantas" and "dhanvavinah" — all who exist in the world in the form of gods, demons, and humans: obeisance to you, O you who are all these [forms]. Since the aspects (amshas) of the Supreme Lord and those who know Him are the more fierce Rudras in their multitude, and because [the address is] intended as a salutation to them, the plural form is used. And thus among these [Rudras], those who are extremely fierce (ghoratara) are the ones addressed

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
९०                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

नमः सृकाविभ्यो जिघांसद्भ्यो सु-
ष्णतां पतये नमः ॥

अथ षष्ठः— नमः सृकाविभ्य इति । सृकेण यन्त्रे-
णात्मानमवन्तीति सृकाविणः, प्राणिनो हन्तुमिच्छन्ते
जिघांसन्तः, तदुभयरूपाय; सुष्णतां केदारादिषु धान्य-
चोराणां स्वामिने नमः । अत्रापि सुष्णतां पतित्वं समये त-
रक्षणचतुरस्य तद्विरोधिनो जिषांसत एव भवतीति विशे-
षणद्वयम् । बहुवचनं छान्दसम्, अन्यथा विशेषणाविशेष्य-
भावायोगात् । एवमुत्तरमन्त्रेऽपि । इति षष्ठः ॥

नमोऽसिमञ्ज्यो नक्तञ्च वः प्रकुन्तानां
पतये नमः ॥

अथ सप्तमः— नमोऽसिमञ्ज्य इति । असिमन्तः
खड्गधारिणः, नक्तञ्चरन्तः राखिषरा:, तदूपायेत्यर्थः ।
इलैवापहरन्तश्चोराः प्रकुन्ताः, तेषां स्वामिने नमः । अत्र
प्रकृन्तस्वामित्वानुगुणरूपप्रदर्शनाय विशेषणद्वयम् । इति
सप्तमः ॥

नम उर्णिषिणे गिरिचराय कुलुञ्छा-
नां पतये नमः ॥

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । अ॒सि-म॑त्-भ्यः । न॒क्त॒म्-चर॑त्-भ्यः । प्र॒-कृ॒न्ताना᳚म् । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो॑-ऽसि॒मद्भ्यः॑ । अ॒सि॒मद्भ्यो॒ नक्त॒ञ्चर॑द्भ्यः । नक्त॒ञ्चर॑द्भ्यः प्रकृ॒न्ताना᳚म् । प्रकृ॒न्तानां॒ पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥
नम॑ उष्णी॒षिणे॑ । उष्णी॒षिणे॑ गिरिच॒राय॑ । गिरिच॒राय॑ कुलु॒ञ्चाना᳚म् । कुलु॒ञ्चानां॒ पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥
नम॒ इषु॑मद्भ्यः । इषु॑मद्भ्यो धन्वा॒विभ्यः॑ । धन्वा॒विभ्य॑श्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥
नम॑ आतन्वा॒नेभ्यः॑ । आतन्वा॒नेभ्यः॑ प्रति॒दधा॑नेभ्यः । प्रति॒दधा॑नेभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥
नम॑ आ॒यच्छ॑द्भ्यः । आ॒यच्छ॑द्भ्यो विसृ॒जद्भ्यः॑ । विसृ॒जद्भ्य॑श्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    93
---

those who ride horses, to the horse-lords — obeisance to you all.

Now the fourth [commentary]: — "Namo'syattva" (Obeisance to those who shoot). "Astana" means the [act of] the released arrow traveling up to the target — those who do this are the "asyantah" (shooters); "vedha" means the penetration of the target by the arrow that has reached it — those who do this are the "vidhayantah" (piercers). The five mantras from "namo shachinebhyah" (obeisance to the auspicious ones) up to "ashvapatibhyanca vo namah" (obeisance to the horse-lords) are clear in their meaning. This third anuvaka (chapter section), which takes the form of a single mantra composed of a collection of sub-mantras in the form of salutations on both sides and having eight parts, is [the subject of the following specifications]: Its rishi (seer) is the Lord Himself; the deity is He Himself; the meter is Brihati (a Vedic metrical form). The meditation verse (dhyana) is:

"Endowed with beauty and youth like a forest goddess; adorned above like a champaka and mango tree in full bloom. Her crown formed like a peacock displaying its twenty-eight constellations; her face resembling the spotless, full autumn moon. Clothed in freshly opened lotus garments and a fine-worked hide; adorned with a garland made of arrows held in both right and left hands. With a quiver hanging down to the navel region, like a kadamba bunch from a tree; with a bow which, though merely to be looked at, is a sight to behold. With beautiful dancing hair adorned with a girl's ornaments on the crown; a mirror-like form, shining and pure like another woman. In whose hand is placed a bow, with a single pure arrow; whose shoulder [the Lord] embraces with His left arm. Smelling again and again a fragrant flower-cluster with His hand; being slowly gazed upon [by Her] with a forest-lotus branch. Surrounded by roe-deer and lovely birds with charming sounds. The world-teacher going and returning, lovely and worthy of contemplation. Such is the Supreme Sovereign of great radiance, in the form of a forest-hunter (Kirata)." This mantra bestows the fruits of protection of one's own kingdom, conquest of enemy kingdoms, and the destruction of [dangers]. This mantra is perfected by reciting it five thousand and one times, preceded by the Prajapati [rite]. After that one becomes fit to employ it [in practice].

Thus ends the third anuvaka.

[decorative floral ornament]

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
९२                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

उभयतो नमस्कारा:, अर्थेते शान्ततरा रुद्रा ये अन्यतो नम-
स्कारा इति ॥

नम आतन्वानेभ्यः प्रतिदघानेभ्यञ्च वो
नमः ॥

अथ द्वितीयः— नम आतन्वानेभ्य इति । धनुषि
ज्यामारोपयन्तः आतन्वानाः, आततज्ये धनुषि झरार्-
खंदघानाः प्रतिदघानाः; ते च जगति यावन्तः तदूपेभ्यः
वो नमः इति पूर्ववत् ॥

नम आयच्छद्भ्यो विसृजञ्ज्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ तृतीयः— नम आयच्छत्त्व इति । ज्याकर्षणं कु-
र्वन्तः आयच्छन्तः, आकृष्य बाणान् मुञ्चन्तः विसृजन्तः;
शेषमत्र पूर्वेण द्रष्टव्यम् ॥

नमोऽस्यद्भ्यो विधद्भ्यञ्च वो नमो
नम आसीनेभ्यः शयानेभ्यञ्च वो नमो
नमः स्वपद्भ्यो जाग्रद्भ्यञ्च वो नमो नम-
स्तिष्ठद्भ्यो धावद्भ्यञ्च वो  नमो नमः स-
माभ्यः समापतिभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमो

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । अस्य॑त्-भ्यः । विध्य॑त्-भ्यः । च॒ । वः॒ । नमः॑ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो-ऽस्य॑द्भ्यः । अस्य॑द्भ्यो॒ विद्य॑द्भ्यः । विद्य॑द्भ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

sanskrit.pada: नमः॑ । आसी॑नेभ्यः । शया॑नेभ्यः । च॒ । वः॒ । नमः॑ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नम॒ आसी॑नेभ्यः । आसी॑नेभ्य-श्शया॑नेभ्यः । शया॑नेभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | asinebhyah | shayanebhyah | cha | vah | namah || 13 ||

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah asinebhyah shayanebhyashcha vo namah'. Both are well-known. [Grammatical note]. (13)

Salutation to those who are sleeping, to those who are awake, salutation to you.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    93
---

those who ride horses, to the horse-lords — obeisance to you all.

Now the fourth [commentary]: — "Namo'syattva" (Obeisance to those who shoot). "Astana" means the [act of] the released arrow traveling up to the target — those who do this are the "asyantah" (shooters); "vedha" means the penetration of the target by the arrow that has reached it — those who do this are the "vidhayantah" (piercers). The five mantras from "namo shachinebhyah" (obeisance to the auspicious ones) up to "ashvapatibhyanca vo namah" (obeisance to the horse-lords) are clear in their meaning. This third anuvaka (chapter section), which takes the form of a single mantra composed of a collection of sub-mantras in the form of salutations on both sides and having eight parts, is [the subject of the following specifications]: Its rishi (seer) is the Lord Himself; the deity is He Himself; the meter is Brihati (a Vedic metrical form). The meditation verse (dhyana) is:

"Endowed with beauty and youth like a forest goddess; adorned above like a champaka and mango tree in full bloom. Her crown formed like a peacock displaying its twenty-eight constellations; her face resembling the spotless, full autumn moon. Clothed in freshly opened lotus garments and a fine-worked hide; adorned with a garland made of arrows held in both right and left hands. With a quiver hanging down to the navel region, like a kadamba bunch from a tree; with a bow which, though merely to be looked at, is a sight to behold. With beautiful dancing hair adorned with a girl's ornaments on the crown; a mirror-like form, shining and pure like another woman. In whose hand is placed a bow, with a single pure arrow; whose shoulder [the Lord] embraces with His left arm. Smelling again and again a fragrant flower-cluster with His hand; being slowly gazed upon [by Her] with a forest-lotus branch. Surrounded by roe-deer and lovely birds with charming sounds. The world-teacher going and returning, lovely and worthy of contemplation. Such is the Supreme Sovereign of great radiance, in the form of a forest-hunter (Kirata)." This mantra bestows the fruits of protection of one's own kingdom, conquest of enemy kingdoms, and the destruction of [dangers]. This mantra is perfected by reciting it five thousand and one times, preceded by the Prajapati [rite]. After that one becomes fit to employ it [in practice].

Thus ends the third anuvaka.

[decorative floral ornament]

sanskrit.krama: नम॒स्तिष्ठ॑द्भ्यः । तिष्ठ॑द्भ्यो॒ धाव॑द्भ्यः । धाव॑द्भ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥
नमः॑ स॒भाभ्यः॑ । स॒भाभ्यः॑ स॒भाप॑तिभ्यः । स॒भाप॑तिभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | sabhabhyah | sabha-patibhyah | iti | sabha-pati-bhyah | cha | vah | namah || 16 ||

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah sabhabhyah'—to those seated in assemblies. 'Sabhapatibhyashcha vo namah'—to the Masters of the assemblies, or to the Lords of those places. [Grammatical note]. (16)

Salutation to the horses and to the lords of horses, salutation to you.

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । अश्वे᳚भ्यः । अश्व॑-पतिभ्यः । च॒ । वः॒ । नमः॑ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो॒ अश्वे᳚भ्यः । अश्वे᳚भ्यो-ऽश्व॑पतिभ्यः । अश्व॑पतिभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमः॑ । नम॒ इति॑ नमः॑ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | ashvebhyah | ashva-patibhyah | iti | ashva-pati-bhyah | cha | vah | namah || 17 ||

|| Thus ends the Third Anuvaka. ||

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo ashvebhyah'—to those with the bodies of horses. Some say: 'ashvah' means those who have no personal property (svam dhanam), the 'a-shvas'; to those who are property-less (ascetics). [Note: some read 's' and 'sh' as interchangeable]. 'Ashvapatibhyashcha vo namah'—to those who are supervisors of horses, or to the very wealthy. All these are either attendants of the God or specific play-forms of the God himself. (17)

Now for the Purashcharana of this anuvaka: having performed Krichhra penance, one achieves siddhi by five thousand chants. When there are epidemics (janapada-vyadhi), the king should have a peace-rite performed. The chanting should be done by eleven perfected Brahmins in a temple of the Lord. Five chant, five perform homa, and one Brahmin offers sesame-rice for each salutation. A lakh (100,000) of chants should be performed. When this is done, the epidemic perishes. In case of diseases of vehicles (horses, elephants, etc.), by offering eleven thousand oblations of cakes mixed with sesame paste, the common diseases of vehicles perish. For other diseases, in the place where vehicles are kept, with Apamarga rice and ghee...
...

sanskrit.krama: नम॑ आव्या॒धिनी᳚भ्यः । आव्या॒धिनी᳚भ्यो वि॒विध्य॑न्तीभ्यः । वि॒विध्य॑न्तीभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | a-vyadhinibhyah | vi-vidhyantibhyah | iti | vi-vidhyantibhyah | cha | vah | namah || 1 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Avyadhinyah' are female forms (stri-murtayah) of Rudra capable of striking from all sides. 'Vividhyantyah' are those capable of piercing in a special or diverse way. Salutation to them. (1)
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    97
---

[Mantras 8 through 16 — a continuous group:]
Obeisance to those with chariots, to those without chariots — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to those in chariots, to the chariot-lords — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the armies, to the army-leaders — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the chariot-drivers (kshattrih), to those who hold the reins (sangrahitrih) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the carpenters (taksha), to the chariot-makers (rathakara) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the potters (kulala), to the blacksmiths (karmara) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the weavers (punjishtha), to the forest-dwellers (nishada) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the arrow-makers (ishu-krit), to the bow-makers (dhanva-krit) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the hunters (mrigayu), to the dog-keepers (shvani) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the dogs (shvabhyah), to the dog-lords (shvapatibhyah) — obeisance to you all.

Now the eighth [commentary]: — "Namo rathibhyah" (Obeisance to those with chariots). "Rathinah" means those who have chariots; "arathah" means those without chariots. The nine mantras from the ninth onward, ending with "shvapatibhyah" (dog-lords), are clear in their meaning. Among these: "kshattrih" are the chariot-drivers (sarathi — charioteers); those who hold back and control the chariot are the "sangrahitarih"; there is a distinction between these two as subtypes of chariot-controllers; "karmarah" are iron-smiths (lohakara); the "punjishtha" are those who gather sand-mounds (valuka); "nishadah" are forest-hunters; those who carry nets with dogs tied to them are the "shvan" (dog-keepers).

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    चतुर्थोऽनुवाकः ॥
---

नम आव्याधिनीभ्यो विविभ्यन्तीभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ चतुर्थोऽनुवाकः— तृतीय इव चतुर्थेऽपि भगवा-
न्सर्वात्मकत्वेन नमस्क्रियते । तत्र प्रथमः— नम आव्या-
धिनीभ्य इति । आ समन्ताद्वेदुं शक्ताः शियः आव्याधि-
न्यः, विविधं वेदुं शक्ताः शियः विविभ्यन्त्यः, ताश्च जग-
ति यावन्यः सन्ति, तावदूपेभ्यो वः नमः इति पूर्ववत् ॥

नम उगणाभ्यस्तृंहतीभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ द्वितीयः— नम उगणाभ्य इति । उत्कृष्टगणाः
उगणाः सप्तमातृकाधाः, तथा हिंसितुं समर्थो दुगांधा बम-
देव्वाः हृंहन्त्यः, तदूपेभ्य इति पूर्ववत् ॥

नमो गृत्सेभ्यो गृत्सपतिभ्यञ्च वो
नमः ॥

अथ तृतीयः—नमो गृत्सेभ्य इति । गर्धनशीला गृत्साः

sanskrit.krama: नम॒ उग॑णाभ्यः । उग॑णाभ्यस्तृग्ं-ह॒तीभ्यः॑ । तृग्ं-ह॒तीभ्य॑श्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | uganabhyah | trimhatibhyah | cha | vah | namah || 2 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Uganah' are women (female forms) in excellent groups, such as the Seven Mothers (Sapta-Matrikas) and others. 'Trinhatyah' are fierce deities like Durga and others who are capable of killing (injury). Salutation to them. (2)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Uganabhyah'—to those in excellent groups (utkrishta-gana). [Grammatical note].
"By fives and by multiples, the names in order are: army-front (sena-mukha), bush (gulma), and group (gana)."—thus the definition of a group.
This has been said already. 'Trinhatibhyashcha vo namah'—to those who produce special types of violence/injury. From the root 'trihh' (to kill/strike) of the Rudh-class. [Grammatical note on the word 'trimhatibhyah']. (2)

He speaks the third Yajus:
Salutation to the greedy, and to the lords of the greedy, salutation to you.

Among these: "kshattrih" are the chariot-drivers (sarathi — charioteers); those who hold back and control the chariot are the "sangrahitarih"; there is a distinction between these two as subtypes of chariot-controllers; "karmarah" are iron-smiths (lohakara); the "punjishtha" are those who gather sand-mounds (valuka); "nishadah" are forest-hunters; those who carry nets with dogs tied to them are the "shvan" (dog-keepers).

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    चतुर्थोऽनुवाकः ॥
---

नम आव्याधिनीभ्यो विविभ्यन्तीभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ चतुर्थोऽनुवाकः— तृतीय इव चतुर्थेऽपि भगवा-
न्सर्वात्मकत्वेन नमस्क्रियते । तत्र प्रथमः— नम आव्या-
धिनीभ्य इति । आ समन्ताद्वेदुं शक्ताः शियः आव्याधि-
न्यः, विविधं वेदुं शक्ताः शियः विविभ्यन्त्यः, ताश्च जग-
ति यावन्यः सन्ति, तावदूपेभ्यो वः नमः इति पूर्ववत् ॥

नम उगणाभ्यस्तृंहतीभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ द्वितीयः— नम उगणाभ्य इति । उत्कृष्टगणाः
उगणाः सप्तमातृकाधाः, तथा हिंसितुं समर्थो दुगांधा बम-
देव्वाः हृंहन्त्यः, तदूपेभ्य इति पूर्ववत् ॥

नमो गृत्सेभ्यो गृत्सपतिभ्यञ्च वो
नमः ॥

अथ तृतीयः—नमो गृत्सेभ्य इति । गर्धनशीला गृत्साः

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ गृ॒त्सेभ्यः॑ । गृ॒त्सेभ्यो॑ गृ॒त्सप॑तिभ्यः । गृ॒त्सप॑तिभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    97
---

[Mantras 8 through 16 — a continuous group:]
Obeisance to those with chariots, to those without chariots — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to those in chariots, to the chariot-lords — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the armies, to the army-leaders — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the chariot-drivers (kshattrih), to those who hold the reins (sangrahitrih) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the carpenters (taksha), to the chariot-makers (rathakara) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the potters (kulala), to the blacksmiths (karmara) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the weavers (punjishtha), to the forest-dwellers (nishada) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the arrow-makers (ishu-krit), to the bow-makers (dhanva-krit) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the hunters (mrigayu), to the dog-keepers (shvani) — obeisance to you all.
Obeisance to the dogs (shvabhyah), to the dog-lords (shvapatibhyah) — obeisance to you all.

Now the eighth [commentary]: — "Namo rathibhyah" (Obeisance to those with chariots). "Rathinah" means those who have chariots; "arathah" means those without chariots. The nine mantras from the ninth onward, ending with "shvapatibhyah" (dog-lords), are clear in their meaning. Among these: "kshattrih" are the chariot-drivers (sarathi — charioteers); those who hold back and control the chariot are the "sangrahitarih"; there is a distinction between these two as subtypes of chariot-controllers; "karmarah" are iron-smiths (lohakara); the "punjishtha" are those who gather sand-mounds (valuka); "nishadah" are forest-hunters; those who carry nets with dogs tied to them are the "shvan" (dog-keepers).

sanskrit.krama: नमो॒ व्राते᳚भ्यः । व्राते᳚भ्यो॒ व्रात॑पतिभ्यः । व्रात॑पतिभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | vratebhyah | vratapatibhyah | iti | vrata-pati-bhyah | cha | vah | namah || 4 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Vratah' are assemblies (samghas) of various castes. 'Vratapatayah' are their protectors. Salutation to them. (4)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo vratebhyah'. To the groups of various castes, whose livelihoods are unfixed, and who live by violence/force (utsedha-jivinah). 'Vratapatibhyashcha vo namah'—and to the protectors of those companies. (4)

He speaks the fifth Yajus:
Salutation to the groups, and to the lords of groups, salutation to you.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
96                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam
---

[Note: Page 98 of the source continues from the eighth commentary of the fourth anuvaka, describing various groups among the attendants of the Lord.]

Those who are addicted to sense pleasures (vishaya-lampata); the "gritsapatayah" are their lords — the rest as before.

[Commentary on Mantras 8 onward — continued:]

Among those [listed]: the chariot-drivers (sarathi) are the kshattrih; those who hold back and control the chariot are the sangrahitarah; and between these chariot-controller types there is a sub-distinction; the "karmarah" are iron-smiths (lohakara); the "punjishtha" are those who collect piles of sand-grains (valuka); "nishadah" means forest-hunters; those who carry loops and nets bound to dogs are the shvan (dog-handlers).

[Continuing the fourth anuvaka commentary:]

The groups listed — from those with chariots (rathibhyah) to dog-lords (shvapatibhyah) — represent the various classes of beings in the manifest world, all of whom are saluted as embodiments of the Lord. The scope of these salutations covers beings from the most militarily powerful (those with chariots and armies) down to the most humble and socially marginal (hunters, dog-keepers, and even dogs themselves). This comprehensive enumeration establishes the Lord's all-pervading presence across every stratum of existence, reinforcing the central teaching that the Supreme Rudra is the inner self (antaratman) of all beings without exception.

The Shruti (Vedic scripture) declares: "Verily this whole world is Rudra — to Rudra who rules this [world] we offer obeisance." The salutation of every class of being, from warriors to craftsmen to hunters, embodies this non-dual understanding: the Lord is not merely the sovereign lord over these beings from a position of externality, but is the very self abiding within each form, whether noble or humble, whether powerful or weak.

Thus the fourth anuvaka, in its comprehensive salutation of all forms of being — from divine armies to earthly craftsmen and hunters — celebrates the totality of the Lord's immanence in creation.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
९६                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

विषयलंपटाः, तेषां पतयः गृत्सपतयः; शेषं पूर्ववत् ॥

नमो व्रातेभ्यो व्रातपतिभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ चतुर्थः— नमो व्रातेभ्य इति । नानाजातीयाः
संघाः व्राताः, तेषां पतयः व्रातपतयः ॥

नमो  गणेभ्यो  गणपतिभ्यञ्च  वो
नमः ॥

अथ पञ्चमः— नमो गणेभ्य इति । गणाः प्रमथाः,
तेषां पतयः नन्दिकेश्वरादयः ॥

नमो  विरूपेभ्यो  विश्वरूपेभ्यञ्च  वो
नमः ॥

अथ षष्ठः— नमो विरूपेभ्य इति । विरूपाः विकृत-
रूपाः, नानारूपाः विश्वरूपाः; यद्वा, ब्रह्मणो वस्तुतोऽधि-
कारत्वेन परमार्थतो विगतरूपाः,  'समारोप्यस्य रूपेण
विषयो रूपवान् भवेत्' इति न्यायेन शुक्तिकायां रजतमिव
ब्रह्मणि अध्यस्तं विश्वरूपमिति विश्वरूपाः ॥

नमो महद्भ्यः क्षुल्लकेभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ सप्तमः— नमो महह्वञ्च इति । अणिमाद्यैश्वर्यो-

sanskrit.pada: नमो॑ । ग॒णेभ्यः॑ । ग॒ण-पत॑िभ्यः । च॒ । वः॒ । नमो॒ ॥
sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ ग॒णेभ्यः॑ । ग॒णेभ्यो॑ ग॒णप॑तिभ्यः । ग॒णप॑तिभ्यश्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | ganebhyah | ganapatibhyah | iti | gana-pati-bhyah | cha | vah | namah || 5 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Ganah' are specific classes of spirits (bhuta-visheshah) who are the God's attendants. 'Ganapatayah' are their protectors. Salutation to them. (5)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo ganebhyah'. To the groups of spirits (bhuta-ganas) such as the Pramathas, who are the attendants of the God. 'Ganapatibhyashcha vo namah'—to the protectors of those groups. (5)

He speaks the sixth Yajus:
Salutation to the deformed, and to those of universal form, salutation to you.

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | mahadbhyah | iti | mahat-bhyah | kshullakebhyah | cha | vah | namah || 7 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Mahantah' are those endowed with occult powers like Anima etc. 'Kshullakah' are those without them. (7)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo mahadbhyah'. To those who are great (brihantah) in knowledge, form, and power. Regarding whom this is said:
"Those who could place the earth with the sun, moon, planets, and stars on its surface; who do not go to great amazement even after attaining the eight-fold lordship; and at whose actions Lord Shiva is always pleased."
'Kshullakebhyashcha vo namah'—to those with small knowledge, form, and power. Or: to those who live by crushing (kshodayitva), agitating, and causing destruction. Others say: 'kshulla' are those who take (laanti) hunger (kshudh) or crushing (kshoda). [Grammatical note]. (7)

He speaks the eighth Yajus:
Salutation to the chariot-mounted, and to those without chariots, salutation to you.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
96                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam
---

[Note: Page 98 of the source continues from the eighth commentary of the fourth anuvaka, describing various groups among the attendants of the Lord.]

Those who are addicted to sense pleasures (vishaya-lampata); the "gritsapatayah" are their lords — the rest as before.

[Commentary on Mantras 8 onward — continued:]

Among those [listed]: the chariot-drivers (sarathi) are the kshattrih; those who hold back and control the chariot are the sangrahitarah; and between these chariot-controller types there is a sub-distinction; the "karmarah" are iron-smiths (lohakara); the "punjishtha" are those who collect piles of sand-grains (valuka); "nishadah" means forest-hunters; those who carry loops and nets bound to dogs are the shvan (dog-handlers).

[Continuing the fourth anuvaka commentary:]

The groups listed — from those with chariots (rathibhyah) to dog-lords (shvapatibhyah) — represent the various classes of beings in the manifest world, all of whom are saluted as embodiments of the Lord. The scope of these salutations covers beings from the most militarily powerful (those with chariots and armies) down to the most humble and socially marginal (hunters, dog-keepers, and even dogs themselves). This comprehensive enumeration establishes the Lord's all-pervading presence across every stratum of existence, reinforcing the central teaching that the Supreme Rudra is the inner self (antaratman) of all beings without exception.

The Shruti (Vedic scripture) declares: "Verily this whole world is Rudra — to Rudra who rules this [world] we offer obeisance." The salutation of every class of being, from warriors to craftsmen to hunters, embodies this non-dual understanding: the Lord is not merely the sovereign lord over these beings from a position of externality, but is the very self abiding within each form, whether noble or humble, whether powerful or weak.

Thus the fourth anuvaka, in its comprehensive salutation of all forms of being — from divine armies to earthly craftsmen and hunters — celebrates the totality of the Lord's immanence in creation.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
९६                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

विषयलंपटाः, तेषां पतयः गृत्सपतयः; शेषं पूर्ववत् ॥

नमो व्रातेभ्यो व्रातपतिभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ चतुर्थः— नमो व्रातेभ्य इति । नानाजातीयाः
संघाः व्राताः, तेषां पतयः व्रातपतयः ॥

नमो  गणेभ्यो  गणपतिभ्यञ्च  वो
नमः ॥

अथ पञ्चमः— नमो गणेभ्य इति । गणाः प्रमथाः,
तेषां पतयः नन्दिकेश्वरादयः ॥

नमो  विरूपेभ्यो  विश्वरूपेभ्यञ्च  वो
नमः ॥

अथ षष्ठः— नमो विरूपेभ्य इति । विरूपाः विकृत-
रूपाः, नानारूपाः विश्वरूपाः; यद्वा, ब्रह्मणो वस्तुतोऽधि-
कारत्वेन परमार्थतो विगतरूपाः,  'समारोप्यस्य रूपेण
विषयो रूपवान् भवेत्' इति न्यायेन शुक्तिकायां रजतमिव
ब्रह्मणि अध्यस्तं विश्वरूपमिति विश्वरूपाः ॥

नमो महद्भ्यः क्षुल्लकेभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथ सप्तमः— नमो महह्वञ्च इति । अणिमाद्यैश्वर्यो-

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ र॒थिभ्यः॑ । र॒थिभ्यो॑-ऽर॒थेभ्यः॑ । अर॒थेभ्य॑श्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

[Word-for-word]: Namah | rathibhyah | iti | rathi-bhyah | arathebhyah | cha | vah | namah || 8 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Rathinah' are those mounted on a chariot. 'Arathah' are those without them. Salutation to them. (8)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo rathibhyah'—to those mounted on chariots. 'Arathebhyashcha vo namah'—to those who are the opposite, i.e., foot-soldiers. [Grammatical note]. (8)
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    97
---

[Continuing the fourth anuvaka — eighth commentary, continued:]

...those endowed with greatness through the divine powers such as anima (the power of becoming infinitesimally small); those without such powers are the "kshullaka" (small ones).

Now the eighth [commentary]: — "Namo rathibhyah" (Obeisance to those with chariots). "Rathinah" means those who possess chariots; "arathah" means those without chariots. The nine mantras from the ninth [mantra] onward, ending with "shvapatibhyah" (dog-lords), are clear in their meanings. Among these: "kshattrih" are the chariot-drivers (sarathi — charioteers); those who hold and restrain the chariot are the "sangrahitarih"; the distinction between these two subtypes of chariot-controllers is noted; "karmarah" are iron-smiths (lohakara — blacksmiths); the "punjishtha" are those who collect sand-piles (valuka — sand-workers); "nishadah" are forest-dwelling hunters; those who carry loops and leashes tied to dogs are the "shvani" (dog-handlers).

[Note on the structure of the fourth anuvaka:]

The Acharya (commentator) here completes the eighth commentary by briefly glossing the nine self-evident mantras that form a continuous litany, listing various social and occupational groups — chariot-warriors, chariot-less warriors, chariot-riders, chariot-lords, chariot-drivers, rein-holders, carpenters, chariot-makers, potters, blacksmiths, weavers, forest-hunters, arrow-makers, bow-makers, hunters, dog-keepers, dogs, and dog-lords — all of whom are saluted as manifestations of the Supreme Lord.

The theological principle underlying this section is the doctrine of sarvatmakatva (the Lord being the self of all). Just as the third anuvaka (chapter section) described the Lord in the form of warriors at various stages of combat — stringing bows, placing arrows, drawing bowstrings, releasing arrows, shooting, and piercing targets — the fourth anuvaka extends this vision to encompass all living beings in every condition of existence: seated or lying down, sleeping or waking, standing or running, assembled or leading assemblies, mounted on horses or leading horse-troops. The vision culminates in the comprehensive salutation of all occupational classes, from the noblest warriors to the humblest dog-keepers, affirming that the one Supreme Rudra dwells equally in all.

भ्यो रथकारेभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमः कुला-
लेभ्यः कर्मारेभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमः पुञ्जि-
ष्टेभ्यो निषादेभ्यञ्च वो नमो नम इषुकृ-
द्भ्यो धन्वकृद्भ्यञ्च वो नमो नमो मृगयु-

Salutation to the bird-catchers and to the fishermen, salutation to you.

Sayana's Commentary: 'Punjishtah' are the killers of flocks (punja) of birds. 'Nishadah' are the killers of fish. Salutation to them. (14)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah punjishthebhyah'—to those who bind birds. Indeed, they surround or trap (veshtayitarah) birds that gather in flocks. [Etymology]. 'Nishadebhyashcha vo namah'—to the fishermen (matsyaghatinah); they are indeed 'destroyers' (sadayitarah) in a special way, because of their desire to kill beings in groups. (14)

---

[Continuing the fourth anuvaka — eighth commentary, continued:]

...those endowed with greatness through the divine powers such as anima (the power of becoming infinitesimally small); those without such powers are the "kshullaka" (small ones).

Now the eighth [commentary]: — "Namo rathibhyah" (Obeisance to those with chariots). "Rathinah" means those who possess chariots; "arathah" means those without chariots. The nine mantras from the ninth [mantra] onward, ending with "shvapatibhyah" (dog-lords), are clear in their meanings. Among these: "kshattrih" are the chariot-drivers (sarathi — charioteers); those who hold and restrain the chariot are the "sangrahitarih"; the distinction between these two subtypes of chariot-controllers is noted; "karmarah" are iron-smiths (lohakara — blacksmiths); the "punjishtha" are those who collect sand-piles (valuka — sand-workers); "nishadah" are forest-dwelling hunters; those who carry loops and leashes tied to dogs are the "shvani" (dog-handlers).

[Note on the structure of the fourth anuvaka:]

The Acharya (commentator) here completes the eighth commentary by briefly glossing the nine self-evident mantras that form a continuous litany, listing various social and occupational groups — chariot-warriors, chariot-less warriors, chariot-riders, chariot-lords, chariot-drivers, rein-holders, carpenters, chariot-makers, potters, blacksmiths, weavers, forest-hunters, arrow-makers, bow-makers, hunters, dog-keepers, dogs, and dog-lords — all of whom are saluted as manifestations of the Supreme Lord.

The theological principle underlying this section is the doctrine of sarvatmakatva (the Lord being the self of all). Just as the third anuvaka (chapter section) described the Lord in the form of warriors at various stages of combat — stringing bows, placing arrows, drawing bowstrings, releasing arrows, shooting, and piercing targets — the fourth anuvaka extends this vision to encompass all living beings in every condition of existence: seated or lying down, sleeping or waking, standing or running, assembled or leading assemblies, mounted on horses or leading horse-troops. The vision culminates in the comprehensive salutation of all occupational classes, from the noblest warriors to the humblest dog-keepers, affirming that the one Supreme Rudra dwells equally in all.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    ९७
---

पेताः महान्तः, तद्रहिताः क्षुल्लकाः ॥

नमो रथिभ्योऽरथेभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमो
रथेभ्यो  रथपतिभ्यञ्च वो नमो  नमः से-
नाभ्यः सेनानिभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमः क्षत्तृ-
भ्यः संग्रहीतृभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमस्तक्ष-
भ्यो रथकारेभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमः कुला-
लेभ्यः कर्मारेभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमः पुञ्जि-
ष्टेभ्यो निषादेभ्यञ्च वो नमो नम इषुकृ-
द्भ्यो धन्वकृद्भ्यञ्च वो नमो नमो मृगयु-
भ्यः श्वनिभ्यञ्च वो नमो नमः श्वभ्यः
श्वपतिभ्यञ्च वो नमः ॥

अथाष्टमः— नमो रथिभ्य इति । रथवन्तः रथिनः,
तद्रहिताः अरथाः; नवमाद्यः श्वपतिभ्य इत्यन्ताः नव
मन्त्राः स्पष्टार्थाः। तत्र च रथचालकाः सारथयः क्षत्तारः,
त एव रथं नियच्छन्तः संग्रहीतारः, तश्चरथकारयोरवान्त-
रभेदाद्वेदः; कर्मारा: लोहकाराः, पिञ्जिपुञ्जानां वालुकाः
पुञ्जिष्टाः, निषादाः श्वपशाः, शुनकगळबद्धपाशघराः श्व-

R. B. 7

sanskrit.krama: नम॑ इशु॒कृद्भ्यः॑ । इशु॒कृद्भ्यो॑ धन्व॒कृद्भ्यः॑ । धन्व॒कृद्भ्य॑श्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥
नमो॑ मृग॒युभ्यः॑ । मृग॒युभ्यः॑ श्व॒निभ्यः॑ । श्व॒निभ्य॑श्च । च वो॒ । वो नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | mrigayubhyah | shvanibhyah | iti | shvani-bhyah | cha | vah | namah || 16 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Shvanayah' are the holders of the nooses/leashes (pasha) bound around the necks of dogs. (16)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah mrigayubhyah'—to hunters. Indeed, they seek deer (mriga) continuously with the desire to kill. [Grammatical note]. 'Shvanibhyashcha vo namah'—to the leaders of dogs; those who protect/keep dogs, the dog-handlers. [Grammatical note]. (16)

He speaks the seventeenth Yajus:
Salutation to the dogs and to the lords of dogs, salutation to you.

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | bhavaya | cha | rudraya | cha || 1 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Bhavah'—because living beings arise (bhavanti) from him. 'Rudrah'—because he melts away (dravayati) the sorrow (rut) which is the cause of weeping. (1)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: For the two anuvakas 'Namo bhavaya cha' and 'Namo jyeshthaya cha' (5th and 6th), the Rishi and Deity are Bhagavan Shambhu Himself. The Metre is Mahavirat. Thus, through the (previous) three anuvakas (2, 3, 4), the God's universal lordship (vishvadhipatyam) was established. From here on, the God is praised according to the distinctions of his eight forms (Ashta-murti). The salutations at both ends are gone. From here on, salutations are at one end. Among those, those with salutation at the beginning: 'Namo bhavaya cha rudraya cha'. He who exists (bhavati) everywhere and at all times is 'Bhavah'. [Grammatical note]. He indeed exists, and never at any time does he not exist. Or: he who manifests (bhavayati) everything, or is contemplated (bhavyate) by all. Or: he in whom everything exists. He is the presiding deity (adhishthata) of Water among the eight forms. 'Rudraya' has been explained. He is the presiding deity of the Sun (Surya). Therefore, he is recited again in the section on presiding deities. (1)

Salutation to the destroyer and to the lord of cattle.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Fifth Anuvāka (pañcamo'nuvākaḥ)

                         ———

      Homage to Bhava and to Rudra; homage to
    Śarva and to Paśupati.

      Thus in the fourth anuvāka the Lord was saluted as the Soul of all. Now, keeping in mind the Lord's exclusive distinguishing attributes, the text begins His adoration. From this mantra onward through "drāpe," the salutations are directed toward various aspects of the Lord; each mantra from the opening salutation onward stands as a single unit, as for instance "namo bhavāya ca rudrāya ca" and so forth. The same should be understood for the others as well.

      Here, in the first two mantras, homage is offered to the Lord while contemplating His exclusive, unsurpassed glory as the cause of the birth, sustenance, dissolution, bondage, and liberation of all beings — particularly His lordship over the herd of souls (paśukulayakāritva). "Namo bhavāya ca" — He from whom this world comes into being is Bhava (bhavaty asmāt jagad iti bhavaḥ); through this name is indicated the Lord's role as the cause of world-creation (jagaj-janmahetutva), well-attested in the Śruti by "From Him indeed all these — Brahma, Viṣṇu, Rudra, and Indra — are born." "He who drives away (drāvayati) the suffering of saṃsāra (worldly existence) is Rudra"; through this name, the role of Rudra as the bestower of liberation (mokṣahetutva) is stated, as well-attested in the Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad: "When men shall roll up space as though it were a piece of hide, then shall the end of suffering come without knowing Śiva," and in "Rudra is the liberating (tāraka) Brahman who carries [the dying] across."

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
पञ्चमोऽनुवाकः ॥

                         ———

      नमो भवाय च रुद्राय च नमः श-
    र्वाय च पशुपतये च ॥

      इत्यं चतुर्थे सर्वात्मकत्वेन भगवान्नमस्कृतः ; इदानीं
भगवद्साधारणधर्मरूपतन्यमिह्मानुसंघानपूर्वकं तत्नमस्कार-
मारभते । एतदारभ्य 'द्रापे' इत्येतत्पर्यन्तमन्यतो नमस्-
काराः, सर्वैत्र नमस्कारादिरेकैको मन्त्रः ; यथा— 'नमो
भवाय च रुद्राय च' इत्यादि । एवमन्येऽपि ज्ञेयाः ।
तत्र प्रथमं द्वाभ्यां मन्त्राभ्यां जगज्जन्मस्थितिसंहारबन्घमो-
क्षार्थपशुकुलयकारित्वरूपरपरमेश्वरासाधारणमिह्मानसतुसं-
दधानसतं नमस्करोति— नमो भवाय चेति । भवत्यसा-
ज्जगदिति भवः, अनेन 'सर्वमिदं ब्रह्माविष्णुरुद्रेन्द्रास्ते संप्र-
सूयन्ते' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धं भगवतो जगज्जन्महेतुत्वं प्रद-
शितम् । हदं संसारदुःखं द्रावयतीति रुद्रः, अनेन 'यदा
चमेवदाकाशं वेष्टियिष्यन्ति मानवाः । तदा शिवमिज्ञाय
दुःखस्यान्तो भविष्यति' 'बद्रस्तारकं ब्रह्म ब्याच्छे' इति

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ श॒र्वाय॑ । श॒र्वाय॑ च । च पशु॒पत॑ये । प॒शु॒पत॑ये च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    101

The well-known Śruti passage establishes His role as the bestower of liberation (mokṣahetutva). "He who destroys (śṛṇāti hinaṣti) everything is Śarva"; through this name, the role of Śarva as the destroyer of the universe (jagat-saṃhartṛtva) is stated, as attested in the Śruti: "To that great Time who gathers all in — salutation." By the word Paśupati (lord of souls/beasts), the role of the Lord as the cause of bondage and sustenance is stated: the word paśu (animal, bound soul) refers to the bondage of saṃsāra, and the word pati (lord) refers to protection and enjoyment of that. Accordingly, the Śruti — "He is the cause of saṃsāra-liberation, sustenance, and bondage" — affirms that He is the cause of world-sustenance and bondage; hence, it is concluded that the meaning of these two mantras is homage to the Lord as the cause of the five cosmic functions relating to the herd of souls. Here, to show that the word namaḥ connects individually with each of the four sets of paired names, the conjunctive particles "ca" (and) serve to indicate coordination. Thus the connection runs: "namaḥ bhavāya ca" and "namaḥ śarvāya ca." The same should be understood for all the other pairs throughout.

      Homage to Nīlagrīva and to Śitikaṇṭha.

      Now the third [mantra]: "Namo nīlagrīvāya" — He whose throat and neck (grīvā, kaṇṭha) are blue from drinking the Kālakūṭa poison is Nīlagrīva (the blue-throated one); He whose throat was white (dhavala) before the event of the poison-drinking is Śitikaṇṭha (the white-throated one). By this mantra, the Lord's compassion for all worlds is indicated — His compassion that moved Him, disregarding the natural defect inflicted on His otherwise fair throat by the Kālakūṭa poison, to swallow the venom for the single purpose of protecting the world. This is well-attested in the Śruti: "You have compassion upon this entire world" — and similar passages.

      Homage to Kapardī and to Vyuṣkeśa.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    १०१

श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं मोक्षहेतुत्वमुक्तम् । श्रृणावि हिनस्ति सर्वमिति
शर्वः, अनेन 'तस्मा उपसंहर्तें महामासाय नमः' इत्यादि-
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्संहर्तृत्वमुक्तम् । पशुपतिशब्देन बन्धस्थि-
तिहेतुत्वमुच्यते, पशुशब्देन संसारबन्धस्य पतिशब्देन तद्र-
क्षणस्य भोक्तेः ; तथा च 'संसारमोक्षस्थितिबन्धहेतुः' इति
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्स्थितिबन्धहेतुत्वमुक्तं भवतीत्याह्य पशुकु-
क्षहेतुत्वेन भगवन्नमस्कारो मन्त्रद्वयार्थः । अत्र नमःशब्द-
ल प्रत्येकं चतुष्पर्यन्तपदाभ्यामन्वयं दर्शियितुं समुच्चयार्थौ च-
कारौ फलितौ । तेन भवाय च नमः शर्वाय च नमः इत्य-
न्वयो भवति । एवममेऽपि सर्वत्र द्रष्टव्यम् ॥

      नमो नीलग्रीवाय च शितिकण्ठाय च ॥

      अथ तृतीयः:—— नमो नीलग्रीवायेति । काळकूटमसनेन
नीला ग्रीवा कण्ठो यस्य, स नीलग्रीवः, तद्वृषनात्पूर्वे
स्थितः धवलः कण्ठो यस्य, सः शितिकण्ठः । अनेन स्व-
शेत धवलकण्ठे काळकूटप्रयुक्तं नैसर्ग्य दोषमविगण्यम जगदे-
करक्षणाय विषं कबलितवतो भगवतः सर्वलोकदयालुत्वम्
'अहीमिदं दयसे विश्वम्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धं प्रदशिवम् ॥

      नमः कपर्दिने च ब्युस्केशाय च ॥

sanskrit.krama: नमो॒ नील॑ग्रीवाय । नील॑ग्रीवाय च । च शिति॒कण्ठा॑य । शिति॒कण्ठा॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | nila-grivaya | cha | shiti-kanthaya | cha || 3 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Nilagrivah' is he who has a part of his neck blue in color due to holding the Kalakuta poison. 'Shitikanthah' is he who has the remaining neck region white (shiti). (3)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo nilagrivaya cha'—explained already. 'Shitikanthaya cha'—because 'shiti' means both white and dark according to the Nighantu. Indeed, only a small part of the God's neck became blue due to the fire of the Kalakuta poison. Or: the repetition is for the sake of respect, as in "Salutation to the Lord of Ambika, the Lord of Uma, the Lord of Beings." [Grammatical note on the word 'shitikanthaya']. (3)

Salutation to the one with matted hair and to the one with shaven hair.

Accordingly, the Śruti — "He is the cause of saṃsāra-liberation, sustenance, and bondage" — affirms that He is the cause of world-sustenance and bondage; hence, it is concluded that the meaning of these two mantras is homage to the Lord as the cause of the five cosmic functions relating to the herd of souls. Here, to show that the word namaḥ connects individually with each of the four sets of paired names, the conjunctive particles "ca" (and) serve to indicate coordination. Thus the connection runs: "namaḥ bhavāya ca" and "namaḥ śarvāya ca." The same should be understood for all the other pairs throughout.

      Homage to Nīlagrīva and to Śitikaṇṭha.

      Now the third [mantra]: "Namo nīlagrīvāya" — He whose throat and neck (grīvā, kaṇṭha) are blue from drinking the Kālakūṭa poison is Nīlagrīva (the blue-throated one); He whose throat was white (dhavala) before the event of the poison-drinking is Śitikaṇṭha (the white-throated one). By this mantra, the Lord's compassion for all worlds is indicated — His compassion that moved Him, disregarding the natural defect inflicted on His otherwise fair throat by the Kālakūṭa poison, to swallow the venom for the single purpose of protecting the world. This is well-attested in the Śruti: "You have compassion upon this entire world" — and similar passages.

      Homage to Kapardī and to Vyuṣkeśa.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    १०१

श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं मोक्षहेतुत्वमुक्तम् । श्रृणावि हिनस्ति सर्वमिति
शर्वः, अनेन 'तस्मा उपसंहर्तें महामासाय नमः' इत्यादि-
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्संहर्तृत्वमुक्तम् । पशुपतिशब्देन बन्धस्थि-
तिहेतुत्वमुच्यते, पशुशब्देन संसारबन्धस्य पतिशब्देन तद्र-
क्षणस्य भोक्तेः ; तथा च 'संसारमोक्षस्थितिबन्धहेतुः' इति
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्स्थितिबन्धहेतुत्वमुक्तं भवतीत्याह्य पशुकु-
क्षहेतुत्वेन भगवन्नमस्कारो मन्त्रद्वयार्थः । अत्र नमःशब्द-
ल प्रत्येकं चतुष्पर्यन्तपदाभ्यामन्वयं दर्शियितुं समुच्चयार्थौ च-
कारौ फलितौ । तेन भवाय च नमः शर्वाय च नमः इत्य-
न्वयो भवति । एवममेऽपि सर्वत्र द्रष्टव्यम् ॥

      नमो नीलग्रीवाय च शितिकण्ठाय च ॥

      अथ तृतीयः:—— नमो नीलग्रीवायेति । काळकूटमसनेन
नीला ग्रीवा कण्ठो यस्य, स नीलग्रीवः, तद्वृषनात्पूर्वे
स्थितः धवलः कण्ठो यस्य, सः शितिकण्ठः । अनेन स्व-
शेत धवलकण्ठे काळकूटप्रयुक्तं नैसर्ग्य दोषमविगण्यम जगदे-
करक्षणाय विषं कबलितवतो भगवतः सर्वलोकदयालुत्वम्
'अहीमिदं दयसे विश्वम्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धं प्रदशिवम् ॥

      नमः कपर्दिने च ब्युस्केशाय च ॥

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ कप॒र्दिने॑ । कप॒र्दिने॑ च । च व्यु॑प्तकेशाय । व्यु॑प्तकेशाय च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | kapardine | cha | vyupta-keshaya | cha || 4 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Kapardi' is he who has matted hair (kaparda). 'Vyuptakeshah' is he whose hair is shaven (munidita). The matted hair refers to the guise of a Pashupata ascetic. Shaven hair refers to the guise of a Sannyasi (yati). (4)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah kapardine cha'—clear. 'Vyuptakeshaya cha'—to the shaven-headed one, in his form as the Sacrificer (Yajamana). Others say 'vyuptakesha' means half-shaven hair. [Grammatical note]. (4)

Salutation to the thousand-eyed one and to the one with a hundred bows.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    101

The well-known Śruti passage establishes His role as the bestower of liberation (mokṣahetutva). "He who destroys (śṛṇāti hinaṣti) everything is Śarva"; through this name, the role of Śarva as the destroyer of the universe (jagat-saṃhartṛtva) is stated, as attested in the Śruti: "To that great Time who gathers all in — salutation." By the word Paśupati (lord of souls/beasts), the role of the Lord as the cause of bondage and sustenance is stated: the word paśu (animal, bound soul) refers to the bondage of saṃsāra, and the word pati (lord) refers to protection and enjoyment of that. Accordingly, the Śruti — "He is the cause of saṃsāra-liberation, sustenance, and bondage" — affirms that He is the cause of world-sustenance and bondage; hence, it is concluded that the meaning of these two mantras is homage to the Lord as the cause of the five cosmic functions relating to the herd of souls. Here, to show that the word namaḥ connects individually with each of the four sets of paired names, the conjunctive particles "ca" (and) serve to indicate coordination. Thus the connection runs: "namaḥ bhavāya ca" and "namaḥ śarvāya ca." The same should be understood for all the other pairs throughout.

      Homage to Nīlagrīva and to Śitikaṇṭha.

      Now the third [mantra]: "Namo nīlagrīvāya" — He whose throat and neck (grīvā, kaṇṭha) are blue from drinking the Kālakūṭa poison is Nīlagrīva (the blue-throated one); He whose throat was white (dhavala) before the event of the poison-drinking is Śitikaṇṭha (the white-throated one). By this mantra, the Lord's compassion for all worlds is indicated — His compassion that moved Him, disregarding the natural defect inflicted on His otherwise fair throat by the Kālakūṭa poison, to swallow the venom for the single purpose of protecting the world. This is well-attested in the Śruti: "You have compassion upon this entire world" — and similar passages.

      Homage to Kapardī and to Vyuṣkeśa.

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ सहस्रा॒क्षाय॑ । सहस्रा॒क्षाय॑ च । च श॒तध॑न्वने । श॒तध॑न्वने च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | sahasra-akshaya | cha | shata-dhanvane | cha || 5 ||
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
102                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

      Now the fourth [mantra]: "Namaḥ kapardine" — Kapardas (matted hair coils) are the Lord's mass of matted locks; the one who possesses them is Kapardī. Through this name, the great glory of the Lord's matted-hair-bearing warrior Vīrabhadra — well-known from the Bhāgavata [Purāṇa] in the verse "Kill Dakṣa and his sacrifice; the hero of my matted locks shall lead the battle-forces for me" — is suggested. Thereby is indicated the glory of the destruction of Dakṣa's sacrifice, well-attested in the Śruti: "The gods were about to perform a sacrifice; Rudra would have destroyed that sacrifice" — and so forth.

      He whose hair is cut off (vyuptāḥ) and shaven (śuṇḍitāḥ) is Vyuṣkeśa (the shaven-headed one) — referring to the great Paramahaṃsa wandering ascetic (parivrājaka). This is established by the Śruti: "The wandering renunciant wears one garment, is surrounded by disciples, carries a water-pot and is shaven-headed; those who are knowers through wisdom wear the sacred thread of knowledge (jñānayajñopavītin)." Accordingly, renowned [forms of the Lord] such as Dattātreya and Durvāsas — well-known from the Jābāla Upaniṣad and similar texts — and in the Kali Age, the Śaṅkarācārya Svāmins — well-known from the Purāṇas as divine incarnations of the Supreme Lord, as stated in "Śaṅkara will descend with four accomplished disciples" and "Śaṅkara is Savitā and Agni" — are meant by this form. Through this name, the role of the Lord as the initiator of the tradition of Brahma-vidyā (knowledge of Brahman) is indicated, as well-attested in the Śruti: "He is the lord of the Brahman, the overlord of Brahman" (brahmadhipatiḥ brahmaṇo'dhipatiḥ).

      Homage to Sahasrākṣa and to Śataghānvane.

      Now the fifth [mantra]: "Namaḥ sahasrākṣāya" — He whose eyes are a thousand, i.e., infinite in number, is Sahasrākṣa (the thousand-eyed one). Through this name, the Lord's omniscience (sarvajñatva), well-attested in the Śruti by "He who is all-knowing," is indicated. He who possesses a hundred — i.e., innumerable — bows (dhanūṃṣi), such as the cosmic bow Meru and others, is Śata-

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
१०२                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

      अथ चतुर्थः:—— नमः कपर्दिन इति । कपर्दैः परमे-
श्वरजटाकलापः, तद्वान् कपर्दी, अनेन 'दक्षं सयञ्चं जहि
वं भटानां त्वमप्रणी हद्रजटांशको मे' इति भागवताद्प्र-
सिद्धः भगवज्जटांशवीरमद्रमहिह्मा स्मय्यैते । तेन च 'देवा
वै यञ्चरुद्रमन्तरायन् स यञ्चमविष्यत्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्ध-
दक्षाध्वरबन्धसमहिह्मा दशिवत: । व्युप्ताः श्रुण्डिताः केशा:
यस्य, सः व्युष्केशः, परमहंसपरिव्राजकः ; 'अथ परि-
व्राडेकशाटी शिष्यपरिवृतो मुण्डोदरपात्री' 'ज्ञानिशिक्षिन:
ज्ञानयज्ञोपवीलिन:' इत्यादिश्रुवेः ; तेन दत्तात्रेयदुर्वांसःप्र-
श्रुतयः जाबालोपनिषदादिप्रसिद्धाः, कलियुगे च 'चतुर्भिः
सिद्धतः शिष्यैः शंकरोऽवतरिष्यति' 'शंकरः सविताग्नन:'
इत्यादिपुराणप्रसिद्धपरमेश्वरावतारभावाः शंकराचार्यस्वामि-
नः, तद्रूपायेत्यर्थे । अनेन 'ब्रह्माधिपतिर्ब्रह्मणोऽधिपतिः'
इति श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं ब्रह्मविद्यासंप्रदायप्रवर्तकत्वमुक्तम् ॥

      नमः सहस्राक्षाय च शतघन्वने च ॥

      अथ पञ्चमः:——नमः सहस्राक्षायेति । सहस्रम् अनन्ता-
नि अक्षिणि यस्य, सः सहस्राक्षः, अनेन 'यः सर्वज्ञः'
इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धः सर्वज्ञत्वमहिह्मा दशितः । शतम् अनन्ता-
नि धन्वानि धनूंषि वेद्मणवमेर्वादिरूपाणि यस्य, सः श्रत-

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ गिरि॒शाय॑ । गिरि॒शाय॑ च । च शिपिवि॒ष्टाय॑ । शिपिवि॒ष्टाय च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | girishaya | cha | shipivishtaya | cha || 6 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Girishah' is he who resides (shete) on the mountain Kailasa. 'Shipivishtah' is he who wears the form of Vishnu; for the Shruti says, "Vishnu is indeed Shipivishta." (6)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo girishaya cha'—already explained. 'Shipivishtaya cha'—to him who has cast off the remainder (nirveshtita-sheshaya), always in places like Deodar forests. Or: to the one who is the self of the Sun. As stated in the Skanda Purana:
"Water is called 'shiti' because of coldness or its connection with resting (shayana). Because of drinking or protecting that, rays (rashmayah) are known as 'shipayah'. The Sun who has entered into them is then called 'Shipivishta'."
Or: 'shipayah' means cattle. For the Shruti says: "Sacrifice is indeed Vishnu, cattle are 'shipi'." The meaning is: to him who has entered into all beings as the Inner Ruler (Antaryami). (6)

Salutation to the most bountiful and to the one with arrows.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    103

dhānvan (the hundred-bowed one). As the Mahābhārata describes: "Having said 'so be it' to Viṣṇu and made Him the supreme arrow, having fashioned the salt-mountain (śālya) as the arrowhead, the Great Lord crafted the bow of the syllable Om; and the Great God made the Gāyatrī as the bowstring" — indicating the Lord who is equipped with many bows such as the cosmic Meru, Pināka, and others. Through this name, the glory of the destruction of the Triple Cities (tripura-saṃhāra) is indicated, well-attested in the Śruti: "There were three cities of those demons."

      Homage to Giriśa and to Śipiviṣṭa;
      homage to Mīḍhuṣṭama and to Iṣumat.

      Now with two [mantras], the commentator shows the Lord's nature as the Trinity (trimūrti) and His supremacy over the universe. "Namo giriśāya" — the mountain (giri) is Kailāsa; he who possesses it [as his abode] is Giriśa, with the suffix śa in the sense of "possessing" in the class of derivatives known as lomādi (a Pāṇinian class); the meaning is: "to Him who is of that form [i.e., the one who dwells on Kailāsa]." Śipiviṣṭa is Viṣṇu — "Viṣṇu is Śipiviṣṭa," says the Śruti — meaning: "to Him in the form of Viṣṇu." Mīḍhuṣṭama is Hiraṇyagarbha, the creator of the universe, who is supreme in virility (vīrya) and is possessed of a slightly lesser degree of that power; the meaning is: "to Him in the form of Hiraṇyagarbha." Through this, the nature of the Lord as the Trinity — "He is Brahmā, He is Hari, He is Indra, He is the Imperishable Supreme" — as declared by the Śruti, is affirmed.

      He who possesses excellent arrows (iṣavaḥ) is Iṣumat (the arrow-possessor), with the suffix matuP in the sense of distinction (praśaṃsā); and the excellence of the arrow is stated in the Śruti: "That arrow he fashioned, aimed it at the demons, Soma as the shaft, Viṣṇu as the point" — and so forth. Through this, the participation of all the gods themselves as accessories in the Lord's campaign of Tripura-destruction is acknowledged, and thereby

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    १०३

धन्वा, 'स तथोक्ततथेत्युक्त्वा विष्णुं कृत्वा शरोत्तमम् ।
शाल्यमत्रं तथा कृत्वा धनुर्मेहमकारयत् ॥ कृत्वा च धनुरो-
कारं साविरी ज्यां महेश्वरः' इत्यादिभारतोक्तवेद्मणवमेरु-
पिनाकाद्यनेकधनुर्युक्तायेत्यर्थे। अनेन 'तेषामसुराणां त्रिः
पुर आसन्' इति श्रुतिप्रसिद्धत्रिपुरसंहारमहिह्मा दर्शितः ॥

      नमो गिरिशाय च शिपिविष्टाय च
            नमो मीढुष्टमाय चेषुमते च ॥

      अथ द्वाभ्यां त्रिमूर्त्यात्मकत्वम्, विश्वाधिकत्वं च दर्शयति
— नमो गिरिशायेति । गिरिः कैलाशः, सोऽस्यास्तीति
गिरिशः, लोमादित्वान्मत्वर्थीयः शः, तद्रूपाय इत्यर्थे ।
शिपिविष्टः विष्णुः, 'विष्णुः शिपिविष्टः' इति श्रुवेः;
विष्णुरूपायेत्यर्थे। मीढुष्टमः अतिशयेन वीर्येणेषच्छकः जग-
त्वष्टा हिरण्यगर्भः, तद्रूपायेत्यर्थे। अनेन 'स ब्रह्मा च
हरिः सेन्द्रः सोऽक्षरः परमः खरात्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धत्रि-
मूर्त्यात्मकत्वमुक्तं भवति। प्रशस्तः इषुरस्यास्तीति इषुपान्,
प्रशंसायां मतुप् ; प्राशस्त्यं च इषोः 'स इषुक् समस्कु-
वेताभिप्रमनीक^ सोमं^ शल्यं विष्णुं तेजनम्' इत्यादिश्रु-
त्युक्तं द्रष्टव्यम् । तेन च सर्वदेवानामपि त्रिपुरसंहारीयं भ-

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ मी॒ढुष्ट॑माय । मी॒ढुष्ट॑माय च । चेषु॑मते । इषु॑मते च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | midhuh-tamaya | cha | ishumate | iti | ishu-mate | cha || 7 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Midhustamah'—one who rains (shatters) excessively in the form of a cloud. 'Ishuman'—the holder of arrows. (7)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo midhustamaya cha'—to the greatest shedder/pourer. [Grammatical note]. 'Ishumate cha'—to the one with excellent arrows, or holding arrows in hand; it means a great hero. (7)

Salutation to the short and to the dwarf.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    103

dhānvan (the hundred-bowed one). As the Mahābhārata describes: "Having said 'so be it' to Viṣṇu and made Him the supreme arrow, having fashioned the salt-mountain (śālya) as the arrowhead, the Great Lord crafted the bow of the syllable Om; and the Great God made the Gāyatrī as the bowstring" — indicating the Lord who is equipped with many bows such as the cosmic Meru, Pināka, and others. Through this name, the glory of the destruction of the Triple Cities (tripura-saṃhāra) is indicated, well-attested in the Śruti: "There were three cities of those demons."

      Homage to Giriśa and to Śipiviṣṭa;
      homage to Mīḍhuṣṭama and to Iṣumat.

      Now with two [mantras], the commentator shows the Lord's nature as the Trinity (trimūrti) and His supremacy over the universe. "Namo giriśāya" — the mountain (giri) is Kailāsa; he who possesses it [as his abode] is Giriśa, with the suffix śa in the sense of "possessing" in the class of derivatives known as lomādi (a Pāṇinian class); the meaning is: "to Him who is of that form [i.e., the one who dwells on Kailāsa]." Śipiviṣṭa is Viṣṇu — "Viṣṇu is Śipiviṣṭa," says the Śruti — meaning: "to Him in the form of Viṣṇu." Mīḍhuṣṭama is Hiraṇyagarbha, the creator of the universe, who is supreme in virility (vīrya) and is possessed of a slightly lesser degree of that power; the meaning is: "to Him in the form of Hiraṇyagarbha." Through this, the nature of the Lord as the Trinity — "He is Brahmā, He is Hari, He is Indra, He is the Imperishable Supreme" — as declared by the Śruti, is affirmed.

      He who possesses excellent arrows (iṣavaḥ) is Iṣumat (the arrow-possessor), with the suffix matuP in the sense of distinction (praśaṃsā); and the excellence of the arrow is stated in the Śruti: "That arrow he fashioned, aimed it at the demons, Soma as the shaft, Viṣṇu as the point" — and so forth. Through this, the participation of all the gods themselves as accessories in the Lord's campaign of Tripura-destruction is acknowledged, and thereby

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    १०३

धन्वा, 'स तथोक्ततथेत्युक्त्वा विष्णुं कृत्वा शरोत्तमम् ।
शाल्यमत्रं तथा कृत्वा धनुर्मेहमकारयत् ॥ कृत्वा च धनुरो-
कारं साविरी ज्यां महेश्वरः' इत्यादिभारतोक्तवेद्मणवमेरु-
पिनाकाद्यनेकधनुर्युक्तायेत्यर्थे। अनेन 'तेषामसुराणां त्रिः
पुर आसन्' इति श्रुतिप्रसिद्धत्रिपुरसंहारमहिह्मा दर्शितः ॥

      नमो गिरिशाय च शिपिविष्टाय च
            नमो मीढुष्टमाय चेषुमते च ॥

      अथ द्वाभ्यां त्रिमूर्त्यात्मकत्वम्, विश्वाधिकत्वं च दर्शयति
— नमो गिरिशायेति । गिरिः कैलाशः, सोऽस्यास्तीति
गिरिशः, लोमादित्वान्मत्वर्थीयः शः, तद्रूपाय इत्यर्थे ।
शिपिविष्टः विष्णुः, 'विष्णुः शिपिविष्टः' इति श्रुवेः;
विष्णुरूपायेत्यर्थे। मीढुष्टमः अतिशयेन वीर्येणेषच्छकः जग-
त्वष्टा हिरण्यगर्भः, तद्रूपायेत्यर्थे। अनेन 'स ब्रह्मा च
हरिः सेन्द्रः सोऽक्षरः परमः खरात्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धत्रि-
मूर्त्यात्मकत्वमुक्तं भवति। प्रशस्तः इषुरस्यास्तीति इषुपान्,
प्रशंसायां मतुप् ; प्राशस्त्यं च इषोः 'स इषुक् समस्कु-
वेताभिप्रमनीक^ सोमं^ शल्यं विष्णुं तेजनम्' इत्यादिश्रु-
त्युक्तं द्रष्टव्यम् । तेन च सर्वदेवानामपि त्रिपुरसंहारीयं भ-

sanskrit.krama: नमो᳚ ह्र॒स्वाय॑ । ह्र॒स्वाय॑ च । च वाम॒नाय॑ । वाम॒नाय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमो॑ बृह॒ते । बृह॒ते च । च वर्​षी॑यसे । वर्​षी॑यसे च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | brihate | cha | varshiyase | cha || 9 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Brihan'—large in size. 'Varshiyan'—rich in qualities. (9)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo brihate cha'—to the one great in size. [Grammatical note]. 'Varshiyase cha'—to the one most excellent in size, or in knowledge etc. [Grammatical note]. (9)

Salutation to the old and to the one who well-increases.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
104                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

His supremacy over all the gods — His status as their divine patron — is confirmed. And through this, the Lord's supremacy over the entire universe (viśvādhikatva) is indicated, as well-attested in the Śruti: "He who is the source and the dissolution of the gods," "Rudra, the great sage, is supreme over the universe" (viśvādhiko rudro maharṣiḥ), and "The other deities are partial aspects" — and so forth.

      Homage to Hrasva and to Vāmana.

      Now the eighth [mantra]: "Namo hrasvāya" — He who is of small measure in bodily form is Hrasva (the small one). Through this name, the glory of upāsanā (meditative worship) through the Dahara Vidyā (the meditation on the infinite Brahman within the tiny space of the heart) is indicated, as well-attested in the Śruti: "Even though [the ātman] is tiny, it is free of sorrow, pervading the sky." Vāmana is the Great Gaṇapati, whose limbs such as the feet are contracted — the meaning being: "to Him in the form of Gaṇapati." This is because the Incarnation of the Supreme Lord as the Great Gaṇapati is well-attested in the Mantraśāstra (tantric scriptures), Purāṇas, and other authorities. Through this name, the glory well-attested in the Śruti — "We call upon You, O Gaṇapati, lord of the hosts (gaṇānāṃ tvā gaṇapatiṃ havāmahe)" — is indicated.

      Homage to Bṛhat and to Varṣīyas.

      Now the ninth [mantra]: "Namo bṛhate" — Freedom from the threefold limitation of space, time, and object is what is established in Brahma Mīmāṃsā (Vedānta) as "bṛhattva" (the nature of Bṛhat, the immeasurably vast); hence, the meaning is: "to the Supreme Brahman (Parabrahman), who is Bṛhat." The Atharvāśiras Śruti declares: "Why is it called the Supreme Brahman? Because It is vast (bṛhati) and makes all things vast (bṛṃhayati) — that is why It is called the Supreme Brahman." Through this, the status of the Lord as the Supreme Brahman is indicated, as declared in the Śrutis: "Brahman is truth, knowledge, infinite" and "Sat-Brahman, knowledge, infinite is Brahman" — and so forth.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
१०४                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

गवत्परिकरत्वमनुज्ञायते। तेन च 'यो देवानां प्रभवश्चोद्र-
वश्च' 'विश्वाधिको रुद्रो महर्षिः' 'अञ्ज्ञान्यन्या देवताः'
इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धं विश्वाधिकत्वं प्रदशितम् ॥

      नमो हृस्वाय च वामनाय च ॥

      अथाष्टमः— नमो हृस्वायेति । शरीरे लघुप्रमाणो
हृस्वः, अनेन 'सञ्चापि दहं गगनं विशोकः' इत्यादिश्रुति-
प्रसिद्धं दहरविद्योपासलत्वं प्रदशितम् । संकुचितपादाद्यवयवो
वामनः महागणपतिः, तद्रूपायेत्यर्थे। महागणपतेः साक्षा-
त्परमेश्वरावतारत्वस्य मन्त्रशास्त्रपुराणादिप्रसिद्धेः। अनेन
'गणानां त्वा गणपतिं हवामहे' इति श्रुतिप्रसिद्धो महिह्मा
दशितः ॥

      नमो बृहते च वर्षीयसे च ॥

      अथ नवमः:—— नमो बृहत इति । देशकालबस्तुक्रत-
त्रिविधपरिच्छेदरहित्यं बृहत्त्वमिति ब्रह्ममीमांसायां व्यव-
स्थापितम्; बृहते परब्रह्मण इत्यर्थः। 'अथ कस्मादुच्यते
परं ब्रह्मेति यस्माद्बृहति बृंहयति तस्मादुच्यते परं ब्रह्म'
इति अथर्वेशिरःश्रुतेः। अनेन 'ऋतं सत्यं परं ब्रह्म' 'सदब्रह्म
ज्ञानमनन्तं ब्रह्म' इत्यादिश्रुत्युक्तं परब्रह्मत्वं दशितं भवति ।

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ वृ॒द्धाय॑ । वृ॒द्धाय॑ च । च संवृँध्व॑ने । संवृँध्व॑ने च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | vriddhaya | cha | sam-vridhvane | cha || 10 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Vriddhah'—advanced in age. 'Samvridhva'—well-augmented by the hymns (of the Veda). (10)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo vriddhaya cha'—great in age. 'Samvridhvane cha'—to the one who is well-grown. It means one who is constantly increased (in glory) by hymns of praise. As it is said: "May good praises increase you." Or: to the one who well-increases his devotees. [Grammatical note]. (10)

Salutation to the foremost and to the first.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    105

He who abounds in divine qualities is Varṣīyas (the supremely excellent). Through this name, the glory of the Lord's divine attributes is indicated, as declared in the Śruti: "He who is truth-desiring (satya-kāmaḥ) and whose will is truth (satya-saṅkalpaḥ)" and "His supreme power is heard of as manifold and various — natural, consisting of knowledge, strength, and action" — and similar passages.

      Homage to Vṛddha and to Saṃtṛdhvane.

      Now the tenth [mantra]: "Namo vṛddhāya" — He who is superior to all in age (vayas) is Vṛddha (the ancient, the eldest); He who is venerable to all and for whom no one is an object of veneration — that is the meaning. Indeed, this is heard in the Purāṇa: "He whose head should be bowed to — but who would bow before me? Who would fold his hands in reverence before me? Who could be worshipped by one such as Śikhara? Is not the Lord of all beings [already] venerated by all?" Through this name, the Lord's nature as the object of universal obeisance is affirmed, as well-attested in the Śruti: "To whom all offer triple salutations" (yasmai namas triccharodharme). Though the Śruti declares in general terms "to whom all offer salutations" (yasmai namaḥ triccharaḥ), yet since the statement "You are the most excellent of beings" is made with the Supreme Lord as subject, and the concluding statement is "all their salutations [belong] to Him," the Supreme Lord is indeed the one indicated as the recipient of all salutations in the opening clause "yasmai namaḥ" — this is ascertained. The Lord is described primarily as the support of His own power in the form of the cosmic serpent Śiṃśumāra (Delphinus constellation), and the other deities are described as the head, heart, navel, limbs, feet, and tail of that form — each enumerated separately by their own names. Full detail of this is given elsewhere.

He who receives exclusive and exceptional hymns and salutations from all without exception — with no one superior to render homage to — is Saṃtṛdhvan. Through this name is indicated the well-known truth from Śruti and Purāṇa:

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    १०५

गुणैः सत्रूढो वर्षीयान्, 'अनेन सत्यकामः सत्यसंकल्पः'
'परास्य शक्तिर्विविधैव श्रूयते स्वाभाविकी ज्ञानवलक्रिया
च' इत्यादिश्रुत्युक्तिदिव्यगुणमहिह्मा प्रदशितः ॥

      नमो वृद्धाय च संतृध्वने च ॥

      अथ दशमः:—— नमो वृद्धायेति । सर्वापेक्षया वयो-
ऽधिको वृद्धः सर्वैः स्वयं पूज्यः, न तु स्वस्य कश्चित्पूज्यो
वन्ध्वाऽऽसीत्यर्थे। अत एव खलु पुराणे श्रूयते— 'यस्या-
हि शिकः स तु वन्दितव्यः स कः करोत्यञ्जलिकां पुरो मे।
कः पूजितः स्याच्छिशेखरेण नाराधितो भूतपतिस्तु वा
कैः' इति। अनेन 'यस्मै नमस्त्रिच्छरोधर्मे' इति श्रुति-
प्रसिद्धं सर्वेनमस्कारविषयत्वमुक्तं भवति । यद्यपि 'यस्मै
नमस्त्रिच्छरः' इति सामान्येन श्रूयते, तथापि 'त्वं भूतानां
भेष्ठोऽसि' इति परमेश्वरं प्रस्तुत्य 'सर्वे ते नसः' इत्युपसं-
हारे सर्वेस्यापि नमस्कारस्य तदीयत्वोक्त्या 'यस्मै नमः'
इत्युपक्रमेऽपि नमस्कारविषयत्वेन निर्दिष्टः परमेश्वर एवेति
निश्चीयते; तस्यैव निजच्छिक्तिरूपशिंशुमाराधाधारत्वेन
प्रधानतया वञ्छरोरूपप्रधानावयवत्वेन निरूपणीचिह्लात्
देवतान्तराणां तदाकृति प्रति मूर्षेहनुहृदयमध्वचरणपुच्छरू-
पत्वेन स्वस्वनामिभिः पृथङ्निर्दिष्टत्वादित्संयत्र विस्तरः । स-

sanskrit.krama: नमो॒ अग्रि॑याय । अग्रि॑याय च । च प्रथ॒माय॑ । प्रथ॒माय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | agriyaya | cha | prathamaya | cha || 11 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Agriyah'—existing before the creation of the world. 'Prathamah'—chief in the assembly. (11)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo agriyaya cha'—to the one existing at the beginning (agre). [Grammatical note]. 'Prathamaya cha'—to the principal, the foremost. (11)

Salutation to the swift and to the agile.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
106                    Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam

"O Rudra, we shall sing of You to strengthen You with our praises; like a bull bellowing at the cattle-call, You have expanded in both directions through Your own help (ubhayī-virāse) and You have surpassed through descendants"; "I hear You as the most healing among healers (bhiṣaktamaṃ tvā bhiṣajāṃ śṛṇomi)"; and "To one whose deeds are evil and whose deeds are equal, we shall not offend You in any act — O You who are saluted by all the gods" — thus is displayed from Śruti and Purāṇa the Lord's unique status as the sole object of hymns and salutations from all deities without exception.

      Homage to Agriya and to Prathama.

      Now the eleventh [mantra]: "Namo agriyāya" — He who stands first, prior to the arising of the world (jagadutpatti), is Agriya (the primordial one). Now this "primordialness" (agriyatva) belongs to Hiraṇyagarbha (the cosmic creative principle), as established by the Śruti: "Hiraṇyagarbha was born first." Hence the text further specifies: "Prathamāya" — Hiraṇyagarbha too is first [in the manifest order], but even he is the offspring of a cause (kāraṇasūti), meaning: "to Him who is the cause even of Hiraṇyagarbha." The Śvetāśvatara Śruti declares: "He generated Hiraṇyagarbha first." Through this name, the Lord's status as the ultimate primordial cause (agra-kāraṇatva) is indicated, well-attested in the Śruti: "When there was darkness, there was neither day nor night, neither being nor non-being — Śiva alone existed" — and so forth. Although this was already implied by the mantra "namo bhavāya," the present mantra emphasizes the Lord's role as the generator of Hiraṇyagarbha who himself first brings forth the world.

      Homage to Āśave and to Ajira.

      Now the twelfth [mantra]: "Nama āśave" — He who pervades (āvṛṇoti vyāpnoti, pervades) is Āśu

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
१०६                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

न्यकृ असाधारण्येन स्तुतिनमस्कारेर्विहितः संतृध्वा, अनेन
'भा त्वा हद्र चुकुधामानमोभिर्मोमुट्रतीवृषभमासहूती उभो-
विरासेऽपेय शेषजेभिः' 'भिषक्तमं त्वा भिषजां शृणोमि'
'दुष्ठुला सदभस्तुला समानाह्लानकर्मणा। न वयं चुकुध्याम
त्वां सर्वदेवनमस्कृत' इति श्रुतिपुराणसिद्धमनिवर्देवताया-
धारणस्तुतिनमस्कारैकविषयत्वं दशितं भवति ॥

      नमो अग्रियाय च प्रथमाय च ॥

      अथैकादशः—— नमो अग्रियायेति। अग्रे जागदुत्प-
वेः पूर्वमवस्थितः अग्रियः, इदमग्रियत्वं हिरण्यगर्भस्याय-
ति, 'हिरण्यगर्भः समवर्ततागे' इति श्रुवेः; अतो वि-
शिनष्टि— प्रथमायेति। हिरण्यगर्भोदिपि प्रथमः, तक्षापि
कारणसूत इत्यर्थे। 'हिरण्यगर्भं जनयामास पूर्वम्' इति
हि श्वेताश्वतरश्रुतिः। अनेन 'यदातमस्तञ्च दिवा न रात्रिर्नं
सञ्च चासञ्चिछव एव केवलः' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धं अगत्का-
रणस्वं दशितम् । यद्यपि 'नमो भवाय' इत्यनेनैव तदुक्तम्,
तथापि जगत्प्रादिहिरण्यगर्भजनकत्वेन तदेवतारोपवाद्यते ॥

      नम आश्वे चाजिराय च ॥

      अथ द्वादशः:—— नम आश्वे इति। अभ्रोति व्याप्रो-

sanskrit.krama: नम॑ आ॒शवे॑ । आ॒शवे॑ च । चाजि॒राय॑ । अ॒जि॒राय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

[Word-for-word]: Namah | ashave | cha | ajiraya | cha || 12 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Ashuh'—pervading quickly. 'Ajirah'—skilled in moving. (12)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah ashave cha'—to the one who pervades rapidly. [Grammatical note]. 'Ajiraya cha'—to the one skilled in movement. From the root 'aj' (to go/throw). [Grammatical note]. (12)

sanskrit.krama: नम॒-श्शीघ्रि॑याय । शीघ्रि॑याय च । च शीभ्या॑य । शीभ्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥
नम॑ ऊ॒र्म्या॑य । ऊ॒र्म्या॑य च । चावस्व॒न्या॑य । अ॒वस्व॒न्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | urmyaya | cha | ava-svanyaya | cha || 14 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Urmyah'—one associated with waves (urmi). 'Avasvanyah'—one residing in still water (avasvana) where noise is absent. (14)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah urmyaya cha'—to him who exists in waves. [Grammatical note]. 'Avasvanyaya cha'—'avasvana' is silent/still water; either where sound is diminished or from where sound has departed. 'Ava' is used in the sense of 'absent', referring to still water. To the one existing there. (14)

Salutation to the one in the currents and to the one in the islands.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    107

(pervader) — from the root "aśva" meaning "to pervade," with the suffix u, formed as a Uṇādi derivative with the augment ā at the beginning; the meaning is: "to Him who pervades the entire universe." The reason for this pervasion is then stated — "Ajirāya": to Him who is supremely swift in movement (gamana-kuśala), meaning: "to the swift-mover." Through these two names, the Lord's pervasion of the entire universe (nikhila-jagad-vyāpitva) is indicated, well-attested in the Śruti: "Rudra who is in fire" — and so forth.

      Homage to Śīghriya and to Śībhya; homage
      to Ūrmya and to Avaṣṭhanya; homage to Śrotasya
                  and to Dvīpya.

      Now the thirteenth, fourteenth, and fifteenth [mantras]: "Namaḥ śīghriyāya ca" — He who dwells in swift-flowing waters (śīghragā) is Śīghriya; meaning: "to Him who dwells in such rapid streams." He who dwells in auspicious water currents (śubhe udaka-pravāhe) is Śībhya. He who dwells in waves (ūrmiṣu taraṅgeṣu) is Ūrmya. He who dwells in still water devoid of sound (avaṣkhana — quiet water) is Avaṣkhanya. He who dwells in flowing streams (śrotasi pravāhe) is Śrotasya — Śībha and Śrotas are distinguished by their large and small scale respectively. He who dwells in islands (dvīpe) is Dvīpya.

      Although in a later section [of the Rudra-Namaka] similar expressions such as "namaḥ nāthāya ca vaiśantāya ca" (homage to Him of shallow waters and of small pools), "namaḥ sikatāya ca pravāhāya ca" (homage to Him of sandy beds and flowing streams), and "nama ūrmyāya ca sūryāya ca" (homage to Him of waves and the sun) will describe the Lord's immanence in the various forms of swift, rippling, and wave-filled waters — nonetheless, all the glory of water, declared in the Śruti "Waters, indeed, are everything" (āpo vā idaṃ sarvam) — and so on — belongs entirely to the Supreme Lord who is present as the inner dweller (antaryāmin) of those waters. In order to establish this, what is going to be said later regarding the Lord's immanence in various water-forms

श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    १०७

क्षीति आश्वुः, 'अश्व व्याप्तौ' इति धातोराद्पूर्वादौणादिक
उपत्रयः ; जगव्यापिन इत्यर्थे। तत्र हेतुः— अजिरायेति।
गामतकुशलायेत्यर्थे। अनेन 'यो रुद्रो अग्नौ' इत्यादिश्रुति-
प्रसिद्धं निखिलजगव्यापित्वमुक्तम् ॥

      नमः शीघ्रियाय च शीभ्याय च नम
      ऊर्म्याय चावस्खन्याय च नमः श्रोतस्याय
                  च द्रीप्याय च ॥

चेति। शीघ्रगास्वन्तु भवः शीघ्रियः, तद्वन्तयोमीति यावत्।
शुभे उदकप्रवाहे भवः शीभ्यः, ऊर्मिषु तरङ्गेषु भवः
ऊर्म्यः, अवस्खने ध्वनिरहिते जले भवः अवस्खन्यः, श्रो-
तसि प्रवाहे भवः श्रोतस्यः, शीभश्रोतसोमेहत्वालपत्वाभ्यां
भेदः ; द्रीपे भवः द्रीप्यः ; यद्यपि 'नमो नाथाय च वैश्व-
न्ताय च' 'नमः सिकताय च प्रवाहाय च' 'नम ऊर्म्याय
च सूर्याय च' इत्यादिन्ना शीघ्रशीभ्योर्म्यादिरूपविविधजला-
न्तयोमित्वमप्रकरणे वक्ष्यते, तथापि 'आपो वा इद्र सर्वेम्'
इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धः सर्वोऽप्यपां महिह्मा तद्वन्तयो-

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ स्रोत॒स्या॑य । स्रोत॒स्या॑य च । च द्वीप्या॑य । द्वीप्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | srotasyaya | cha | dvipyaya | cha || 15 ||

|| Thus ends the Fifth Anuvaka. ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Srotasyah'—residing in the current (srotas/flow). 'Dvipyah'—residing on an island (dvipa) in the middle of water. Here, in one single Yajus, two 'cha' particles are recited for the sake of conjunction, to connect the word 'Namah' (salutation) separately to the two words ending in the fourth case. (15)

|| Thus in the Vedartha-prakasha, the commentary on the Yajur-samhita composed by Sayanacharya-Madhava, in the fourth Kanda, fifth Prapathaka, the commentary on the Sri Rudram, ends the Fifth Anuvaka. ||

sanskrit.krama: नमो᳚ ज्ये॒ष्ठाय॑ । ज्ये॒ष्ठाय॑ च । च कनि॒ष्ठाय॑ । कनि॒ष्ठाय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमः॑ पूर्व॒जाय॑ । पूर्व॒जाय॑ च । चापर॒जाय॑ । अ॒पर॒जाय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | purva-jaya | cha | apara-jaya | cha || 2 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Purvajah'—one who was born at the beginning (purva) of the universe in the form of Hiranyagarbha. 'Aparajah'—one born at the end (apara) of the universe in the form of Kalagni (the Fire of Time/Destruction). (2)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah purvajaya cha'—to him born in former times. It means the Unborn (ajata). That existence which precedes all beings is indicated here by 'birth', for there is no primary birth (of God). For it is heard: "Being unborn, some fearful one takes refuge in you." 'Aparajaya cha'—to him born in later times, or existing even after all beings. (2)

Salutation to the middlemost and to the immature.

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | jaghanyaya | cha | budhniyaya | cha || 4 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Jaghanyah'—born in the form of a calf etc. from the rear part (jaghana) of cattle. 'Budhniyah'—born in the form of branches etc. from the root (budhna) of trees etc. (4)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo jaghanyaya cha'—to him existing in the rear/hips (jaghana). [Grammatical note]. 'Budhniyaya cha'—to him existing at the root (budhna). [Grammatical note]. (4)

Salutation to the one in the human world and to the one in the protective thread.

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ सो॒भ्या॑य । सो॒भ्या॑य च । च प्रतिस॒र्या॑य । प्रतिस॒र्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | sobhyaya | cha | pratisaryaya | cha || 5 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Sobhah' is the human world, which exists along with both (sa-ubha) merit and sin. Therefore the followers of the Atharvaveda state: "By merit one wins the world of merit, by sin the world of sin, and by both (the two together) the human world." He who exists there is 'sobhyah'. 'Pratisarah' is the protective thread (raksha-bandha) tied on the hand during weddings etc.; he who is worthy of that is 'pratisaryah'. (5)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah sobhyaya cha'. 'Sobha' is the name of the city of the Gandharvas. As seen in the Mahabharata: "Shalva, presiding over Sobha." This is an indicator (upalakshana) for all cities. To him existing there. [Grammatical note]. Some say: 'Sobha' is that which is with both (sa-ubha) heat and cold, in the form of Indra. To him existing there. 'Pratisaryaya cha'—'pratisara' means contrary movement, or the protective thread. To him existing there. [Grammatical note]. (5)

Salutation to the one in Yama's realm and to the one in the world of security.

Sayana's Commentary: 'Vanyah'—one born in the forest in the form of trees etc. 'Kakshyah'—one born in a thicket (kaksha) in the form of creepers etc. (9)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo vanyaya cha'—existing in the forest in the form of trees etc. 'Kakshyaya cha'—'kakshas' have been explained as forest-thickets; existing there. Creepers etc. are 'kakshya'. These are the distinctions of the presiding deity of the Earth. (9)
