translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | shravaya | cha | prati-shravaya | cha || 10 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Shravah'—the sound (shabda) which is heard. 'Pratishravah'—the echo (pratidhwani). (10)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah shravaya cha'—to him in the form of sound. It is called 'shravah' because it is heard. Or: to him who is heard everywhere, the very famous. [Grammatical note]. 'Pratishravaya cha'—to him in the form of an echo, or in the form of a reply (response). (10)

Salutation to the one with a swift army and to the one with a swift chariot.

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | shrutaya | cha | shruta-senaya | cha || 15 ||

|| Thus ends the Sixth Anuvaka. ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Shrutah'—renowned in the Vedas. 'Shrutasenah'—one whose army (sena) is famous among the panegyrists. (15)

|| Thus in the Vedartha-prakasha, the commentary on the Yajur-samhita composed by Sayanacharya-Madhava, in the fourth Kanda, fifth Prapathaka, the commentary on the Sri Rudram, ends the Sixth Anuvaka. ||

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah shrutaya cha'—to the renowned, or to him famous in the Shrutis (Vedas). As it is said: "You are the Overlord of the universe, the Presider over the universe, the Form of the universe." The idea is that the Shrutis alone are the authority for this. 'Shrutasenaya cha'—to him with a famous army, or an army famous in the Shrutis. Those weapons of yours that were mentioned, like arrows etc., are also famous in the Shrutis. [Grammatical note].

Now for the Purashcharana of these two anuvakas (5th and 6th): having eaten charu for three nights, one should chant continuously thereafter. Within eleven days, one becomes a perfected practitioner. Then the specific rituals are told: for royal prosperity (rajya-shri), one should throw white lotuses on top of the Linga for each salutation eleven times for a month. Prosperity will be obtained. One should feed eleven Brahmins in the presence of the God Dakshinamurti. On the King's birthday, one should offer twenty-one thousand Ashvattha sticks soaked in ghee into the King's fire or a churned fire. Eleven thousand for each of the two (anuvakas). When this is done, the King's victory and longevity increase. For the sake of victory, one should chant a hundred thousand (lakh) times month after month. With these two, when war is near, having offered eleven times for each salutation cooked food from the King's kitchen along with side dishes, curd, honey, and ghee, one should place the remainder on the ground, take it back mixed with water, and throw it toward the enemy army. That army will be destroyed before long. Having offered pure rice soaked in ghee into the fire with these two, one should throw the remainder towards the enemy; he perishes. With these two, having touched all the King's weapons, one should consecrate them a thousand times; their brilliance increases. With these two alone, one should throw (objects) toward the enemy army; that army will be destroyed for a long time.
...

      to Ghṛṣṇave and to Pramṛśa; homage to Dūta and to

      homage to Tīkṣṇeṣave and to Āyudhine; homage to Svāyu-
      dha and to Sudhanvane; homage to Srutyā and to
      Pathyā; homage to Kāśya and to Nīpya;
      homage to Sūdya and to Sarasya; homage to Nā-
      dya and to Vaiśanta; homage to Kūpya and to
      Avaṭya; homage to Varṣya and to Avarṣya;
      homage to Meghya and to Vidyutya; homage to
      Īdhriya and to Ātapya; homage to Vātya and to
      Rebhya; homage to Vāstavya and to
      Vāstupa.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
सप्तमोऽनुवाकः ॥

                         ———

      नमो दुन्दुभ्याय चाहनन्याय च नमो
      घृष्णवे च प्रमृशाय च नमो दूताय च
      प्रहिताय च नमो निषङ्गिणे चेषुधिमते च
      नमस्तीक्ष्णेषवे चायुधिने च नमः स्वायु-
      धाय च सुधन्वने च नमः स्रुत्याय च
      पथ्याय च नमः काश्याय च नीप्याय च
      नमः सूद्याय च सरस्याय च नमो ना-
      याय च वैशन्ताय च नमः कूप्याय चा-
      वट्याय च नमो वर्ष्याय चावर्ष्याय च
      नमो मेघ्याय च विद्युत्याय च नमः ई-
      ध्रियायें चातप्याय च नमो बाल्याय च
      रेघिमयाय च नमो वास्तव्याय च वा-
      स्तुपाय च ॥

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ धृ॒ष्णवे॑ । धृ॒ष्णवे॑ च । च प्रमृ॒शाय॑ । प्रमृ॒शाय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | dhrishnave | cha | pramrishaya | cha || 2 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Dhrishnuh'—one who does not flee in battle. 'Pramrishah'—one who examines (paramarshaka) the essential reality of the enemy's army. (2)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo dhrishnave cha'—to him whose nature is to overpower. Or: to him who is skilled in protecting the entire universe. [Grammatical note]. Even though powerful, he is without partiality towards any particular field—'pramrishaya cha'—to the one skilled in discerning (examining) everything, the wise (pandita). [Grammatical note]. Another says: 'pramrishah' means one of distinguished character. For all indeed worship the God for their own desires, or for well-being, or for supreme liberation; the God, considering that as his own purpose, protects them. (2)

Salutation to the messenger and to the envoy.

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ दू॒ताय॑ । दू॒ताय॑ च । च प्रहि॑ताय । प्रहि॑ताय च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | dutaya | cha | prahitaya | cha || 3 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Dutah'—one skilled in conveying information (news). 'Prahitah'—a person sent (preshi-tah) by his Master. (3)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: What more? He protects his devotees even by performing tasks like being a messenger. 'Namo dutaya cha'—a 'duta' is a messenger (sandesha-hara); to him who sometimes exists in that form out of love for his devotees, or to him who acts as the messenger for the gods in the form of Agni (fire). As is heard: "We choose Agni as the messenger," "Agni was the messenger of the gods," "You are the messenger, the seer, the sentient." 'Prahitaya cha'—to the one who is sent. Out of love for his devotees alone, he sometimes takes that form as well. Or: with reference to Agni, as the sacrificer is sent (prahita) towards the gods (through him). [Grammatical note]. Or: 'prahitah' means one who is extremely beneficial (prakrishtena hitah). (3)

Salutation to the one with a sword and to the one with a quiver.

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ निष॒ङ्गिणे॑ । निष॒ङ्गिणे॑ च । चेषुधि॒मते॑ । इषुधि॒मते॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. Tri. Am.] Rudradhyaya. 37

...the Rudra exists thus (in all things). To contemplate the universal selfhood of Rudra, it is spoken thus in the mantras. In the bodies of thieves etc., Rudra exists in two ways: in the form of the individual soul (jiva) and in the form of the Lord (Ishwara). There, the form of the individual soul is the expressed meaning (vachya-artha) of the words 'thief' (stena) etc. That alone is censured in the scriptures. The form of the Lord is indicated (lakshya) by the words 'thief' etc. The contemplation of that (Lordship) is the supreme goal of life as it is the cause for the destruction of sins. With the intention of conveying the indicated meaning, worldly words are used in the mantras—this is how it should be seen. Through the expressed meaning which acts as an indicator, the indicated meaning can be easily grasped even by the simple-minded. Just as in the expression "the moon is on the tip of the branch." Therefore, even when the indicated meaning is intended, words expressing the primary meaning must be used as a door to the primary understanding. (2)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah kakubhaya'—to the chief, the elevated one; or he who shines on the hump (kakuda) of the bull. [Grammatical note]. 'Nishangine'—to the expert in sword-striking. 'Stenanam pataye namah'—'stenas' are thieves whose bodies are hidden, who steal wealth. [Etymology]. Out of love for those who take refuge in him, even among thieves, he protects them as their Lord by removing their sins. Or: he is established in the midst of those who commit theft; there is no one equal to him in committing 'theft' (i.e., his mysterious taking away of things). (2)

He speaks the third Yajus:
Salutation to the one with a sword and a quiver, salutation to the lord of robbers. (3)

[Word-for-word]: Namah | nishangine | ishudhimate | iti | ishu-dhi-mate | taskaranam | pataye | namah || 3 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Nishanga' is an arrow held in the hand to be placed on the bow. 'Ishudhi' is the container of arrows bound on the back. Salutation to him who has both. 'Taskarah' are manifest (open) thieves; salutation to their protector. (3)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo nishangine'—to the one having constant attachment (sakti) to his devotees. 'Ishudhimate'—'nishanga' and 'ishudhi' have been explained; to him who has both. The word 'nishangi' is mentioned again to establish the possession of both. Or: 'nishanga' is extreme attachment. Carrying the desire for theft and the quiver. 'Taskaranam pataye namah'—those who reside in forests etc. and steal openly, like those who rob caravans, etc., are 'taskaras'. [Grammatical note on 'taskara']. (3)
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
---
                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    88
---

[Mantra 4:]
Obeisance to the one who deceives, to the one who thoroughly deceives, to the Lord of sneaky pilferers (stayus) — obeisance.

Now the fourth [commentary]: — "Namo vanchata" (Obeisance to the deceiver). Those who, while being considered as one's own people, having gained the trust of others, steal their goods — they are the stayavah (sneaky pilferers); obeisance to their Lord. In order to establish the Lord's lordship over sneaky pilferers, the two qualifying epithets "vanchate" (deceiver) and "parivanchate" (thorough deceiver) are given: one who, while appearing as a trustworthy person in others' affairs and in transactions of buying and selling of goods belonging to others, steals something here and there — he is called a "vanchashrita" (partial deceiver); one who commits deception in all transactions is called a "parivanchashrita" (complete deceiver); [the Lord appears in] such forms — this is the meaning. Thus ends the fourth.

[Mantra 5:]
Obeisance to the one who roams quietly, to the one who roams on all sides, to the Lord of forest-dwellers (aranyanas) — obeisance.

Now the fifth [commentary]: — "Namo nicerava" (Obeisance to the quiet roamer). Those who live in forests are the aranyavasi (forest-dwellers), and of these aranyanahs, obeisance to their Lord — this is what is being established. [The text] supports this very point with: "Namo nicerave paricara" — one who by nature roams quietly (nicerava) [in forests], surpassing [in stealth] even the forest-dwelling thieves; and "paricara" — one who roams in all directions to steal [more than them]; the idea being that since He is supreme among them by virtue of being a more skilled roamer than even those [forest thieves], it is fitting that He be their lord. Thus ends the fifth.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
---
८८                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।
---

स्यापरिहार्यत्वादिल्याशङ्कथ, भक्तापराधसह्नस्वभावे भगवति
नेयं शङ्केत्याह— सहमानायेति । इति प्रथमः ॥

नमः ककुभाय निषङ्गिणे स्तेनानां पत-
ये नमः ॥

अथ द्वितीयः— नमः ककुभायेति । ककुभाय महते,
निषङ्गिणे खड्गवते, स्तेनानां गुप्तचोराणां स्वामिने नमः ।
अत्र ककुभशब्दवाच्यमहत्त्वस्य प्रतियोग्याकाङ्कायाम्, स्व-
वाक्योपात्तस्तेनानासमेव तत्प्रतियोगित्वेलसान्वयः; तथा च स्ते-
नेषु महत्त्वं स्वेनस्वामित्वे हेतुः ककुभाय इत्यनेन चोच्यते ।
निषङ्गिणे इत्यनेन स्तेनकियापेक्षितोपकरणसंपत्तिः प्रदर्श्य-
ते । इति द्वितीयः ॥

नमो निषङ्गिण इषुधिमते तस्कराणां
पतये नमः ॥

अथ तृतीयः— नमो निषङ्गिण इति । धनुषि संबाहुं
हस्ते धृतो बाणः निषङ्गः, पृष्ठे बद्धो वाणाधारः इषुधिः,
तदुभययुक्ताय; तस्कराणां प्रकाशचोराणां स्वामिते नमः ।
अत्र तस्कराधिपत्सानुगुणरूपप्रदर्शनार्थत्वेन विशेषणं संगय-
यितव्ये । इति तृतीयः ॥

sanskrit.krama: नम॑स्ती॒क्ष्णेष॑वे । ती॒क्ष्णेष॑वे च । चायु॒धिने॑ । आ॒यु॒धिने॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | tikshna-ishave | cha | ayudhine | cha || 5 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Tikshneshuh'—one whose arrows (ishu) are sharp (tikshna). 'Ayudhi'—one who possesses many weapons. (5)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah tikshneshave cha'—to him with sharpened arrows. 'Ayudhine cha'—to him with many or with excellent (praiseworthy) weapons. [Grammatical note]. (5)

Salutation to the one with good weapons and to the one with a good bow.

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ स्वायु॒धाय॑ । स्वायु॒धाय॑ च । च सु॒धन्व॑ने । सु॒धन्व॑ने च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | su-ayudhaya | cha | su-dhanvane | cha || 6 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Svayudhah'—one whose weapon (ayudha) is beautiful (shobhana), in the form of a trident (trishula) etc. 'Sudhanva'—one whose bow (dhanus) is beautiful, in the form of the Pinaka. (6)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah svayudhaya cha'—to him with a beautiful weapon, or to him with an unfailing (amogha) weapon. [Grammatical note]. Or: 'svayudhah' is one who possesses weapons of a beautiful form. Furthermore, because you alone are worthy of them, they are beautiful. As it is said: "You shine with arrows and the bow." 'Sudhanvane'—to him with an excellent bow. It is mentioned separately because of its importance, or to show its extreme excellence—because it is made of gold. [Grammatical note]. (6)

Salutation to the one in the foot-paths and to the one in the main roads.

translations.sayana:
[Na. Na. A.] Rudradhyaya | 77

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Kapardi' is one with matted hair (jata-bandha). 'Pulastih' is one who stands in front of devotees. || 3 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Kapardine'. (Explained before). To 'Pulastaye'. 'Pulastayah' means having long hair (kuntala); salutations to the one who has that. The possessive suffix is dropped. At the time of grace, the Lord sometimes comes with matted hair and sometimes with long hair. Others say: 'Pulastih' means one who always stands in front of everyone. The form is derived from 'pru-shodara' etc. This shows that even though He is of such great majesty, He is always easily accessible. || 3 ||

Salutations to the one in the cowshed (Goshthyaya) and to the one in the house (Grihyaya). || 4 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Goshtham' is the place for cows; He who is in that is 'Goshthyah'. 'Grihyah' is He who is in the house. || 4 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: To further elaborate on the Lord's accessibility, He says: Salutations to 'Goshthyaya'. 'Goshtham' is the place where cows stay. To the one who exists there. To 'Grihyaya', the one who exists in the house. || 4 ||

Salutations to the one on the couch (Talpyaya) and to the one in the mansion (Gehyaya). || 5 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Talpyah' is one lying on a bed or couch (khatva). 'Gehyah' is one who exists in a house or palace (prasada). || 5 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Talpyaya'. 'Talpam' is a bed/couch etc.; He who exists there. To 'Gehyaya'. 'Geham' is a palace etc.; He who exists there. || 5 ||

Salutations to the one in the dense forest (Katyaya) and to the one who stands in the mountain cave (Gahvareshthaya). || 6 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Katam' is a thorny forest or a place full of creepers, making it difficult to enter; a dense and inaccessible forest is 'katam', and He who exists in that is 'Katyah'. 'Gahvareshthah' is one who stands in uneven places like mountain caves. || 6 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Katyaya'. This is explained. Or He graces the world in the form of water in a well ('kata'). To 'Gahvareshthaya'. To the one who stands in mountain caves. (Grammatical explanation for 'shthah' and 'shatvam'). || 6 ||

sanskrit.krama: नम॒-स्सूद्या॑य । सूद्या॑य च । च सर॒स्या॑य । सर॒स्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमो॑ ना॒द्याय॑ । ना॒द्याय॑ च । च वैश॒न्ताय॑ । वैश॒न्ताय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:

'Vaishantas' are those (ponds) made by the gods (natural), where water enters for the purpose of pleasing the deities etc. [Grammatical note]. (10)

Salutation to the one in the rain and to the one not in the rain.

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | vatyaya | cha | reshmiyaya | cha || 15 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Vatyah'—one seen along with the wind (vata). 'Reshma' is the time of dissolution (pralaya) where beings perish (rishyanti); he who exists there in the form of a specific type of rainwater mixed with gravel, stones, etc., is 'reshmiyah'. (15)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah vatyaya cha'—to him existing in rains produced by the wind. 'Reshmiyaya cha'—'reshma' is the end of an age (yuganta) when a wind named Samvarta blows, raining stones and gravel; that is 'reshma'. Some say it refers to the summer season. Others say 'reshma' are the dust clouds raised by the wind. To him existing there. [Grammatical note]. (15)

Salutation to the one in the wealth and to the one who protects the site.

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ वास्त॒व्या॑य । वास्त॒व्या॑य च । च वास्तु॒पाय॑ । वास्तु॒पाय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | vastavyaya | cha | vastupaya | iti | vastu-paya | cha || 16 ||

|| Thus ends the Seventh Anuvaka. ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Vastu' is wealth in the form of cattle, horses, and other objects; he who exists there as those objects is 'vastavyah'. 'Vastu' is land for building a house; its protector is 'vastupah'. (16)

|| Thus in the Vedartha-prakasha, the commentary on the Yajur-samhita composed by Sayanacharya-Madhava, in the fourth Kanda, fifth Prapathaka, the commentary on the Sri Rudram, ends the Seventh Anuvaka. ||

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah vastavyaya cha'. 'Vastu' is wealth like cattle, horses, etc.; to him existing as those effects. Or: to him existing in the building-site (vastu). 'Vastupaya cha'—one who protects (pa-ti) the site. [Grammatical note]. Or to the Master of the dwelling. As it is said: "Salutation to Rudra, the Lord of the Dwelling (vastoshpati)." (16)

Now for the Purashcharana of this anuvaka: having fasted for one day and chanting eight hundred times, one becomes a perfected practitioner. By this, one desiring the control of the four castes should offer ten thousand sticks of Ashvattha, Udumbara, Vata, and Plaksha wood, soaked in curd, honey, and ghee; they will then be in his control. One desiring intelligence (medha) should offer five thousand oblations of Kapila-cow ghee; he becomes intelligent. One desiring nourishment (pushti) should offer nine thousand cakes (apupa). One desiring health...
...

Sayana's Commentary: 'Tamrah'—the extremely red one at the time of rising in the form of the Sun. 'Arunah'—the slightly red one after rising. (2)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah tamraya charunaya cha'. These two regarding the Sun have been explained. As the Sun, the God graces the world by granting awakening etc., and directly witnesses the actions of beings. (2)

translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    117

"Thus says the Śruti" — in accord with all these supporting testimonies and consistent with those corroborations. Not only does the Lord's association with Umā account for His role as the bestower of liberation alone — but indeed all His greatness: omniscience, omnipotence (sarveśvaratva), His inner presence in all (sarvāntaryāmitva), His being the cause of everything (sarvaikāraṇatva), and so forth — all this glory is but the play of the supreme power (para-śakti-vilāsa). Thus the Śrutis proclaim: "His supreme power is heard of as manifold and various — natural, consisting of knowledge, strength, and action" — and so forth. The Itihāsas and Purāṇas elaborate upon this, and the learned affirm: "Power is the body of this deity; it is the inner Self, knowledge, action, and the instrument — the cluster of desires, the abode, the coverings — all this is You, O crowned one with tender shoots [Bālaśaṭhāṅkumau, epithet for Śiva]. What is there that you are not, O dwelling place of all?" And: "If the deity were not thus [united with Śakti], truly He would not be capable of even a quiver." Therefore, since the immense glory of such supreme power of Umā is precisely the reason why the Taittirīya Upaniṣad, having used the term "abhīkāpataye" (to the lord of the beloved one), further employs the term "amāpataye" (to the husband of Amā, i.e., Umā) — whereby the Lord is saluted in His capacity as the Lord of Umā — this much for now.

      Homage to Tāmra and to Aruṇa.

      Now the second mantra — in which the Lord is shown to be the sole object of worship for all, being directly perceptible in the form of Āditya (the Sun), the guide and lord of all, and the bestower of all auspiciousness: "Namo tāmrāya" — At the time of sunrise, [the solar orb appears] copper-coloured (tāmra) in its interior; thereafter, slightly reddish (aruṇa); and from the conjunctive particle "ca," it takes on many more colours above. Thus —

न खलु कुशलः स्पन्दितुमपि' इति च। अत एव खलु ताद-
क्षमहिह्मप्रयुक्कायाश्रुमायासाद्राविश्यादेव तैत्तिरीयोपनिषदि

translations.abhinava_shankara:
Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    119

---

"bhaya ca arunaya ca" (to the fearsome and to the ruddy one) was declared. How supremely compassionate is this Rudropanishad (the Upanishad pertaining to Rudra)!

           Namah shanghaya ca pashupataye ca
           (Salutation to the bringer of happiness and to the Lord of all beings)

Now, to show that the Supreme Lord alone is worthy of worship by all who seek happiness, on account of His being the bestower of happiness — this is the third mantra: "Namah shanghaya" etc. Shangha means one who leads to or bestows happiness (sham sukham gamayati prapaayatiiti shanghah). The reason for this is stated by the word pashupataye: He is the master and protector (pati, palaka) of all creatures (pashu), from Brahma down to the immovable. Thus the Lord alone is the cause of happiness for the entire world, because whatever happiness belongs to those who are to be nurtured (palaniya) depends upon the one who nurtures (palaka) — such is the meaning.

           Namo ughraya ca bhimaya ca
           (Salutation to the supreme one and to the terrifying one)

Now, to show that the Supreme Lord alone, being superior to all the worlds, is worthy of worship by all — this is the fourth mantra: "Namo ughraya" etc. Ughra means the highest or supreme. By the Unadi grammatical rule "kaprendradu..." with the suffix ran, the derivation yields the meaning of one who is supremely excellent. Indeed, in the mantra "You are ughra (supreme); may I be ughra among my kinsmen," the word ughra is employed in the sense of highest praise. Since no other being of that same category (sajata) possessing comparable supremacy exists, by the principle of the absence of restriction (sankocabhava-nyaya), the nature of being supremely above all — namely being superior to the entire universe — is established. The reason for this is stated by: "bhimaya" (the terrifying one). Bhima means fearsome, from the Shruti: "Out of fear of Him, Vata (wind) blows" — and so forth. Thus, even toward the great Indra, Agni and others, the Lord's fearsome quality establishes His supremacy over all — such is the meaning.

सुखं गमयित प्रापयतीति शंग:, तत्र हेतु:— पशुपतय
इति । पशूनां ब्रह्मादिस्थावरान्तानां पति: पाळक: । तथा

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | agre-vadhaya | cha | dure-vadhaya | cha || 5 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Agrevadhah'—one whose killing (vadha) occurs in front (agre). Similarly, 'durevadhah' is one whose killing occurs from a distance. (5)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah agrevadhaya cha'—one who, standing in front (facing), kills. [Grammatical note]. Or: one who kills those desiring to kill, even before they act. 'Durevadhaya cha'—to the one who kills those even at a distance; to the one who kills the wicked even across a long span of time. (5)

Salutation to the killer and to the excessive killer.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
120                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam

---

"from whom Vata (wind) blows out of fear" — so the Shruti says. Thus, by being terrifying even toward the great Indra, Agni and others, the Lord controls and governs them all, and this establishes His supremacy — such is the meaning.

           Namo agrevadha ya ca durevadha ya ca
           (Salutation to Him who strikes the enemy who stands before and to Him who strikes the enemy who stands afar)

Now, to show that the Supreme Lord alone — being the cause of victory in all battles — is worthy of service by all who seek protection — this is the fifth mantra: "Namo agrevadhaya" etc. Agrevadha is He who, having entered the field of battle, stands in front of His own devotee and slays the devotee's enemies before him. Durevadhah is He who, even before battle has commenced, breaks the strength and valor of those who obstruct His devotee's path, even when they are stationed far away. The quality of being Agrevadha (one who strikes from the front) was stated by Arjuna in the Drona Parva (of the Mahabharata): "In the battle, while scattering the enemy forces with showers of arrows, I beheld before me a blazing Person, resplendent as fire, hurling a blazing spear. In whatever direction He proceeded, the enemies were shattered in that direction, O great-minded one. After He had burned all the enemies, I then pursued and slew them. The people thought that the army, routed and fleeing, was conquered by me." This same meaning is narrated in the Ashramanik section: "From the preceding night of Arjuna's prowess onward, the Lord smote the army with His trident, striking against what faced Arjuna. O great king, who could challenge that army, even in thought — guarded as it was by Drona, Karna, and Kripa with their mighty bows, and by Maheshvara Himself?" The quality of Durevadhah (one who strikes from afar) was declared by Krishna to Arjuna in the Mokshadharma (section of the Mahabharata):

१२०                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ ह॒न्त्रे । ह॒न्त्रे च । च हनी॑यसे । हनी॑यसे च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | hantre | cha | haniyase | cha || 6 ||

Sayana's Commentary: It means he who kills an opponent without effort, whether that opponent is in front or far away. In the world also, whoever kills an opponent, he (the God) alone is the killer in that form. Therefore, the Lord said to Arjuna:
"These have already been slain by me; be you a mere instrument, O left-handed archer (Savyasachin)."
'Haniyan' is the excessive killer of all at the time of destruction (samhara). (6)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah hantre cha'—to the self of whoever indeed kills; the idea is that every killer kills through your power. 'Haniyase cha'—to the excessive killer. [Grammatical note]. One whose power exceeds all, by which you alone deserve to be the Lord of beings (Pashupati). (6)

Salutation to the trees with green leaves.

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ वृ॒क्षेभ्यः॑ । वृ॒क्षेभ्यो॒ हरि॑केशेभ्यः । हरि॑केशेभ्य इति॑ हरि-केशेभ्यः ॥

translations.sayana:
26  Sayanacharya—Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya-Sahita— [Na. Dvi. Am.]

[Word-for-word]: Namah | vrikshebhyah | hari-keshebhyah | pashunam | pataye | namah || 2 ||

Sayana's Commentary: Trees whose hair-like leaves are green (harita) in color are 'harikesha'. Salutation be to those trees which are the tree-shaped forms of Rudra. Salutation to Rudra who is the protector of cattle (pashu). (2)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: Salutation to those who are in the form of trees, or who reside in trees. The word 'tree' points to the resident by the container, as in "the platforms are shouting" (meaning people on them). 'Harikeshebhyah'—those who have leaves in the place of hair that are green, or those whose hair is green. Or: 'Vriksha'—all beings from Brahma down to a blade of grass, grown in the Cosmic Egg (Brahmanda), are indeed 'trees'. Derived from the root 'vriksh' (to cut/destroy); the meaning is those who are subject to destruction. Salutation to him who is the self of those. 'Harikeshebhyah'—to him with green-colored hair. Salutation to the Lord of cattle (pashu); to him who exists as the Master of those cattle belonging to him, whether two-legged or four-legged. As it is said: "Of which cattle Pashupati is the Lord, the four-legged and the two-legged."
And in the Purana: "All from Brahma down to the stationary things are proclaimed as 'Pashu' (tethered beings). Because he is their Lord, Bhavah is remembered as Pashupati."
In the Mahabharata: "Because he always protects the 'Pashus' and delights with them, and because he is their Overlord, he is remembered as Pashupati." (2)

He speaks the third Yajus:
Salutation to the one who is golden-red like young grass, to the radiant one, salutation to the lord of paths. (3)

[Word-for-word]: Namah | saspinjaraya | tvishi-mate | pathinam | pataye | namah || 3 ||

Sayana's Commentary: The word 'saspinja' refers to young grass. 'Pinjara' is a mixed yellow-red color. One who is of a mixed color like young grass is 'saspinjara'. He is 'tvishiman'—radiant/shining...
...
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                   73

---

— referring to the triple-quality (triguṇa) karma-kāṇḍa (the ritual section of the Vedas) characterised by leaves in the form of Vedic metres (chandas), as [indicated by the Vedic text]: "The Vedas deal with the subjects of the three guṇas," and "Leaves are its hymns (chandāṃsi yasya parṇāni)." Just as in the case of a tree, leaves serve to protect it, so the Vedic metres (chandas) that are of the nature of karma-kāṇḍa serve to protect the tree of saṃsāra, since they perpetuate worldly existence by inculcating action. And these, on account of their infinite abundance surpassing the understanding of the head (śiras), are described as hair (keśa). Thus the portion of the mantra "namo vṛkṣebhyo hirikeśebhyaḥ" is concluded in meaning as: salutation to the Supreme Lord in his aspect as the tree of saṃsāra, intertwined with the threefold web of karmic activity. Through this, the two questions — how [creatures] have pāśutva (the condition of being paśu, bound), and how [the Lord] has patitva (the condition of being pati, lord) — are both answered: creatures are paśu by virtue of being bound by the ropes of saṃsāra (saṃsārapāśa), and the Supreme Lord is pati of those [creatures] by virtue of causing them to perform their respective functions. This has been clearly amplified by some: "From Brahmā to the stationary beings, all are the paśus (bound livestock) of the trident-bearing Lord; they are called paśus because they are under the sway of saṃsāra. Because of his lordship over them, the Lord — indeed Śiva — is known as Paśupati (lord of the bound). Mahāśvara has bound the paśus from Brahmā to the pillar. By these bonds (pāśa), the Lord God himself causes them to perform their work. He alone is their liberator, when properly worshipped with devotion." Thus have similar statements from the Skānda, Śaiva and Kūrma Purāṇas been cited. Here, by the statement "from Brahmā to the stationary" it is explained that in the mantra "Eṣāmīśe" (the lord of these), the two-footed and four-footed [beings] serve as indicative of the entire manifest world, composed of conscious and inert beings.

sanskrit.krama: नम॑स्ता॒राय॑ । ता॒रायेति॑ ताराय ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | taraya || 8 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Tarah'—the subject matter expressed by the Pranava (Om). (8)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah taraya'. He who carries (tarayati) beings across the ocean of worldly existence is 'tarah'. [Grammatical note]. As the Jabala Upanishad says: "Here indeed, when the life-breath of a being is departing, Rudra explains the saving (taraka) Brahman." Salutation to him. Or: to him in the form of the Pranava. (8)

Salutation to the source of welfare and to the source of delight.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    123

---

"From the Sun (Aditya), from whom it arose, the bilva tree sprang up" — thus the glory arising from the leaf-nature (of the bilva), just as from its sacredness, is proclaimed in the Puranas. Likewise other trees may also be cited as examples.

           Namastara ya
           (Salutation to the Deliverer)

Now, to declare that the Lord alone is to be taken refuge in not only as the means to heaven but also as the means to liberation (moksha) — this is the eighth mantra: "Namastara ya." The Tara is the Pranava (the sacred syllable Om); salutation to Him who is of that form. For it is heard in the Atharvashiras (Upanishad): "He is the Om, the Deliverer (tarah)" — having introduced this — "He alone is Rudra, He is Ishana (the Ruler)." Similarly, in the Taittiriya (Upanishad): "May Brahman be auspicious (shiva) to me; Om, ever-auspicious one (sadashiva)." The Lord's being of the form of the Pranava amounts to the same as His being the subject matter to be conveyed by the Pranava; as the Shivapurana states: "Shiva indeed is the Pranava, or the Pranava is called Shiva. The difference between the denoted object (vachya) and the denoting word (vachaka) is nowhere absolute." And from the Shruti: "He who is beyond that which has merged into its primordial cause — He is the great Lord (Maheshvara)."

           Namah shambhave ca mayobhave ca
           (Salutation to the source of blessedness and to the source of joy)

Now, just as the drops of water in the ocean of the supreme bliss of the Self (paramatman) — which transcends the limit of the highest sacrifice — compare to all the worldly joys of creatures, so too the Lord is to be taken refuge in by all devotees. This is shown in the ninth mantra: "Namah shambhave" etc. Shambi-

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | shivaya | cha | shiva-taraya | cha || 11 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Shivah'—of the form of welfare (kalyana); meaning he is himself stainless. 'Shivatarah'—excessively auspicious/stainless. (11)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah shivaya cha'. Shiva is the stainless one, the one without qualities (nirguna). Salutation to the very famous Shiva who is welfare in all ways. 'Shivataraya cha'—the word 'shiva' is also used figuratively for things that cause welfare such as prosperity and liberation. For example: "Auspicious (shiva) is the path, auspicious is the means." Likewise, the God is called 'Shiva' because he is the maker of welfare (shivankara). As the Atharvashikha Upanishad says: "Shiva alone is to be meditated upon, the maker of welfare (shivankara), abandoning all else."
And in the Mahabharata:
"Because he always accomplishes all goals in all actions, desiring the welfare (shiva) of men, therefore the God is remembered as Shiva."
He who is excessively auspicious is 'shivatarah' or 'shivankarah'. Salutation to him who produces welfare in various ways. (11)

Salutation to the one in the holy fords and to the one on the banks.

sanskrit.krama: नम॒स्तीर्थ्या॑य । तीर्थ्या॑य च । च कूल्या॑य । कूल्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

१२६                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

---

करत्वेनाविष्कृतम् । सोऽयमखिलश्रुतिहारायमाणहृद्रोपनिष-
द्रायकमणि: महामन्त्र: । यदाहु:— 'विद्यासु श्रुतिशतकुठा-
ह्रद्रेकादिशनी श्रुतौ । तत्र पञ्चाक्षरी तस्यां शिव इत्यक्षर-
द्वयम्' इति । जीवब्रह्मिति च शिवशब्दस्य आगमेषु व्य-
वहार: । सर्वाण्येव शिवपुराणवचनानि एतनमाहत्त्यप्रमा-
णत्वेनोद्धारणीयानीति संक्षेप: ।।

           नमस्तीर्थ्याय च कूल्याय च नम: पा-
           र्घाय चावार्याय च नम: प्रतरणाय चोत्त-
           र्णाय च नम आतार्याय चालाद्याय च
           नम: शष्प्याय च फेन्याय च नम: सिक-
           त्याय च प्रवाह्याय च ॥

   तदेवम् 'नम: सोमाय' इत्यादिभिरेतदन्तैर्मन्त्रैरखिलो-
पास्तवधमर्महेतुविशिष्टतया भगवाचमस्कृत: । अधान्तयोमि-
लेन च नमस्करोति नमस्तीर्थ्यायेत्यादिभि:— तीर्थेषु ग-
ङ्गादिषु भव: तीर्थ्य:, कूलेषु भव: कूल्य:, नद्यादीनां परं
तीरं पारम्, तत्र भव: पार्य:, अर्वाक्तीरम् अवारम्, तत्र
भव: अवार्य:, प्रकृष्टेन मार्गेण पापवरणहेतु: प्रतरण:,
तवोऽप्युत्कृष्टेन ज्ञानमार्गेण अविधातरणहेतु: उत्तरण: इति

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ पा॒र्या॑य । पा॒र्या॑य च । चावा॒र्या॑य । अवा॒र्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | paryaya | cha | avaryaya | cha || 13 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Paryah'—he who exists on the far shore (para-tire) of the ocean of worldly existence (Samsara), as the object of meditation for those seeking liberation. 'Avaryah'—he who exists on the near shore (avara-tire), in the midst of Samsara, as the granter of desired fruits. (13)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah paryaya cha'—to him existing on the far shore (para-tire) of rivers etc. [Grammatical note]. 'Avaryaya cha'—to him existing on the near shore (avara-tire). Someone says: "Tirthas and the like are with respect to Samsara." Tirthas are the holy assemblies (samavayas) like the Shaivas etc.; he exists in those. Staying there, he graces the world. To him existing on the banks, during times (of transition) etc. The far shore (para) is pure; the near shore (avara) is impure. (13)

Salutation to the crosser and to the deliverer.

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ प्र॒तर॑णाय । प्र॒तर॑णाय च । चो॒त्तर॑णाय । उ॒त्तर॑णाय च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | pra-taranaya | cha | ut-taranaya | cha || 14 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Prataranah'—the cause of crossing over (tarana) sins through excellent (prakrishta) means like mantra-chanting. 'Uttaranah'—the cause of crossing out of (uttarana) this worldly existence through the form of essential knowledge (tattva-jnana). (14)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namah prataranaya cha'. 'Taranam' is living or crossing. Excellence is birth in higher wombs. [Grammatical note]. He is the means for crossing over Samsara. To the cause of crossing over with excellence in this world. 'Uttaranaya cha'—to the cause of crossing out of (exiting) this world. [Grammatical note]. (14)

Salutation to the one on the stepping-stones and to the one on the stairs.

sanskrit.krama: नम॑ आता॒र्या॑य । आता॒र्या॑य च । चाला॒द्या॑य । आला॒द्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | a-taryaya | cha | a-labyaya | cha || 15 ||
...

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
१२६                    श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।

---

करत्वेनाविष्कृतम् । सोऽयमखिलश्रुतिहारायमाणहृद्रोपनिष-
द्रायकमणि: महामन्त्र: । यदाहु:— 'विद्यासु श्रुतिशतकुठा-
ह्रद्रेकादिशनी श्रुतौ । तत्र पञ्चाक्षरी तस्यां शिव इत्यक्षर-
द्वयम्' इति । जीवब्रह्मिति च शिवशब्दस्य आगमेषु व्य-
वहार: । सर्वाण्येव शिवपुराणवचनानि एतनमाहत्त्यप्रमा-
णत्वेनोद्धारणीयानीति संक्षेप: ।।

           नमस्तीर्थ्याय च कूल्याय च नम: पा-
           र्घाय चावार्याय च नम: प्रतरणाय चोत्त-
           र्णाय च नम आतार्याय चालाद्याय च
           नम: शष्प्याय च फेन्याय च नम: सिक-
           त्याय च प्रवाह्याय च ॥

   तदेवम् 'नम: सोमाय' इत्यादिभिरेतदन्तैर्मन्त्रैरखिलो-
पास्तवधमर्महेतुविशिष्टतया भगवाचमस्कृत: । अधान्तयोमि-
लेन च नमस्करोति नमस्तीर्थ्यायेत्यादिभि:— तीर्थेषु ग-
ङ्गादिषु भव: तीर्थ्य:, कूलेषु भव: कूल्य:, नद्यादीनां परं
तीरं पारम्, तत्र भव: पार्य:, अर्वाक्तीरम् अवारम्, तत्र
भव: अवार्य:, प्रकृष्टेन मार्गेण पापवरणहेतु: प्रतरण:,
तवोऽप्युत्कृष्टेन ज्ञानमार्गेण अविधातरणहेतु: उत्तरण: इति

sanskrit.krama: नम॒-श्शष्प्या॑य । शष्प्या॑य च । च फेन्या॑य । फेन्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमः॑ सिक॒त्या॑य । सिक॒त्या॑य च । च प्रवा॒ह्या॑य । प्रवा॒ह्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. A. A.] Rudradhyaya | 79

He who deserves/is among those who return again to the world (Samsara) is 'Ataryah'. The 'Aladah' is the Jiva (individual soul) who completely ('alam') experiences the fruits of its actions. According to the Shruti: "Of the two, the other eats the sweet pippala fruit." Because He (Rudra) is the prompter and is related to that (Jiva), He is called 'Aladyah'. || 15 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Ataryaya'. 'Atarah' are those who return again to the world due to the remnants of their actions, even after reaching the shore of the ocean of Samsara. By the rule of 'bahulam' (variety), the suffix 'yat' is applied. He who exists there to grace them is Ataryah. And to 'Aladyaya'. 'Aladah' refers to the cycles of creation. 'Aladah' is the wandering in the ocean of Samsara in the form of whirlpools again and again. In the root 'lanj', 'n' is replaced by 'd' by letter-substitution. Or 'ladir' is another root. He who exists in that is Aladyah. Some say: 'Aladah' is in heaven, for it surpasses other happiness. || 15 ||

Salutations to the one in the young grass (Shashpyaya) and to the one in the foam (Phenyaya). || 16 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Shashpam' refers to young grass, kusha sprouts, etc., growing on the banks of the Ganga; He who deserves that is 'Shashpyah'. He who deserves the foam in the middle of the river is 'Phenyah'. || 16 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Shashpyaya'. Shashpa is sacred grass like Kusha and Darbha; He who exists there. To 'Phenyaya'; He who exists in the foam. || 16 ||

Salutations to the one in the sands (Sikatyaya) and to the one in the flow (Pravahyaya). || 17 ||

|| Thus ends the Eighth Anuvaka ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: He who deserves the sand is 'Sikatyah'. He who deserves the flow (of the river) is 'Pravahyah'. The underlying meaning of these four terms (Shashpya etc.) is that He is in the form of the faithful person who constantly remains on the banks of the Ganga, etc., for purposes like bathing. || 17 ||

Thus ends the Eighth Anuvaka in the Shri Rudra Bhashya of the Yajur-Samhita, Fourth Kanda, Fifth Prapathaka, in the Madhaviya Vedartha Prakasha composed by Sayanacharya. || 8 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Sikatyaya'. He who exists in the sands. To 'Pravahyaya'; He who exists in the flow/current of the river. || 17 ||

Thus ends the Eighth Anuvaka in the Yajurveda Bhashya of Shri Rudra Prashna composed by Shri Bhatta Bhaskara. || 8 ||

sanskrit.krama: नम॑ इरि॒ण्या॑य । इरि॒ण्या॑य च । च प्रप॒थ्या॑य । प्रप॒थ्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
76 Sayanacharya—along with Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya— [Na. Na. A.]

|| Thus ends the Eighth Anuvaka ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: In the eighth Anuvaka, the Yajus with salutations on one side were mentioned. Now, in the ninth Anuvaka, some others are being mentioned. He states the nineteen Yajus present there:

Salutations to the one in the barren land (Irinyaya) and to the one in the path (Prapathyaya). || 1 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Irinam' is barren/salty land; He who is in that is 'Irinyah'. 'Prapathah' is a path frequented by many; He who is in that is 'Prapathyah'. || 1 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Irinyaya'. 'Irinam' is barren land; He who is in that. To 'Prapathyaya'. 'Prapathah' is a great path frequented by many; He who is in that. || 1 ||

Salutations to the one in the small pebbles (Kinshilaya) and to the one in the habitable place (Kshayanaya). || 2 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Kinshilah' is a gravelly region where there are small, vile stones. 'Kshayanah' is a place suitable for residence. || 2 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Kinshilaya'. Where there are small, vile stones, that gravelly region is 'kinshilah'; He who stands there. (By the rule of location, the word denotes the dweller). Or 'kinshilah' is a specific region where there is doubt whether they are small stones or crystals; He who stands there. To 'Kshayanaya'. From the root 'kshi' (to dwell). The suffix 'lyut' is used in the sense of location. 'Kshayanah' is a place suitable for residence; He who stands there. The usage is as before. Others say: All these from 'Shashpya' onwards are specific types of transmigrating beings. 'Shashpah' are those like young grass with very little strength. 'Phenah' are those like foam with very short lives. 'Sikatah' are like sand, extremely small. 'Pravahah' are those with great power and opulence. 'Irinah' are like barren land, with zero power. 'Prapathah' are those on the main path. 'Kinshilah' are those of vile character (kutsita-shila). 'Kshayanah' are those subject to decay. He exists in all of them without distinction as their benefactor. The sense is that He is the grace-giver of all. || 2 ||

Salutations to the one with matted hair (Kapardine) and to the one who stands before (Pulastaye). || 3 ||
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Ninth Anuvaka (Chapter Nine)

---*---

   Nama irinya ya ca prapathya ya ca namah
   kimshilaya ca kshayanaya ca namah kapa-
   rdine ca pulastaye ca namo goshtya ca
   grhya ya ca namastalpya ya ca gehya ya ca
   namah kathya ya ca gahresthaya ca namo
   hridayya ya ca niveshpya ya ca namah
   pamsavya ya ca rajasya ya ca namah
   shushkya ya ca haritya ya ca namo
   lopya ya colpya ya nama u-
   rvya ya ca surmya ya ca namah
   pannya ya ca parneshadhya ya ca

   In the eighth chapter, the Lord was saluted through the quality of His being the inner controller (antaryamin) — by citing reasons why He is worthy of universal worship. In the ninth chapter too, the Lord is saluted in His role as inner controller — by the mantra beginning "Nama irinya ya." Irina means a salt desert or barren wasteland (ushara); He who abides there is Irinya. Prapatha means the well-worn path (ksunna-marga); He who abides there is Prapathya. Kimshila is the place where there are defective or jagged stones (kutsitah shilah); Kshayana is a region fit for habitation —

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
नवमोऽनुवाकः ॥

---*---

   नम इरिण्याय च प्रपथ्याय च नम:
किण्शिलाय च क्षयणाय च नम: कप-
दिने च पुलस्तये च नमो गोष्ठ्य च गृ-
ह्याय च नमस्तल्प्याय च गेह्याय च नम:
काठ्याय च गहृरेष्ठाय च नमो हृदय्याय
च निवेष्प्याय च नम: पांस्रव्याय च
रजस्याय च नम: शुष्क्याय च हरित्याय
च नमो लोप्याय चोलप्याय च नम ऊ-
व्र्यांच सूर्म्यांच नम: पण्याय च
पर्णेनध्याय च ॥

   अष्टमे जगतुपास्यत्वहेतुभिरन्तर्यामिमतया भगवाचमस्कृत: ।
नवमे पुनरन्तर्यामिमतया भगवाचमस्किर्यते— नम इरिण्या-
येति । इरिणम् ऊषरम्, तत्र भव: इरिण्य:, प्रपथ: क्षुण्ण-
मार्ग:, तत्र भव: प्रपथ्य:, कुत्सिता: शिला यस्मिन् स:

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ किग्ंशि॒लाय॑ । किग्ंशि॒लाय॑ च । च क्षय॑णाय । क्षय॑णाय च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमः॑ कप॒र्दिने॑ । कप॒र्दिने॑ च । च पुल॒स्तये॑ । पुल॒स्तये॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Word-for-word]: Namah | nila-grivaya | cha | shiti-kanthaya | cha || 3 ||

Sayana's Commentary: 'Nilagrivah' is he who has a part of his neck blue in color due to holding the Kalakuta poison. 'Shitikanthah' is he who has the remaining neck region white (shiti). (3)

Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: 'Namo nilagrivaya cha'—explained already. 'Shitikanthaya cha'—because 'shiti' means both white and dark according to the Nighantu. Indeed, only a small part of the God's neck became blue due to the fire of the Kalakuta poison. Or: the repetition is for the sake of respect, as in "Salutation to the Lord of Ambika, the Lord of Uma, the Lord of Beings." [Grammatical note on the word 'shitikanthaya']. (3)

Salutation to the one with matted hair and to the one with shaven hair.
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Śrī Rudra Bhāṣyam                    101

The well-known Śruti passage establishes His role as the bestower of liberation (mokṣahetutva). "He who destroys (śṛṇāti hinaṣti) everything is Śarva"; through this name, the role of Śarva as the destroyer of the universe (jagat-saṃhartṛtva) is stated, as attested in the Śruti: "To that great Time who gathers all in — salutation." By the word Paśupati (lord of souls/beasts), the role of the Lord as the cause of bondage and sustenance is stated: the word paśu (animal, bound soul) refers to the bondage of saṃsāra, and the word pati (lord) refers to protection and enjoyment of that. Accordingly, the Śruti — "He is the cause of saṃsāra-liberation, sustenance, and bondage" — affirms that He is the cause of world-sustenance and bondage; hence, it is concluded that the meaning of these two mantras is homage to the Lord as the cause of the five cosmic functions relating to the herd of souls. Here, to show that the word namaḥ connects individually with each of the four sets of paired names, the conjunctive particles "ca" (and) serve to indicate coordination. Thus the connection runs: "namaḥ bhavāya ca" and "namaḥ śarvāya ca." The same should be understood for all the other pairs throughout.

      Homage to Nīlagrīva and to Śitikaṇṭha.

      Now the third [mantra]: "Namo nīlagrīvāya" — He whose throat and neck (grīvā, kaṇṭha) are blue from drinking the Kālakūṭa poison is Nīlagrīva (the blue-throated one); He whose throat was white (dhavala) before the event of the poison-drinking is Śitikaṇṭha (the white-throated one). By this mantra, the Lord's compassion for all worlds is indicated — His compassion that moved Him, disregarding the natural defect inflicted on His otherwise fair throat by the Kālakūṭa poison, to swallow the venom for the single purpose of protecting the world. This is well-attested in the Śruti: "You have compassion upon this entire world" — and similar passages.

      Homage to Kapardī and to Vyuṣkeśa.

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
श्रीरुद्रभाष्यम् ।                    १०१

श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं मोक्षहेतुत्वमुक्तम् । श्रृणावि हिनस्ति सर्वमिति
शर्वः, अनेन 'तस्मा उपसंहर्तें महामासाय नमः' इत्यादि-
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्संहर्तृत्वमुक्तम् । पशुपतिशब्देन बन्धस्थि-
तिहेतुत्वमुच्यते, पशुशब्देन संसारबन्धस्य पतिशब्देन तद्र-
क्षणस्य भोक्तेः ; तथा च 'संसारमोक्षस्थितिबन्धहेतुः' इति
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्स्थितिबन्धहेतुत्वमुक्तं भवतीत्याह्य पशुकु-
क्षहेतुत्वेन भगवन्नमस्कारो मन्त्रद्वयार्थः । अत्र नमःशब्द-
ल प्रत्येकं चतुष्पर्यन्तपदाभ्यामन्वयं दर्शियितुं समुच्चयार्थौ च-
कारौ फलितौ । तेन भवाय च नमः शर्वाय च नमः इत्य-
न्वयो भवति । एवममेऽपि सर्वत्र द्रष्टव्यम् ॥

      नमो नीलग्रीवाय च शितिकण्ठाय च ॥

      अथ तृतीयः:—— नमो नीलग्रीवायेति । काळकूटमसनेन
नीला ग्रीवा कण्ठो यस्य, स नीलग्रीवः, तद्वृषनात्पूर्वे
स्थितः धवलः कण्ठो यस्य, सः शितिकण्ठः । अनेन स्व-
शेत धवलकण्ठे काळकूटप्रयुक्तं नैसर्ग्य दोषमविगण्यम जगदे-
करक्षणाय विषं कबलितवतो भगवतः सर्वलोकदयालुत्वम्
'अहीमिदं दयसे विश्वम्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धं प्रदशिवम् ॥

      नमः कपर्दिने च ब्युस्केशाय च ॥

sanskrit.krama: नमो॒ गोष्ठ्या॑य । गोष्ठ्या॑य च । च गृह्या॑य । गृह्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. Na. A.] Rudradhyaya | 77

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Kapardi' is one with matted hair (jata-bandha). 'Pulastih' is one who stands in front of devotees. || 3 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Kapardine'. (Explained before). To 'Pulastaye'. 'Pulastayah' means having long hair (kuntala); salutations to the one who has that. The possessive suffix is dropped. At the time of grace, the Lord sometimes comes with matted hair and sometimes with long hair. Others say: 'Pulastih' means one who always stands in front of everyone. The form is derived from 'pru-shodara' etc. This shows that even though He is of such great majesty, He is always easily accessible. || 3 ||

Salutations to the one in the cowshed (Goshthyaya) and to the one in the house (Grihyaya). || 4 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Goshtham' is the place for cows; He who is in that is 'Goshthyah'. 'Grihyah' is He who is in the house. || 4 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: To further elaborate on the Lord's accessibility, He says: Salutations to 'Goshthyaya'. 'Goshtham' is the place where cows stay. To the one who exists there. To 'Grihyaya', the one who exists in the house. || 4 ||

Salutations to the one on the couch (Talpyaya) and to the one in the mansion (Gehyaya). || 5 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Talpyah' is one lying on a bed or couch (khatva). 'Gehyah' is one who exists in a house or palace (prasada). || 5 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Talpyaya'. 'Talpam' is a bed/couch etc.; He who exists there. To 'Gehyaya'. 'Geham' is a palace etc.; He who exists there. || 5 ||

Salutations to the one in the dense forest (Katyaya) and to the one who stands in the mountain cave (Gahvareshthaya). || 6 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Katam' is a thorny forest or a place full of creepers, making it difficult to enter; a dense and inaccessible forest is 'katam', and He who exists in that is 'Katyah'. 'Gahvareshthah' is one who stands in uneven places like mountain caves. || 6 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Katyaya'. This is explained. Or He graces the world in the form of water in a well ('kata'). To 'Gahvareshthaya'. To the one who stands in mountain caves. (Grammatical explanation for 'shthah' and 'shatvam'). || 6 ||

Kimshila is the place where there are defective or jagged stones (kutsitah shilah); Kshayana is a region fit for habitation —

commentaries_sanskrit.abhinava_shankara:
नवमोऽनुवाकः ॥

---*---

   नम इरिण्याय च प्रपथ्याय च नम:
किण्शिलाय च क्षयणाय च नम: कप-
दिने च पुलस्तये च नमो गोष्ठ्य च गृ-
ह्याय च नमस्तल्प्याय च गेह्याय च नम:
काठ्याय च गहृरेष्ठाय च नमो हृदय्याय
च निवेष्प्याय च नम: पांस्रव्याय च
रजस्याय च नम: शुष्क्याय च हरित्याय
च नमो लोप्याय चोलप्याय च नम ऊ-
व्र्यांच सूर्म्यांच नम: पण्याय च
पर्णेनध्याय च ॥

   अष्टमे जगतुपास्यत्वहेतुभिरन्तर्यामिमतया भगवाचमस्कृत: ।

मार्ग:, तत्र भव: प्रपथ्य:, कुत्सिता: शिला यस्मिन् स:

sanskrit.krama: नम॒स्तल्प्या॑य । तल्प्या॑य च । च गेह्या॑य । गेह्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. Na. A.] Rudradhyaya | 77

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Kapardi' is one with matted hair (jata-bandha). 'Pulastih' is one who stands in front of devotees. || 3 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Kapardine'. (Explained before). To 'Pulastaye'. 'Pulastayah' means having long hair (kuntala); salutations to the one who has that. The possessive suffix is dropped. At the time of grace, the Lord sometimes comes with matted hair and sometimes with long hair. Others say: 'Pulastih' means one who always stands in front of everyone. The form is derived from 'pru-shodara' etc. This shows that even though He is of such great majesty, He is always easily accessible. || 3 ||

Salutations to the one in the cowshed (Goshthyaya) and to the one in the house (Grihyaya). || 4 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Goshtham' is the place for cows; He who is in that is 'Goshthyah'. 'Grihyah' is He who is in the house. || 4 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: To further elaborate on the Lord's accessibility, He says: Salutations to 'Goshthyaya'. 'Goshtham' is the place where cows stay. To the one who exists there. To 'Grihyaya', the one who exists in the house. || 4 ||

Salutations to the one on the couch (Talpyaya) and to the one in the mansion (Gehyaya). || 5 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Talpyah' is one lying on a bed or couch (khatva). 'Gehyah' is one who exists in a house or palace (prasada). || 5 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Talpyaya'. 'Talpam' is a bed/couch etc.; He who exists there. To 'Gehyaya'. 'Geham' is a palace etc.; He who exists there. || 5 ||

Salutations to the one in the dense forest (Katyaya) and to the one who stands in the mountain cave (Gahvareshthaya). || 6 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Katam' is a thorny forest or a place full of creepers, making it difficult to enter; a dense and inaccessible forest is 'katam', and He who exists in that is 'Katyah'. 'Gahvareshthah' is one who stands in uneven places like mountain caves. || 6 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Katyaya'. This is explained. Or He graces the world in the form of water in a well ('kata'). To 'Gahvareshthaya'. To the one who stands in mountain caves. (Grammatical explanation for 'shthah' and 'shatvam'). || 6 ||
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Ninth Anuvaka (Chapter Nine)

---*---

   Nama irinya ya ca prapathya ya ca namah
   kimshilaya ca kshayanaya ca namah kapa-
   rdine ca pulastaye ca namo goshtya ca
   grhya ya ca namastalpya ya ca gehya ya ca
   namah kathya ya ca gahresthaya ca namo
   hridayya ya ca niveshpya ya ca namah
   pamsavya ya ca rajasya ya ca namah
   shushkya ya ca haritya ya ca namo
   lopya ya colpya ya nama u-
   rvya ya ca surmya ya ca namah
   pannya ya ca parneshadhya ya ca

   In the eighth chapter, the Lord was saluted through the quality of His being the inner controller (antaryamin) — by citing reasons why He is worthy of universal worship. In the ninth chapter too, the Lord is saluted in His role as inner controller — by the mantra beginning "Nama irinya ya." Irina means a salt desert or barren wasteland (ushara); He who abides there is Irinya. Prapatha means the well-worn path (ksunna-marga); He who abides there is Prapathya. Kimshila is the place where there are defective or jagged stones (kutsitah shilah); Kshayana is a region fit for habitation —

---*---

दिने च पुलस्तये च नमो गोष्ठ्य च गृ-
ह्याय च नमस्तल्प्याय च गेह्याय च नम:

sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ का॒ट्या॑य । का॒ट्या॑य च । च गह्वरे॒ष्ठाय॑ । गह्वरे॒ष्ठाय॑ च । च इति॑ च ॥

[Na. Na. A.] Rudradhyaya | 77

Salutations to the one on the couch (Talpyaya) and to the one in the mansion (Gehyaya). || 5 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Talpyah' is one lying on a bed or couch (khatva). 'Gehyah' is one who exists in a house or palace (prasada). || 5 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Talpyaya'. 'Talpam' is a bed/couch etc.; He who exists there. To 'Gehyaya'. 'Geham' is a palace etc.; He who exists there. || 5 ||

Salutations to the one in the dense forest (Katyaya) and to the one who stands in the mountain cave (Gahvareshthaya). || 6 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Katam' is a thorny forest or a place full of creepers, making it difficult to enter; a dense and inaccessible forest is 'katam', and He who exists in that is 'Katyah'. 'Gahvareshthah' is one who stands in uneven places like mountain caves. || 6 ||

sanskrit.krama: नमो᳚ ह्रद॒य्या॑य । ह्रद॒य्या॑य च । च निवे॒ष्प्या॑य । निवे॒ष्प्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

sanskrit.samhita: नमः॑ पाग्ं स॒व्या॑य च रज॒स्या॑य च॒
sanskrit.krama: नमः॑ पाग्ं स॒व्या॑य । पाग्ं स॒व्या॑य च । च रज॒स्या॑य । रज॒स्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥
नम॒-श्शुष्क्या॑य । शुष्क्या॑य च । च हरि॒त्या॑य । हरि॒त्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमो॒ लोप्या॑य । लोप्या॑य च । चोल॒प्या॑य । उ॒ल॒प्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
78 Sayanacharya—along with Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya— [Na. Na. A.]

Salutations to the one in deep waters (Hridayyaya) and to the one in the dew (Niveshpyaya). || 7 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Hridayyah' is He who exists in 'hrida', i.e., deep, unfathomable waters. 'Niveshpah' is dew-water (nihara-jala); He who exists in that is 'Niveshpyah'. || 7 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Hridayyaya'. To the one in the deep pool (hrida). To 'Niveshpyaya'. 'Niveshpyam' is water found in snow or frost. Salutations to the one who exists in that. || 7 ||

Salutations to the one in the fine dust (Pansavyaya) and to the one in the visible dust (Rajasyaya). || 8 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Pansavyah' is He who is situated in 'pansu', i.e., atoms or fine dust. 'Rajasyah' is He who is situated in 'rajas', i.e., clearly visible dust. || 8 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Pansavyaya'. To the one in 'pansu', the subtle dust particles. To 'Rajasyaya'. To the one in the opposite, i.e., coarse dust particles. Or the word 'rajas' refers to the qualities (gunas) or the worlds (loka), as in "Yadasya pare rajasah". || 8 ||

Salutations to the one in the dry wood (Shushkyaya) and to the one in the green wood (Harityaya). || 9 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Shushkyah' is He who exists in dry wood (shushka-kashta). 'Harityah' is He who exists in green, moist wood. || 9 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Shushkyaya'. 'Shushkam' is juiceless wood; to the one who is in that. To 'Harityaya'. 'Haritam' is green/moist wood; to the one who is in that. || 9 ||

Salutations to the one in the hard ground (Lopyaya) and to the one in the grass (Ulapyaya). || 10 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Lopa' is a hard region where grass etc. is missing; He who is in that is 'Lopyah'. 'Ulapa' refers to grasses like balvaja; He who is in that is 'Ulapyah'. || 10 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Lopyaya'. 'Lopa' is a hard region where grass is broken or destroyed; to the one who is in that. To 'Ulapyaya'. 'Ulapa' are grasses like balvaja; to the one who is in that. Or to the one in a land containing them. || 10 ||

sanskrit.krama: नम॑ ऊ॒र्व्या॑य । ऊ॒र्व्या॑य च । च सू॒र्म्या॑य । सू॒र्म्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमः॑ प॒र्ण्या॑य । प॒र्ण्या॑य च । च पर्णश॒द्या॑य । पर्णश॒द्या॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. Na. A.] Rudradhyaya | 79

Salutations to the one on the earth (Urdhyaya) and to the one in the waves (Surmyaya). || 11 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Urdhyah' is He who exists on the 'urdhya', i.e., the earth. A river having beautiful waves ('urmi') is 'surmi'; He who exists in that is 'surmyah'. || 11 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Urdhyaya'. To the one on the 'urdhya', the earth. (Grammatical note on long vowel). It refers to the entire ground. To 'Surmyaya'. 'Surmi' is a hollow metal image or a flowing stream. Salutations to the one who exists in that. Cites "Surmim jvalantim...". || 11 ||

Salutations to the one in the leaves (Parnyaya) and to the one in the heap of dry leaves (Parnashadyaya). || 12 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Parnyah' is He who exists in leaves (patra). A heap of dry leaves is 'parnashad'; He who exists in that is 'parnashadyah'. || 12 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Parnyaya'. To the one in the leaves. In these three (Urdhya etc.), the initial accent is absent as before. To 'Parnashadyaya'. A heap of dry leaves is 'parnashad'; to the one who is in that. This shows how accessible He is. These are the ways the Lord graces the world. || 12 ||

Salutations to the one who raises weapons (Apaguramanaya) and to the one who strikes (Abhighnate). || 13 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Apaguramanah' is one with weapons raised. 'Abhighnan' is one who strikes or kills. || 13 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Now He describes how the Lord acts towards the wicked souls who are to be restrained: Salutations to 'Apaguramanaya', the one who raises weapons to strike those to be punished. To 'Abhighnate', the one who strikes or kills the enemies who are in front. || 13 ||

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑-ऽपगु॒रमा॑णाय । अ॒पगु॒रमा॑णाय च । चाभिघ्न॒ते । अ॒भिघ्न॒ते च । च इति॑ च ॥
नम॑ आख्खिद॒ते । आख्खिद॒ते च । च प्रख्खिद॒ते । प्रख्खिद॒ते च । च इति॑ च ॥
नमो॑ वः । वो॑ किरि॒केभ्यः॑ । किरि॒केभ्यो॑ दे॒वाना᳚म् । दे॒वानां॒ हृद॑येभ्यः । हृद॑येभ्य॒ इति॑ हृद॑येभ्यः ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. Na. A.] Rudradhyaya | 81

By that mark, he should be known as a Rudra-worshipper. Any other king whose hands have that mark should also be known as a Rudra-worshipper. Thus, Rudra-aradhana should be performed every year with 11 Brahmins. Another method is mentioned here: A king desiring the destruction of enemies should have it done by a very powerful Brahmin. Having made an image of the enemy king out of Khadira wood, in a secluded place, invoke the deity in a pot full of water. Having lit the fire nearby, sacrifice 100,000 Shami sticks with thorns and smeared with blood. Cat blood is considered excellent. Then, for each 'Namaskara', drive iron nails into every limb of that image. The king should place his foot on it. During the placement, the Guru should chant. Then the king should feed 100 Brahmins. Then dismiss the deity. By doing this, the enemy king will be distressed for three years. Another method involves a 'Shushthali' image and Homa as before; the enemy king will soon come under the king's control. Another method for control: In the enemy's city, royal agents disguised as Brahmins, Siddhas, or beggars should secretly place iron nails, enchanted 100,000 times, at crossroads, temples, royal courts, and gates. Then sprinkle enchanted ash and water everywhere. By doing this, the city remains distressed for three years. Thorny plants like Khadira will grow there. Then, in the enemy's water bodies, men in disguise should perform penance in neck-deep water facing the sun for 800 days, chanting 800 times daily. During chanting, they should imagine the Halahala poison in their hands. By doing this, those who drink that water (mounts etc.) perish as if bitten by snakes. Wise men should do this secretly; otherwise, fear of the (enemy) king arises. Therefore, it must be done in secret. The dakshina for these performers should be equal to half the kingdom. Then the meditation:

"One should meditate on the Blue-necked one with matted hair, holding the Pinaka, the Lord of beings, brilliant with the power of the fierce sun and fire, adorned with snakes."

Salutations to you, the Kirikas (who shower wealth), who are the hearts of the gods. || 15 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Kirikah' are the generous incarnations of Rudra who shower ('kiranti') wealth on devotees. They are the hearts of the gods because they are dear to all gods. Salutations to such as you. || 15 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: From "Namo vah kirikebhyah" until the end of the Anuvaka is one mantra. Its Rishi and Devata are Bhagavan Shambhu. Chhandas is Jagati with six feet. This mantra is direct, due to the use of the word 'yushmat' (you). Thus, the various forms of the Lord's play, distinct in attributes, actions, class, and shape, have been saluted individually.

sanskrit.krama: नमो॑ विक्षीण॒केभ्यः॑ । विक्षीण॒केभ्यो॒ नमो॑ । नमो॑ विचिन्व॒त्केभ्यः॑ । विचिन्व॒त्केभ्य॒ इति॑ वि-चिन्वत्-केभ्यः ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. Na. A.] Rudradhyaya | 83

Salutations to the non-decaying ones (Vikshinakebhyah). || 16 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Vikshinakah' is the opposite of 'kshina' (decayed). It means those who are never subject to decay. || 16 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Vikshinakebhyah'. To those who cause various types of destruction ('kshina') to non-devotees through diseases, thieves, hell, etc. 'Kshinu' means violence/destruction. Those who destroy in various ways are 'Vikshinakah'. (Grammatical derivation with Vedic lengthening). Others say: 'Kshiyu' means violence; from that, 'Vikshinakah' refers to those who destroy diverse beings. The Vajasaneyins read this as 'Vikshinakebhyah'. || 16 ||

Salutations to those who distinguish (Vichinvatkebhyah). || 17 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Vichinvatkah' are those who distinguish or provide the required objects. || 17 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Vichinvatkebhyah'. To those who distinguish between the virtuous and the sinful among beings in order to reward or punish them. || 17 ||

Salutations to those who kill with certainty (Anihritebhyah). || 18 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Anihritah' are those by whom sins have been completely destroyed/removed. || 18 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: Salutations to 'Anihritebhyah'. 'Hatam' means violence. 'A' indicates limit, 'ni' indicates certainty. Those who kill within limits or with certainty are 'Anihritah'. They do not act unjustly, nor do they ignore offenders. Or: Those who kill within the limit and with certainty. Others say: This refers to those who have been separated ('ni-gamita') from other deities. It means those who have been kept separate from other gods because of the fierce nature of their shared sacrifice. There is another mantra: "O Rudra, do not be angry...". The Brahmana says: "One repels Rudra in his own direction." || 18 ||
translations.abhinava_shankara:
130                    Sri Rudra Bhashyam

---

He who stands with weapon raised in a threatening posture (apaguramana) — striking those sinners (abhighnan) — afflicts some lightly in accordance with their sins (attikhaddan) — and others severely (prakhikhaddan) — such is the meaning intended. This entire group — from "Namah somaya" onward, the collection of twenty-one inner mantras in the form of salutations constituting a different type of Yajus (prose Vedic text) — forms one single mantra. Its Rishi is the Lord Himself. The meter is Anushtup. The deity too is the Lord. The meditation verses are those declared in the third anuvaka, beginning with "rupa, yavvana" etc. This mantra yields as its fruit — the state of equality with the Sages and Lords of creation, the Shiva state and direct experience. Mastery of this mantra is achieved through five hundred repetitions (japa), preceded by the performance of two krichhra penances.

           Namo vah kirikebhyo devanam hridaye-
           bhyo namo vikshina kebhyo namo vicinva-
           tkebhyo nama anihritebhyo nama amiva-
           tkebhyah

           (Salutation to you, O destroyers, who dwell in the hearts of the
           gods; salutation to those who diminish all things; salutation to
           those who examine; salutation to those who have been charged
           with punishment; salutation to those who spread disease)

With five mantras beginning "Namo vah kirikebhyah," the Rudraganas (Rudra's attendant hosts) who serve as the world's regulators are saluted in the form of a group (gana). Their hiddenness is understood from the two qualifications "to those in the hearts of the gods" — meaning, they dwell only in the hearts of the gods, and we (ordinary people) do not know them. This qualification applies to all five categories: the Karikas and the rest. Those who destroy whatever they wish are called Karikas (the destroyers).

translations.sayana:
68 Sayanacharya—along with Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya— [Na. Da. A.]

He states the first Rik:

O driver of the vile state! O Lord of food! O Detached one! O Blue-necked and Red one! Do not terrify these our men, nor these our cattle. Let none of these perish or be diseased. || 1 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: 'Drapih' is one who causes the vile path (hell for sinners). 'Andhasas pate' is the Lord/protector of food (andhas). He protects the food of devotees. 'Daridrat' means one without possessions, detached, or alone. As the Shruti says: "Brahman is one without a second." 'Nilalohitah' is blue in the neck and red elsewhere. O Rudra, addressed by these names! Do not terrify ('ma bheh') the prosperity of these our men (sons, grandsons, etc.) and these our cattle (cows, buffaloes, etc.). Let not even one thing among all these mentioned perish ('ma aras'). Let none of them be diseased ('mo amamat'). || 1 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: The Rishi of "Drape andhasaspate" is Pulaha. The Chhandas is Astara-pankti. The deity is Shri Rudra. After praising the Lord in His universal form, he now asks for fearlessness:

'Drape': O driver of the vile state of suffering or poverty, which is the result of bad deeds. Or the driver of the pitiable state of the end of the world. 'Dra' means vile path/motion. (Grammatical derivation). Or 'Drapih' is a name for a cloak; by metaphor it refers to one having it. 'Andhasaspate': 'Andhas' is food. Lord/Master of that. 'Daridrat': One without possessions or attachments. Even being the Lord of food, He is detached. (Grammatical note on accents). 'Nilalohita': Rudra appearing from the heart of the Lord is Nilalohita; He who performed the act of begging, etc. Half blue and half red due to the male-female nature. Or because of having name and form. Others say: Blue in the neck and red elsewhere. Or 'Nila' means one whose impurities are completely removed. Thus addressing the Lord, he asks for fearlessness: 'Esham Purusharam': Our sons, etc. 'Esham Pashuram': Our cows, etc. 'Ma Bheh': Do not terrify them. 'Ma Aras': Do not let them perish or suffer. (Grammatical explanation for 'aras' and 'amamat'). Let no disease (amamat) strike them. || 1 ||
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Tenth Anuvaka (Chapter Ten)

---*---

   Drape andhasaspate daridran nilalohita
   esham purushanamasham pashuna ma bhe-
   mo ro mo esham kim ca namamat

   Thus, having established the foundation through the first anuvaka, the Lord was saluted through the second through ninth anuvakas by means of His qualities of being the Lord of all (sarveshvaratva), being supremely excellent (sarvottamatva), and being the inner controller of all (sarvantaryamimatva) and so forth. Now, through the tenth anuvaka, a prayer is offered. In this anuvaka there are twelve mantras in all. The first among them is: "Drape" etc. The word drapa is a term of censure, meaning one who leads creatures to wretched states; thus "Andhasaspati" means the lord of food (andhasa, which is food, and pati, lord). Daridra means one who is utterly without possessions (nishkimcana). Because half the body is dark blue and half is reddish, He is called Nilalohita (the blue-red one). Being thus addressed, the prayer is made: "Of these our people (esham) — our sons, grandsons and the rest — and of these animals — our cows, horses and the rest — do not cause fear; such is the meaning. Let not even one single thing (kimcana ekam api vastu) of ours perish or be destroyed — such is the meaning. And may none of them be made ill (mo amamatu); may none suffer from disease." In the passage "ma bheh, mara, mo amamatu," the vocatives —

|| He speaks the third verse ||

O Rudra, that body of yours which is auspicious, non-terrible, and revealing no sin—

translations.abhinava_shankara:
50                    SRI RUDRA BHASHYAM

— That form (tanuh): of what nature is it? It is answered: It is aghora —
not terrifying by means of weapons without cause; aghora means not ghora
(not terrifying). Not only is it the absence of terror, but further it is shiva —
auspicious, a bestower of happiness. And not merely a giver of worldly
happiness; rather, it bestows the knowledge that is the means to liberation
(moksha), which is the supreme human goal (paramapurusharta) — this is
declared by: "apapakashini." By the word apapa, the knowledge that is opposed
to sin — that is, the knowledge of Brahman (brahman-jnana, the knowledge of
the supreme reality) — is indicated, as per the Shruti: "tad yathaisika-tulam
agnau protam praduyeta evam ha va asya sarve papmanah praduyante" (just as
a reed's tip thrust into fire is burned up, so too all sins of this person are
burned away); and as the Gita states: "the fire of knowledge burns all actions
to ashes." Apapa (freedom from sin) + kashini (illuminer) = one who illumines
freedom from sin, i.e., a giver of knowledge — such is the meaning. [This is
confirmed by] the Shruti: "atmabuddhi-prakasham" (the light of Self-knowledge).

Such being that form of Yours: with that shantamaya (utterly tranquil)
tanva (form), the form that is of the nature of unclouded bliss — abhichakashihi
(shine forth upon us, illuminate us on all sides) — illuminate us completely,
as beings shining on all sides with the non-dual identity of Brahman and the
Self. "May all of this be known when this one thing is known" — [as the
Shruti says.] The form abhichakashihi, being a yak-tug verb form (an intensive),
shows the intensification of the verbal action indicating the seeking of complete
illumination — the meaning being "bring us to a direct, unmediated
realization of Brahman." The phrase "shantamaya abhichakashihi" shows that
the Supreme Lord is of the nature of consciousness-bliss (chidananda).

But, one might think: "If you want liberation through mere salutation alone,
[without] the great efforts of hearing, reflection, and deep meditation
(shravana, manana, nididhyasana), [why use the term girisha?]" — so the
second mode of address is used, correcting this misunderstanding:

"Girisha" —

translations.sayana:
[Na. Da. A.] Rudradhyaya | 91

(Grammatical explanation continued). Now the 'prabharanam' (offering) is qualified: In such a way that by this offering, there may be peace ('sham') for our own bipeds (sons etc.) and quadrupeds (cattle etc.). (Grammatical note on 'ashat'). "Asmin grame": In this village where we live, let all living beings—even those that do not belong to us—be well-nourished and healthy. What kind of thought should we offer?

The Puranic sages say:
"In a village or city where a chanter of Rudradhyaya lives, there is no hunger, thirst, famine, or disease."

Or, in such a way that in our village, there may be peace for bipeds and quadrupeds, and every object like wealth and grain may be abundant and healthy. || 1 ||

He states the fourth and fifth [Note: 4th is here]:

O Rudra, make us happy; grant us happiness. We serve you, the destroyer of enemies, with salutations. May we attain that peace and separation from suffering which father Manu sought through sacrifice; may we reach that under your guidance, O Rudra. || 4 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: O Rudra, make us happy ('mridaya') in this world. And also ('uta') grant us happiness ('mayas') in the next world. We serve ('vidhema') you, the destroyer of our sins, with salutations. Father Manu (Prajapati), the protector, sought peace and happiness and separation from suffering; all that, O Rudra, may we attain under your guidance/love. || 4 ||

sanskrit.samhita: मा नो॑ म॒हान्त॑मु॒त मा नो॑ अर्भ॒क-म्मा न॒ उक्ष॑न्तमु॒त मा न॑ उक्षि॒तम् । मा नो॑ वधीः पि॒तर॑-म्मोत मा॒तर॑-म्प्रि॒या मा न॑स्त॒नुवो॑ रुद्र रीरिषः ॥

translations.sayana:
[Na. Da. A.] Rudradhyaya | 99

(Grammatical note continued). || 1 ||

[Purashcharana for these two (Riks 3 and 4)]: Having performed a Kricchra penance, one should chant the mantra 9,000 times to complete the Purashcharana. On a New Moon day, having lit the fire at a crossroads, sacrifice 10,000 offerings of thick curd (dadhi-sara); there will be peace for cattle etc. For the peace of royal mounts, having touched water, chant the mantra 10,000 times and sprinkle the stable with that water; there will be peace for the mounts. Or mix that water with whatever is to be used for the mounts. To pacify animals, sacrifice 10,000 sesame seeds mixed with ghee. For the peace of others, sacrifice 10,000 Durva grasses. Meditation:

"Meditate on Soma (the Moon), sitting on a divine throne, being praised by great sages, with a pleased face, wearing a crescent moon."

He states the fifth Rik:

Do not kill our elders, nor our children, nor our young men, nor the unborn. Do not kill our father, nor our mother. O Rudra, do not harm our dear bodies. || 5 ||

Sayanacharya Bhashya: O Rudra, do not harm ('ma ririshah') our elderly men (sthavira). And also do not harm our infants (arbhaka). Furthermore, do not harm our young men capable of procreation (ukshantam). And do not harm our unborn/fetal men (ukshitam). Do not kill ('ma vadhih') our father. And also do not kill our mother. Do not harm our dear bodies. || 5 ||

Bhatta Bhaskara Bhashya: The Rishi of "Ma no mahantam" is Devarata. Chhandas is Jagati. The deity is Rudra in the form of Nandishvara.

"Ma no mahantam": O Rudra, do not kill our elder, like a grandfather etc., or an elderly son, or a son worthy of grace having qualities of knowledge and age. Do not kill through diseases etc. And do not kill our infant child. Do not kill our young man capable of procreation, who continues the lineage. And do not kill the unborn (ukshitam), who is even more pitiable in the womb. Father and mother are most venerable; do not kill them. And do not harm our dear bodies, because of their very dearness. Do not afflict them with diseases. The plural is used for wife, sons, etc. Do not harm our dear bodies. || 5 ||
translations.abhinava_shankara:
Sri Rudra Bhashyam                    137

---

growing child (ukshantam), nor the fully grown one (ukshitam). Do not slay our father, nor our mother (mataram). Do not harm (ma ririshih) what is dear to us (priya), O Rudra — our beloved ones. Nor harm our own bodies (tanucha). This fifth mantra is a prayer for the protection of all family members — young and old, male and female — from affliction, injury and death. The Lord who is Rudra, the remover of all suffering, is petitioned not to bring any harm, whether through disease or destruction, to any among our dear ones. The prayer extends from the eldest to the youngest, from parents to children, encompassing all that is near and precious to the devotee. Such is the meaning intended by this mantra.
